A. Hyperplastic polyp with inflammatory changes B. Pseudopolyp with inflammatory changes C. Tubular adenoma with low grade dysplasia D. Tubulovillous adenoma with low grade dysplasia Answer= D
Hyperplastic polyps have a low risk of malignant transformation and do not require any follow up if <10mm in size. High risk polyps include adenomas larger than 10mm or adenomas with tubulovillous histology or adenomas with high grade histology. Serrated high risk polyps include sessile serrated >10mm, sessile serrated with dysplasia or traditional serrated adenoma. (NZ bowel guidelines)
questions.stem:
A 22 year old woman with a history of atopic illness presents to specialist review after failed management of reflux in the community. An upper GI endoscopy view of the oesophagus is shown below.
What is the most likely diagnosis?

questions.explanation: Cardiac amyloidosis is the result of proteins that destabilize rearranging into beta pleated sheets that deposit in the myocardium. Under polarized light amyloid deposits exhibit apple green birefringence with congo red staining. Cardiac amyloid is most often due to amyloid light chain disease (AL) and amyloid transthyretin (ATTR) aggregates that lead to a restrictive cardiomyopathy. AL is the result of a monoclonal plasma cell producing immunoglobulins. ATTR is the result from the breakdown of the tetramer transthyretin protein (pre-albumin). This can be a wild type or variant. (wtATTR or vATTR). The encoding gene lies on chromosome 18 and the most common mutation is Val122lle. Amyloid acquired (AA) is caused amyloid acquired due to an inflammatory condition. The untreated median survival of AL, vATTR and wtATTR is 1.5 years, 2.5 years and 3.6 years respectively. AA amyloid or secondary amyloid has a much longer prognosis.
questions.stem: Which form of cardiac amyloidosis has the worst outcome?

questions.explanation: MMF is contraindicated in pregnancy due to its high risk of teratogenicity associated with congenital malformations and pregnancy loss. It should be discontinued before conception, with other safer immunosuppressive options considered. Prednisone, hydroxychloroquine and azathioprine can often be used in pregnancy under medical guidance.
questions.stem: A 31 year old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is planning to become pregnant. Her SLE has been managed with multiple medications, including prednisone, hydroxychloroquine, azathioprine and mycophenolate. Which of her medications should be discontinued prior to conception due to contraindications in pregnancy?

questions.explanation:
The primary approach to managing BK virus nephropathy is to reduce immunosuppression, as BK virus reactivation is linked to overly aggressive immune suppression. Increasing prednisone or adding valganciclovir is not recommended for BK

virus. Cyclosporine may be considered if there is no improvement with immunosuppression reduction, but is not first-line intervention.
questions.stem: A 55 year old man underwent a renal transplant six months ago and is on tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and low dose prednisone. He now presents with rising serum creatinine. Urine PCR detects high levels of BK virus DNA, and a renal biopsy confirms BK virus nephropathy. What is the most appropriate initial management step?

questions.explanation: Type 1 RTA is characterized by an inability to acidify urine, leading to metabolic acidosis with a normal anion gap (NAGMA). In this patient, the persistently high urine pH (>5.5) indicates impaired H+ secretion in the distal tubules, a hallmark of Type 1 RTA. The elevated urine calcium-creatinine ratio suggests a predisposition to nephrolithiasis, which is a common complication of Type 1 RTA. Risk factors include autoimmune conditions like RA, long-term corticosteroid use
questions.stem: A 45 year old woman with a history of rheumatoid arthritis presents with weakness, fatigue and persistent metabolic acidosis. She has been on long-term prednisone therapy for RA and reports frequent episodes of nephrolithiasis. Laboratory results show a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis (NAGMA) with a serum bicarbonate of 14mmol/L. Her urine pH is persistently >5.5 and the urine calcium-creatinine ratio is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

You are treating a 50 year old lady who has a long-standing in-dwelling urethral catheter as she has multiple sclerosis with a neurogenic bladder. You suspect she has presented with urosepsis and start treatment with IV Cefuroxime. 2 sets of blood cultures later grow Serratia marcescens. How does this new information guide your ongoing treatment?

options.text: Continue on IV cefuroxime but extend the course
This does not change your treatment as this is likely a contaminant.

questions.explanation: Serratia is one of the ESCHAPPM organisms which produce ampC. This leads to inducible resistance to almost all penicillins and cephalosporins. Treatment for these organisms are similar to that of ESBL - carbopenems.

questions.stem: A 32 year old Turkish man presents with recurrent oral and genital ulcers, uveitis, and erythema nodosum. He reports episodes of joint pain and has a family history of similar symptoms. His physical exam reveals a painful, erythematous lesion on his shin and bilateral anterior uveitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The patients recurrent oral and genital ulcers, uveitis, erythema nodosum and joint pain are consistent with Behcets disease which is more common in countries such as Turkey. Colchicine is commonly used for managing mucocutaneous symptoms, while immunosuppressive therapies are considered for more severe manifestations such as uveitis.

questions.stem:
A 28 year old man presents with a history of recurrent respiratory tract infections, chronic diarrhoea, and fatigue. He reports several hospitalizations for pneumonia and sinusitis since childhood. Laboratory results show:
 Serum IgG: 2g/L (normal 7-16g/L) Serum IgA: <0.07g/L (normal 0.7-4.0g/L) Serum IgM: 0.1g/L (normal 0.4-2.3g/L) Lymphocyte count: Normal No detectable specific antibody response to vaccination What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The patients recurrent infections, low levels of immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA and IgM) and lack of specific responses to vaccination are indicative of CVID. CVID typically presents with recurrent infections, autoimmune features and gastrointestinal symptoms. ABPA typically presents with asthma, eosinophilia and positive aspergillus-specific IgE. Thiis patient has low immunoglobulins and no suggestion of asthma or eosinophilia. Hyper-IgM syndrome is characterized by normal or elevated IgM levels and very low IgG and IgA, often with recurrent infections and defects in class switching. This patient has low IgG, IgA and IgM. X-linked agammaglobulinemia typically presents in early childhood with profound absence of all immunoglobulin classes and a lack of B cells. This patient has detectable but low immunoglobulins.

questions.stem: A 58 year old woman with bronchiectasis presents with recurrent exacerbations despite optimal treatment. Sputum cultures have consistently grown Pseudomonas aeruginosa. She has been on inhaled antibiotics intermittently for the past 18 months, but her symptoms persist. What is the most appropriate management for Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection in bronchiectasis?

options.text: Long term inhaled antibiotics such as tobramycin
Short term IV antibiotics during exacerbations only

questions.explanation: Long-term inhaled antibiotics such as tobramycin are the cornerstone of managing chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection in bronchiectasis. They help control bacterial colonization and reduce exacerbations without the systemic side e>ects associated with oral or IV antibiotics. Oral ciprofloxacin is generally not recommended for chronic management due to resistance concerns. Short term IV antibiotics are used for acute exacerbations but not long term suppression. Systemic corticosteroids are not indicated for chronic infection management.

The patients age, chronic alcohol abuse and CSF findings (pleocytosis with elevated protein and low glucose) are highly suggestive of listeria meningitis. Listeria monocytogenes is a common cause of aseptic meningitis in older adults with alcohol abuse and the negative gram stain is consistent with the non-purulent nature of this infection. Other pathogens such as Streptococcus pneumoniae or Neisseria meningitidis would more commonly show a positive Gram stain and a higher white blood cell counts.
questions.stem:
A 70 year old man with a history of chronic alcohol abuse presents with fever, confusion and a headache. He has a recent history of mild gastrointestinal symptoms but no known trauma. His CSF analysis reveals the following:
 White blood cell count: 80/uL (predominantly mononuclear cells) Glucose: 2.2mmol/L (normal 2.2-4.4mmol/L) Protein: 0.9 (elevated) Gram stain: No organisms seen

What pathogen is most likely causing his condition?

questions.explanation: The patient's history of travel to an endemic area, along with the characteristic presentation of fever with relative bradycardia and the appearance of salmon-coloured macules (rose spots) on the abdomen strongly suggests Typhoid fever caused by Salmonella typhi. This is a classic presentation of enteric fever, which is common in travellers to endemic regions. The other options are less likely to present with the described skin findings and relative bradycardia.
questions.stem: A 34 year old man returns from a recent trip to Southeast Asia. He presents with a 10 day history of fever, abdominal discomfort and malaise. On examination he has relative bradycardia and develops salmon coloured macules on his abdomen. His temperature is 38.3 degrees Celsius. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

questions.explanation:
The combination of INR >6.5 and pH <7.3 despite treatment are key indications for liver transplantation in cases of acute liver failure as per King's College Hospital criteria. These criteria are used to assess patients for liver transplant eligibility in the setting of paracetamol toxicity or other causes of acute liver failure. The criteria are as follows:
 pH of arterial blood (after rehydration) of <7.3 or All 3 of following criteria
 INR >6.5 Serum creatinine >300umol/L Grade III or IV encephalopathy
questions.stem: A 28 year old woman presents with a massive paracetamol overdose. She is comatose, has severe metabolic acidosis and coagulopathy. Despite treatment her pH remains <7.3 and her INR is >6.5. Which of the following is an indication for liver transplant?

questions.explanation: Anticholinergic toxidrome includes dry mouth, flushed skin, dilated pupils, confusion, tachycardia and decreased bowel sounds Serotonergic toxidrome often presents with hyperreflexia, clonus and agitation. Muscarinic toxidrome features excessive salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea and bradycardia. Sympathomimetic toxidrome causes tachycardia, hypertension, agitation and sweating.
questions.stem: A 34 year old man presents to the emergency department with confusion, dry mouth, flushed skin, dilated pupils and decreased bowel sounds. His heart rate is elevated. What toxidrome best describes this presentation?

questions.explanation: Selenium's beneficial e>ects are mainly due to its role as an antioxidant and its antiinflammatory properties. Graves orbitopathy involves inflammation and oxidative stress in the tissues around the eyes. A six month trial is generally recommended especially in selenium deplete areas.
questions.stem: A 52 year old female patient with Graves orbitopathy is experiencing eye discomfort, dryness and inflammation. Which of the following supplements may be beneficial in managing these symptoms?

options.text: Pa02 <60mmHg with no signs of right heart failure of pulmonary hypertension
Pa02 = 64mmHg with new onset cor pulmonale

questions.explanation: Indications for LTOT in COPD include a Pa02 <55mmHg or Pa02 <60mmHg with right heart failure or pulmonary hypertension
questions.stem: A 70 year old man with severe COPD is being assessed for long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT). Which of the following would be an indication to start LTOT?

A 45 year old man with newly diagnosed HIV has been started on antiretroviral therapy (ART) and is also being treated for active tuberculosis. After two weeks of treatment, he

develops fevers, worsening pulmonary symptoms and lymphadenopathy. Chest imaging shows new infiltrates. What is the most likely cause of his symptoms?
questions.explanation: IRIS occurs when the immune system, recovering after ART initiation, overreacts to previously controlled infections such as tuberculosis. This leads to inflammation and worsening symptoms. In contrast, drug-resistant tuberculosis would show persistence of symptoms despite treatment while other options such as CMV or bacterial pneumonia do not typically present with such a pattern in this context

questions.stem: A 68 year old man with metastatic prostate cancer is started on abiraterone as part of his treatment regime. Why is it necessary to co-administer prednisone with abiraterone?

options.text: To counteract the suppression of adrenal corticosteroid production
To prevent the development of resistance to abiraterone
To enhance the e>icacy of abiraterone in inhibiting androgen synthesis
To mitigate gastrointestinal disturbances caused by abiraterone

questions.explanation: Abiraterone inhibits the enzyme CYP17, which plays a key role in androgen biosynthesis, as well as in the production of corticosteroids. This inhibition results in adrenal insu>iciency, leading to reduced cortisol levels. Prednisone is given to compensate for the suppressed cortisol production, prevention symptoms of adrenal insu>iciency.

options.text: Estrogen receptor (ER) positive, Progesterone receptor (PR) negative, HER-2 positive

questions.explanation: Hormone receptor positive, HER-2 negative breast cancers have the highest risk of late recurrence, particularly beyond 5 years after treatment. These cancers tend to have a slower growth rate, meaning they are more likely to recur years after initial therapy. Other subtypes such as triple negative and HER-2 positive cancers typically have more aggressive disease courses and recurrence often occur earlier in the post treatment period.
questions.stem: A 52 year old woman with early stage breast cancer is being evaluated for risk factors for late recurrence. Which of the following breast cancer subtypes has the highest risk of recurrence more than 5 years after initial treatment?

A 45 year old woman presents with a 2 week history of immobility, mutism, and posturing. She has a history of bipolar disorder. What is the most appropriate initial treatment for her suspected catatonia?
questions.explanation: Lorazepam is the first-line treatment for catatonia, particularly when there is no underlying structural cause such as infection or metabolic disturbances. It acts quickly to relieve symptoms. If symptoms do not improve with benzodiazepines, electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) may be considered. Other options, such as antisphysics or antidepressants, are not typically used as first line treatment for catatonia.

Calciphylaxis is a rare, life-threatening condition characterized by vascular calcification and skin necrosis, commonly seen in patients with chronic kidney disease. The initial treatment involves supportive care, strict control of calcium and phosphate levels, and sodium thiosulfate which has shown e>icacy in reducing vascular calcification and promoting healing of skin lesions. Other treatments, such as parathyroidectomy or bisphosphonates may be considered in refractory cases but are not first line.
questions.stem: A 62 year old woman with end stage renal disease on haemodialysis presents with painful, necrotic skin lesions on her thighs and abdomen. Laboratory results show elevated calcium and phosphate levels. What is the most appropriate initial management for her suspected calciphylaxis?

A 62 year old female with end stage kidney disease on peritoneal dialysis presents with abdominal pain, fever and cloudy dialysate. A gram stain of the dialysate fluid reveals gram positive cocci. What is the most appropriate treatment?

options.text: Immediate PD catheter removal and intraperitoneal vancomycin and gentamicin

questions.explanation: The first line treatment for PD peritonitis involves intraperitoneal antibiotics as they are most e>ective in delivering high concentrations directly to the site of infection. Cefazolin targets gram-positive organisms and gentamicin covers gram-negative bacteria. Oral antibiotics (option A) and IV antibiotics (option C) are less e>ective for this condition. Option D which includes vancomycin is reserved for cases involving methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus.

questions.stem:
A 30 year old patient presents to clinic after being newly diagnosed with diabetes. Her sister and father also have a history of diabetes and kidney cysts. Your patient is noted

to have mild liver enzyme derangements. Which is the most likely genetic mutation to be found on testing?
questions.explanation: MODY 5, caused by mutations in the HNF1B gene, commonly presents with early-onset diabetes along with renal abnormalities like cystic kidney disease and genitourinary abnormalities. Patients may also have mild liver enzyme abnormalities and other extrapancreatic manifestations. PAX4 is associated with MODY9. HNF1A is associated with MODY 3 and is the most common form of inherited diabetes. It is sensitive to sulphonylureas. GCK is associated with MODY2 which is a mild stable hyperglycaemia that often does not require pharmacologic treatment.

questions.stem: A 67 year old female is diagnosed with metastatic lung cancer. Her regular medications include clozapine, aspirin, metoprolol and allopurinol. She is currently a heavy smoker but has decided to stop smoking. Which of the above medications will need to be adjusted?
questions.explanation:
Clozapine is metabolized in the liver by the enzyme CYP1A2 which is induced by smoking. With smoking cessation the clozapine metabolism is slowed and can lead to higher blood levels which may result in potentially serious side e>ects like seizures or agranulocytosis. Clozapine levels should be monitored and the medication reduced to avoid toxicity. Aspirin is not a>ected by smoking. Metoprolol is metabolized by CYP2D6 which is not induced by smoking. Allopurinol is metabolized to oxypurinol and neither smoking or enzyme interactions play a major role in the process.
For each of the following neurological presentations, please select the answer that corresponds to the MOST likely diagnosis

A. Gerstmann syndrome B. Lateral medullary syndrome C. Internal capsule infarct D. Functional neurological disorder E. Anterior cord syndrome F. Posterior cord syndrome G. Central cord syndrome H. Hemi-cord/Brown-Sequard Question 89 A 27 year old man presents with subacute numbness in both of his legs. Motor function in his lower limbs is intact. You take a detailed history - he has been taking large amounts of recreational nitric oxide.
Answer = F Large amounts of nitric oxide causes B12 deficiency, thereby causing subacute combined degeneration which a>ects the posterior columns in the spinal cord.

questions.stem: Which of the following extraintestinal manifestations of inflammatory bowel disease is associated with active gastrointestinal inflammation?
questions.explanation: Arthritis is the most frequent extra-intestinal manifestation of IBD. IBD is associated with a non-destructive peripheral arthritis, which primarily involves large joints and mirror the activity of bowel inflammation. Other musculoskeletal manifestations of IBD include a small joint peripheral arthritis and sacroiliitis, (which are independent of bowel inflammation), osteoporosis, and osteonecrosis.

options.text: Reversible H2 receptor antagonist in chief cells
Irreversible H2 receptor antagonist in parietal cells
Reversible H K ATPase inhibitor in chief cells
Irreversible H K ATPase inhibition in parietal cells

questions.explanation:
Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor that is used in the treatment of gastroesophageal reflux disease, peptic ulcers and Zollinger- Ellison syndrome. It works

by blocking the proton pump (H+/K+ ATPase) in the gastric parietal cells which reduces secretion of hydrogen ions (protons) into the gastric lumen. Omeprazole is a prodrug that is activated in an acidic environment and the inhibitory e9ect lasts longer than 24 hours because of irreversible binding to the proton pump.
questions.stem: What is the mechanism of omeprazole?

An 89 year old female presents with generalised abdominal pain and bloody stools. She is profoundly hypotensive. A CT angiography shows ischemic colitis. Which of the following sites in the large colon is most prone to ischemia?
questions.explanation: Colonic blood supply is from the superior mesenteric, inferior mesenteric arteries and iliac arteries. The superior mesenteric artery supplies the ascending colon, caecum and proximal 2/3 of the transverse colon. The inferior mesenteric artery supplies the distal one third of the transverse colon, descending colon, sigmoid and the upper part of the rectum. The iliac arteries supply the lower part of the rectum. The sites most prone to ischemia are at the areas between the major arteries- the most common being the splenic flexure followed by the rectosigmoid junction.

Huntington's exhibits an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern but is caused by a CAG trinucleotide repeat (not a CAT trinucleotide expansion). Index cases can occur due to a de novo mutation or due to anticipation causing younger age of onset in subsequent generations. MCQ logic applies here as well - never choose the answer that deals in absolutes ("no chance", "never", "will always" etc).

options.text: Huntington's disease has an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, caused by CAT trinucleotide expansion in the Huntington gene.

As neither of his parents have the clinical features of Huntington's disease, then there is no chance that his brother or sister will develop Huntington's disease, as this is likely a new sporadic mutation
Huntington's disease is a genetic condition that displays anticipation

questions.stem: Thomas is a 57 year old man who has been referred to a Neurologist for review after presenting to his GP with choreiform movements. Genetic testing confirmed a diagnosis of Huntington's disease. Which of the below statements is most appropriate when discussing the genetic inheritance of this condition with Thomas and his family?

options.text: No conception advice is required as their child is not at risk of transfusion-dependent thalassaemia
The chance of their child having HbH disease is 25%
The chance of their child having hydrops fetalis is zero
Pre-implantation embryonic selection will reduce the risk of thalassaemia in their child to zero

questions.explanation: Hydrops faetalis is - -/- -
questions.stem: A couple who both have alpha-thalassaemia trait present for conception advice. The father is aa/- - and the mother is a-/a-. Which of the following statements is correct?

Rachel is given a watch for her anniversary. After a few weeks of wearing the watch, she develops a red rash on the skin under the band. After a while she stops wearing the watch, and this rash resolves over 3 weeks. This reaction was due to what type of hypersensitivity?
questions.explanation: Nickel allergy is a contact allergy (also referred to as type IV or delayed type hypersensitivity). Unlike immediate hypersensitivities (such as pollen, insect venom and most food allergies), the reaction in delayed type hypersensitivity occurs sometime after exposure. The most e9ective method for relieving symptoms is avoidance.

Signal One: T cells are generated in the Thymus and are programmed to be specific for one particular antigen. Once they leave the thymus, they circulate throughout the body until they recognise their antigen on the surface of antigen presenting cells (APCs). The T cell receptor (TCR) on both CD4+ helper T cells and CD8+ cytotoxic T cells bind to the antigen as it is held in a structure called the MHC complex, on the surface of the APC. This triggers initial activation of the T cells. The CD4 and CD8 molecules then bind to the MHC molecule, stabilising the whole structure. This initial binding between a T cell specific for one antigen and the antigen-MHC it matches sets the whole response in motion. This normally takes place in the secondary lymphoid organs.
Signal Two: In addition to TCR binding to antigen-loaded MHC, both helper T cells and cytotoxic T cells require a number of secondary signals to become activated and respond to the threat. In the case of helper T cells, the first of these is provided by CD28. This molecule on the T cell binds to one of two molecules on the APC ­ B7.1 (CD80) or B7.2 (CD86) ­ and initiates T-cell proliferation. This process leads to the production of many millions of T cells that recognise the antigen. To control the response, stimulation of CD28 by B7 induces the production of CTLA-4 (CD152). This molecule competes with CD28 for B7 and so reduces activation signals to the T cell and winds down the immune response. Cytotoxic T cells are less reliant on CD28 for activation but do require signals from other co-stimulatory molecules such as CD70 and 4-1BB (CD137).

options.text: Antigen presentation via the MHC complex
Di9erentiation of T cells into subtypes
Interaction between CD28 on T cells and CD80/86 on antigen presenting cells
Interaction between CTLA-4 on T cells and CD80/86 on antigen presenting cells

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes signal two in T cell activation?

options.text: Complement activation causing mast cell degranulation
IgE binding causing mast cell degranulation
Abnormalities in arachidonic acid metabolism leading to increased leukotriene production

questions.explanation: The vignette describes a type of vancomycin hypersensitivity, the "red man syndrome". This is not an allergic reaction and does not involve IgE. Rather, vancomycin directly degranulates mast cells releasing histamine. Management in mild cases simply involves pausing the infusion and restarting at a lower rate. More severe reactions can be treated with antihistamines, and the infusion can be restarted later at a much lower rate.
questions.stem: A 55 year old gentleman with type 2 diabetes, COPD, hypertension, and an ex-smoker who is on renal replacement therapy with haemodialysis via central line, presents feeling generally unwell. questions.explanation:
Vancomycin inhibits enterococci by binding to the D-alanyl-D-alanine (D-Ala-D-Ala) terminus of cell wall precursors, compromising the subsequent enzymatic steps in the synthesis of cell wall. High-level resistance to vancomycin is encoded by di9erent clusters of genes referred to as the vancomycin-resistance gene clusters (eg, vanA, vanB, and vanD gene clusters). The result is the replacement of D-Ala-D-Ala­ending

peptidoglycan precursors with D-alanyl-D-lactate termini, to which vancomycin binds with significantly lower a9inity.

The normal dose of drug V is 50mg for the treatment of hypertension. The fraction excreted unchanged is 0.4. What is the renally adjusted dose for a patient with only 25% of normal renal function?
questions.explanation: The fraction excreted unchanged (fe) is the proportion of the active drug cleared renally in an average healthy person. 0.4 x 50 = 20 (i.e. 20mg usually cleared renally with normal renal function) 25% of 20 = 5 (i.e. only 5mg is renally cleared in someone with 25% of normal renal function) 20 - 5 = 15mg. Dose needs to be 50 - 15 = 35mg
Bioavailability is the fraction of the dose that reaches the systemic circulation as intact drug. F = AUC oral / AUC iv. F = (200/50) / (100/20) = 4/5= 80%

Which of the following antibodies is most specific for the diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis?
questions.explanation: Anti-CCP antibodies have higher specificity for RA than Rheumatoid Factor (RF) - 95 to 98 %. The specificity is greater in patients with higher titres of anti-CCP antibodies (at least three times the upper limit of normal). The sensitivity is similar to RF which is present in over 80 percent of patients with RA. The diagnostic utility of RF is limited by their relatively poor specificity, since RF is found in 5 to 10 percent of healthy individuals, 20 to 30 percent of people with SLE, virtually all patients with mixed cryoglobulinemia (usually caused by hepatitis C virus [HCV] infection), and in those with many other inflammatory conditions. Higher titers of RF (at least three times the upper limit of normal) have somewhat greater specificity for RA. Anti-Smith antibodies are highly specific for SLE, but lack sensitivity (seen in only 30% of patients with SLE). ANA - may be positive in up to one-third of patients with RA. In patients with a positive ANA, anti-double stranded DNA and anti-Smith antibody testing should also be performed; these antibodies have high specificity for SLE. A negative ANA excludes SLE when following the most recently developed classification Criteria (EULAR/ACR 2019).

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best definition of a type II error?
questions.explanation: Type I error: Incorrectly rejecting the null hypothesis, therefore stating there is an association where none exists. Also known as a false positive. Type II error: Falsely accepting the null hypothesis, therefore stating there is no e9ect when an e9ect exists. Also known as a false negative.

The primary end-point was time in ED; secondary end-points were need for admission, patient satisfaction on a 10 point scale, and use of any anti-epileptic medication other than Norma-Line. 200 patient encounters are recorded (103 exposure, 97 control). Baseline characteristics were similar between the groups. Primary outcome: Patients in the exposure group (Norma-Line) spent an average of 134 minutes in the ED, compared to 169 minutes in the control group (p <0.005). Secondary outcomes: 14 patients (13.6%) in the exposure group were admitted compared to 28 patients (28.9%) in the control group (p value <0.005). Average patient satisfaction score in exposure group was 7.8/10 (95% CI 5.89.8) compared to 4.1/10 in the control group (95% CI 3.3-4.8). questions.explanation: Gynecomastia is caused by excess oestrogen action and is usually the result of an increased oestrogen to androgen ratio. Spironolactone is a steroidal mineral corticoid antagonist (MRA) which is an inhibitor of aldosterone and a weak inhibitor of the androgen receptor and testosterone synthesis. This leads to inhibition of testosterone binding to the androgen receptor and less testosterone availability ­ leading to an increased oestrogen to testosterone ratio. Finerenone is a non-steroidal MRA with less a9inity to androgen receptors and inhibition of testosterone synthesis. It also has a higher selectivity towards mineralocorticoid receptor. It does not have any active metabolites but this does not have any e9ect on hormone levels and is thought to be the reason for less hyperkalaemia associated with finenerone.

Why does finenerone have a lower risk of gynecomastia?
Which part of the adrenal gland secretes aldosterone?
questions.explanation: The adrenal gland is divided into main parts: the adrenal cortex and the adrenal medulla. The adrenal cortex is most peripheral and consists of three zones each producing di9erent hormones. From superficial to deep is 1) the Zona Glomerulosa which produces mineralocorticoids (mainly aldosterone) 2) Zona Fasiculata which produces glucocorticoids (primarily cortisol) and 3) Zona Reticularis which produces androgens (such as DHEA). The Adrenal medulla is the innermost part and produces catecholamines (adrenaline and noradrenaline).

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of metaraminol?
questions.explanation: Metaraminol is a commonly used peripheral vasoconstrictor and sympathomimetic agent. It has both alpha and beta activity but alpha predominates. It is mainly an alpha one receptor agonist directly and indirectly through noradrenaline release. It causes vasoconstriction with increases blood pressure, a reflex bradycardia and an indirect increase in coronary artery flow. Alpha one receptors are found predominantly in blood vessels and smooth muscle. Alpha two receptors are located in the CNS and some peripheral tissues- they inhibit the release of norepinephrine and reduce sympathetic flow leading to decreased blood pressure. An example of an alpha two agonist is clonidine. Beta one receptors are located in the heart and agonism increases heart rate and contractility. Beta two receptors are found in the lungs, blood vessels and smooth muscles and activation leads to bronchodilation and vasodilation.

questions.stem: You are asked to see an unwell 45 year old female who is undergoing treatment for metastatic adenocarcinoma. She has been admitted with sudden onset shortness of breath. When you arrive their blood pressure is 70/30 and heart rate is 145 beats per minute. Their jugular veins are distended and lung fields are clear. Electrocardiogram shows low QRS voltage and a sinus tachycardia. You have di9iculty hearing their quiet heart sounds. Which of the following is likely to be DECREASED in this patient?
questions.explanation: Cardiac tamponade is a medical emergency and occurs when abnormal amounts of fluid accumulate in the pericardial sac compressing the heart and limiting the ability its ability to fill and pump e9ectively. In tamponade, the accumulation of fluid in the pericardial sac increases the intrapericardial pressure. This pressure restricts the heart's ability to fill during diastole, especially a9ecting the right side of the heart first, but eventually impacting both ventricles. This results in decreased stroke volume and cardiac output, contributing to hypotension. Intrapericardial pressure: In tamponade, the intrapericardial pressure is increased, not decreased. The fluid within the pericardial sac exerts pressure on the heart, impairing its ability to expand and fill.Jugular venous pressure (JVP): Jugular venous pressure is typically increased in tamponade due to impaired venous return from the right side of the heart. The increased pericardial pressure limits right ventricular filling, causing blood to back up in the systemic veins.Left atrial pressure: Left atrial pressure can be increased in tamponade, as the restriction in ventricular filling leads to a backup of pressure that a9ects both the right and left sides of the heart. Left ventricular end diastolic volume : This is decreased in tamponade. The increased pericardial pressure impairs ventricular filling, and particularly in the left ventricle, where the restrictive e9ect from the pericardium reduces the volume of blood that can enter the ventricle during diastole.

questions.explanation: Many congenital patients with tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) are now reaching adulthood. Tetralogy of Fallot is the combination of four heart changes which are an overriding aorta, right ventricular hypertrophy, right outflow tract obstruction, ventricular septal defect. TOF is classified as a cyanotic heart condition. In adults patients may develop pulmonary regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis, arrhythmias, ventricular dysfunction or aortic root dilatation.

questions.explanation: The HLA-B58:01 allele is associated with an increased risk of severe cutaneous adverse reactions including Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis in Asian populations taking allopurinol. HLA-B27 is associated with ankylosing spondylitis and other spondyloarthropathies. HLA-DR4 is often associated with rheumatoid arthritis and type 1 diabetes. Bone density compared to a healthy young adult; result is osteopenia
Bone density compared to an age-matched reference; result is osteoporosis

questions.explanation: A T score compares a patient's bone density to that of a healthy young adult; a score of 2.5 or lower is diagnostic of osteoporosis. In contrast, a Z score compares bone density to an age-matched population and helps identify secondary osteoporosis in younger patients.

questions.stem: A 55 year old man with a necrotizing soft tissue infection is being treated with broad spectrum antibiotics. The medical team considers adding clindamycin to the regime. What is the primary rationale for this addition?

questions.explanation: Clinidamycin is added to treat necrotizing soft tissue infections primarily to inhibit toxin production by Streptococcus pyogenes, which plays a major role in the pathogenesis of these infections. It does not primarily target anaerobic bacteria (although it has activity), nor does it directly enhance vascular perfusion.

questions.stem: A 58 year old man with relapsed di9use large B cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is being considered for CAR-T cell therapy. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of chimeric antigen receptor T-cell (CAR-T) therapy?

CAR-T cells are genetically modified to express receptors that target and kill cancer cells

CAR-T cells inhibit angiogenesis in the tumor microenvironment

questions.explanation: CAR-T therapy involves genetically modifying a patient's T-cells to express chimeric antigen receptors (CARs) that target specific antigens on tumour cells, leading to T cell mediated destruction of those cells. It does not involve the direct delivery of chemotherapy, blocking immune checkpoints or inhibiting angiogenesis.

questions.explanation: X linked dominant is an inheritance pattern that occurs when a mutated gene on the X chromosome is passed down. A single mutated gene is enough to cause the condition in both males and females however females are often less severely a9ected than males. X linked dominant is less common than X linked recessive and includes vitamin D resistant rickets and some forms of Charcot-Marie-tooth disease. A9ected males are linked by a9ected females because an a9ected male would inherit the mutated X chromosome from his mother. There is female to female transmission because females can pass the a9ected X chromosome to their daughters. A9ected females are linked by a9ected males or females.

questions.stem:
A 65-year-old man presents with an ST-elevation myocardial infarction. He has a past history of hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia and coronary disease. He has had 2 previous acute coronary syndromes in the last 12 months.
He is treated with anti-thrombotic and anti-coagulant therapies and subsequent placement of a drug eluting stent to his left anterior descending (LAD) artery. A transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates an LVEF of 55%, apical antero-septal hypokinesis and no significant valvular pathology.
His current medications include aspirin 100mg daily, metformin 1000mg XR daily, linagliptin 5mg daily and atorvastatin 20mg nocte.
His lipid profile demonstrates an LDL-C of 1.4 mmol/L. His HbA1c is 7.5%.
Once his angiogram has been performed his observations demonstrate a HR of 90bpm (NSR), BP of 140/90mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98% on RA and he is afebrile. He is euvolemic and has no features consistent with heart failure.
Which is the LEAST appropriate adjunctive therapy to commence once he returns to the ward?

A 58-year-old lady with metastatic melanoma (BRAF negative) has been receiving nivolumab for the past 6 months. She attends the oncology outpatient's clinic for routine review, reporting mild fatigue but no other symptoms of note. Her other medications include omeprazole and atorvastatin.

Her most recent blood tests are show below:

Creatinine eGFR Sodium Potassium Bicarbonate Urea Hb WCC Neutrophils Lymphocytes Eosinophils

155umol/L (previously 70umol/L) 42mL/min/1.73m2 139mmol/L 4.7mmol/L 24mmol/L 8.9mmol/L
124g/L 8.9 x 109/L 5.79 x 109/L 1.86 x 109/L 0.80 x 109/L

(45-90umol/L) (>60) (135 ­ 145) (3.5 ­ 5.0) (22 ­ 29) (2.5 ­ 7.0) (115 ­ 160) (4 ­ 11) (2 ­ 8) (1 ­ 4) (< 0.6)

Urinalysis shows sterile pyruria and mild proteinuria.

Which of the following is the next most appropriate step in management?

options.text: Continue nivolumab and repeat renal function in 1 week
Withhold nivolumab and start oral prednisolone at 0.5 ­ 1mg/kg/day
Withhold nivolumab and admit to hospital for IV methylprednisolone 2mg/kg/day
Stop omeprazole and repeat renal function in 1 week

questions.explanation: It is important to recognise this could be an iRAE from her Nivolumab therapy. Nephritis from ICPI is rare but often delayed in presentation. It typically causes an acute interstitial nephritis; found in 80-90% of patients in studies where a renal biopsy has been evaluated. As per ESMO guidelines on management of immunotherapy toxicities, this patient's laboratory results are consistent with a grade 2 nephritis. Other evidence to suggest as such include the raised eosinophils with sterile pyuria. Management at this stage should include temporary cessation of the immune checkpoint inhibitor, and commencement of oral prednisolone therapy. Other causes of renal failure should be considered, and nephrotoxic drugs should be ceased. Omeprazole can cause an acute interstitial nephritis pattern so is present as a distractor.

The concern for this patient is that they have raised intracranial pressure which is a known complication of cryptococcal meningitis which can cause significant morbidity and mortality in patients if left untreated. This risk is higher in patients with HIV who tend to have a high fungal burden within the CSF. There is also an increased risk of raised ICP in the context of Immune reconstitution syndrome if antiviral treatment has been recently commenced.

The definitive treatment is therapeutic lumber puncture. Initial LP at time of diagnosis should be performed with OP to screen patients for the complication as some may remain asymptomatic. Routinely they will have a repeat LP in 2 week to ensure fungal clearance however they should be performed earlier if patient has symptoms of raised ICP. LP should be performed to reduce OP to < 20cm or by 50% in context of extremely high CSF pressures (>50cm CSF). Daily LP until patient is asymptomatic is recommended. Lumbar drains and ventricular drains can be considered in patients where LP is unsuccessful.
Steroids should not be used routinely in the acute setting as they likely reduce the efficacy of antifungal management. There is a role if raised ICP is thought to be in the context of IRIS however.

There is no role in changing the antifungal regime in this context. Flucytosine can cause nausea and vomiting alone but is associated with bone marrow toxicity and liver toxicity. If these were to occur fluconazole can be used as an alternative agent.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old who is HIV positive is being treated for confirmed Cryptococcus infection. They have been started on empirical management with Amphotericin and Flucytosine. Day 2 of admission the patient develops severe headache, blurred vision, lethargy, nausea, and vomiting. What is the best management to alleviate their symptoms?
A 60 year gentleman presents to an outpatient clinic who has known atrial fibrillation. Please interpret the above ECG.
questions.explanation: ECG interpretation usually represents one question within the exam. The key to this question is identifying the presence of what appears to be fibrillation/flutter waves however with regular QRS complexes. This is atrial fibrillation with complete heart block.

options.text: Atrial fibrillation with complete heart block

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man is referred for evaluation of muscle and bone pain, fatigue, weakness, fractures and difficulty walking. DEXA scanning shows Z scores of between ­3 and ­4 at all sites. Serum calcium 2.3 mmol/L Serum phosphate 0.4 mmol/L PTH 98 pg/ml (10-65) 25(OH) vitamin D 69 nmol/L (62-199) 1,25 dihydroxyvitamin D 31 pmol/L (41-169) Maximum tubular phosphate reabsorption ­ low Which of the following is the best next test to order?
questions.explanation: Autosomal dominant hypophosphataemic rickets (ADHR) is a rare genetic disorder caused by mutations in the FGF23 gene which results in elevated and uncleavable FGF23 levels, leading to a buildup of the hormone. FGF23 is a phophaturic hormone that decreases the amount of phosphate reabsorbed by the kidneys, lowering phosphate levels in the blood. It also suppresses the production of calcitriol (the active form of vitamin D) in the kidneys. PTH is high in autosomal dominant hypophosphatemic rickets (ADHR) due to the mutated and overactive fibroblast growth factor-23 (FGF23) protein, which both directly stimulates parathyroid glands to produce more PTH and indirectly increases PTH by decreasing vitamin D activation.

questions.stem:
A 42 year old male collapses and has a cardiac arrest in the shopping centre. There has been no preceding chest pain or palpitations. His rhythm strip showed VF arrest. He has no past medical history. He was resuscitated successfully.
Which of the following is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation:
IHD (most likely acute ischaemia) is most likely in a 42 yo male. ARVD is uncommon, presents at a relatively young age and is progressive. Pathologically it is a degenerative condition of the right ventricle with replacement of the free wall with fibro-fatty tissue. HCM is the commonest cause of sudden cardiac death in younger people during exertion. Congenital long QT syndrome is also relatively rare, tending to present in childhood with recurrent syncope. Hypokalaemic and medication caused long QT is much more common but we don't have any info in the question regarding this ­ still rarer than IHD.
NB whilst not an option - Brugada syndrome ­ typically present with sudden death in sleep. While it was first described as disease of young Asian males, it affects a much broader demographic than that. It is much more common than ARVD or long QT, but also not as common as IHD.

The above presentation is in keeping with acute urticaria. Chronic urticaria is defined as urticaria as daily itching and wealing present for > 6 weeks. Urticaria can either be spontaneous (no clear insult) or inducible with a clear insult. Below is a list of common inducible causes of urticaria. Previously unrecognised food allergy is unlikely and the presentation with strawberries is very common and nonspecific to long term risk of allergy. Cholinergic urticaria is an inducible type of urticaria associated with sweating or hot water. Inducible causes of Acute urticaria; Viral and bacterial infections, food allergy, drug induced, vaccination, insect stings/bites. Inducible causes of chronic urticaria; Dermographism, Cold urticaria, cholinergic urticaria, delayed pressure urticaria, solar urticaria, heart urticaria, aquagenic urticaria.
questions.stem:

He reports feeling mildly unwell recently with a sore throat for which he is taking intermittent paracetamol. He recalls a previous episode of urticaria as a child associated with strawberries. On examination, there is widespread generalised urticaria with scratch marks with no signs of cardiovascular or respiratory compromise.
What is the most likely cause for his symptoms?

questions.explanation: Valproate mostly causes weight gain, as do the rest of the options, except for topiramate which causes anorexia and weight loss.

questions.explanation:
Absolute CIs: hypersensitivity, thyroid cancer (personal or FH medullary thyroid cancer or MEN2), pregnancy


Relative CIs: pancreatitis, gastroparesis, IBD, advanced stage CKD, gallbladder disease

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of death in patients with Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease?
questions.explanation: Causes of death in ADPKD 36% cardiac disease (note CKD is a major risk factor) 24% infection 12% neurological event (6% due to aneurysm rupture and 5% due to hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage)

questions.stem:
A 55-year-old woman with Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus is brought to ED semi-comatose having been ill for several days. Past medical history is significant for left ventricular failure and her current medications include digoxin and indapamide. Investigations are performed, including an arterial blood gas as shown below:
pH 7.41 PCO2 32 mmHg PO2 82 mmHg HCO3 19 mmol/L Anion gap 34 Glucose 67 mmol/L K+ 2.7 mmol/L
What is the most likely explanation for the arterial blood gas results?

options.text: Fully compensated metabolic acidosis secondary to DKA

questions.explanation:
This lady has a severe diabetic ketoacidosis complicating a pre-existing metabolic alkalosis (due to thiazide use). Both the pCO2 and bicarb are lower than normal so this disproves the option of no acidbase disorder. The glucose is very high confirming diabetic decompensation. (knowing the presence or absence of ketones would be valuable). The high anion gap indicates that there is a severe high anion gap acidosis, which is much higher than expected given the relatively modest decrease in bicarbonate. This alerts us to the possibility of a pre-existing metabolic alkalosis related to the diuretic therapy. The delta ratio here is [(34 ­ 12) / (24 ­ 19)] = 22/5 = 4.4, and this very high value is consistent with this suggestion of a high anion gap acidosis in association with a pre-existing metabolic alkalosis. As we know


there is a metabolic acidosis lets us the "one and a half plus 8 rule" (rule 5) to assess the compensatory response: The expected pCO2 is (1.5 x 19 + 8) = 36.5mmHg. This is not that much different from the actual value of 32mmHg so there is only a minor & insignificant respiratory component present. The hypokalaemia may be chronic and due to the metabolic alkalosis.

Anti RNA polymerase III is most strongly associated with scleroderma renal crisis. However recent studies have shown the development of SRC with anti-topoisomerase 1 antibodies may confer worse mortality. Steroids are known to precipitate crisis and crisis needs to be identified early to avoid significant morbidity or mortality namely in context of renal failure or adverse events from severe HTN. The treatment of this is ACE inhibitors.
Anti topoisomerase 1 is associated with diffuse scleroderma Anti U1RNP is more strongly associated with mixed connective tissue disease but may be present in patient with scleroderma and lupus. Anti centromere antibodies are associated with limited scleroderma.
questions.stem: Which antibody is most associated with scleroderma renal crisis?

questions.explanation: First rank symptoms are usually present in schizophrenia.

questions.explanation:
Studies have shown that escitalopram and citalopram are most likely of the SSRIs to cause prolongation of QTc. Recent study showed that QTc prolongation was negligible when using agents such as paroxetine, sertraline, and fluoxetine.
A Comparison of the Risk of QT Prolongation Among SSRIs - Kylee A. Funk, Jolene R. Bostwick, 2013 (sagepub.com)
questions.stem: A 28-year-old patient with depression wants to be commenced on medication. A routine ECG is performed prior to initiation of treatment which shows a QTc of 480ms. Which antidepressant is most likely to prolong QTc further?

questions.explanation: The diagnosis is Lofgren's syndrome, observed in <5-10% of patients with sarcoidosis. This is the combination of erythema nodosum, hilar adenopathy, migratory polyarthrlagia and fever seen primarily in women. The presence of all features has a 95% diagnostic specificity for sarcoidosis allowing a clinical diagnosis to be made without biopsy. Diagnosis is typically made on the presence of the classic triad of symptoms and is supported by imaging. Treatment with NSAIDs is usually adequate to control symptoms, but occasional patients will need additional therapy such as corticosteroids, immunomodulatory agents, colchicine.

options.text: Endobronchial ultrasound guided needle aspirate of an enlarged hilar lymph node

questions.stem:
A 29-year-old female presents for assessment of painful, red patches over bilateral anterior lower legs, fevers and sore and swollen joints of the hands, knees and shoulder. Her ankles appear as below. Further workup including a CXR is performed as shown.


Which of the following is the next most appropriate step in management?
Which of the following is a characteristic radiological finding of the spondyloarthropathies?
questions.explanation: Romanus lesion ­ characteristic erosions of the anterior and posterior vertebral endplates. Healing of the erosion results in sclerotic increase in density resulting in the shiny corner sign. Most easily diagnosed on MRI.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old gives a history of recurrent morning myoclonus and absences after sleep deprivation. The most likely epilepsy syndrome is:
questions.explanation: JME ­ reasonably common form of generalised epilepsy. If short on sleep may have brief myoclonus in the morning in the hands, as well as brief absence seizures. BRE typically characterised by focal sensory or motor symptoms on one side of the face, starts in childhood and typically seizures remit by adulthood/mid-adolescence. BME usually starts in infancy, not as typical to see absence seizures.

questions.stem:
A 49-year-old woman with metastatic melanoma commences on immunotherapy with ipilimumab. She has baseline symptoms of dry coughing and right pleural chest pain. At her first restaging scans after completing four doses of ipilimumab three weekly, some of her lung metastatses are reported as being larger in size than compared to baseline by 20%. Her symptoms are currently stable with satisfactory biochemistry and haematology.
What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Transient worsening of disease can be seen with ipilimumab before tumour regression. Given stable symptoms, she should be closely monitored until the next scan
Transient worsening of disease can be seen with immunotherapy before tumour regression. Given stable symptoms, she should proceed with further doses of ipilimumab
Worsening of disease on radiology is consistent with disease progression. She should be considered for nivolumab as the second-line therapy
Worsening of disease on radiology is consistent with disease progression. She should be considered for chemotherapy

questions.explanation: Pseudoprogression is a phenomenon where immune checkpoint inhibitors cause an initial, temporary increase in tumor size or new lesions before the tumor eventually shrinks and responds to the therapy. This is caused by an influx of immune cells into the tumor site, leading to inflammation and swelling that can mimic true disease progression on scans. Differentiating pseudoprogression from true progression is a challenge, leading to the development of specific criteria like the immune-related response criteria (irRC) and iRECIST, which require a follow-up scan to confirm progression.

options.text: Continue with current dose of azathioprine, and increased dose of steroid

questions.explanation: The need to increase therapy is clear here, with a further flare despite current treatment with a thiopurine and steroids. Perianal involvement signifies a high-risk patient, as does a diagnosis before the age of thirty years. Other factors resulting in high risk are: raised CRP/faecal calprotectin levels; deep ulcers on colonoscopy; tobacco use; extra-intestinal manifestations, such as arthritis, uveitis and psoriasis; and long segments of small and/or large bowel involvement.
questions.stem: A 22 year old female university student with moderately severe Crohn disease including perianal involvement presents with a further flare despite current treatment with azathioprine in conjunction with a reducing dose of steroid, which of the following is the treatment of choice?
Which one of the following features of encephalopathy in thiamine deficiency reverses most quickly with thiamine replacement therapy?
questions.explanation: The feature of encephalopathy in thiamine deficiency that reverses most quickly with treatment is ocular abnormalities, specifically the nystagmus and ophthalmopelgia.

questions.stem: Which herniation causes an ipsilateral dilated pupil?
questions.explanation:
Ipsilateral dilated pupil is associated with Uncal herniation. This is due to compression of CN III which can result in the combination of CN III palsy + dilated pupil + papilledema. This is often an earlier sign prior to increased lethargy coma and death. Causes include expanding mass lesions, trauma ­ resulting in bleeds or obstructive hydrocephalus.
Transcalvarial results from either an open skull fracture or procedurally ­ no specific symptoms associated with this.
Subfalcine is the most common form form of intracranial herniation where the frontal lobe herniates under the falx cerebri. This occurs in setting of midline shift in setting of bleeds/mass lesion.
Tonsillar herniation or "coning" is where the is herniation of the cerebellum into the formen magnum. This is associated with Cushing's triad and high mortality without intervention. Would expect low level consciousness, blood pressure instability and bradycardia.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old lady is travelling to Angola as part of a missionary group. She has a diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica and is on prednisone 10 mg daily. She had a yellow fever vaccination 15 years ago and still has the vaccination certificate. Which of the statements is correct with regards to further yellow fever vaccination for this patient:
questions.explanation: One dose enough for lifetime Prednisolone dose not high enough to be contraindicated

options.text: Yellow fever vaccination is required as she is travelling to a high risk country and it is greater than 10 years since her last vaccination
Yellow fever vaccination is contraindicated due to concurrent prednisone use
Yellow fever vaccination as a single dose is protective
Yellow fever vaccination is not required as it has been eradicated from Africa

questions.explanation: This presentation is classically in keeping with Gitelman syndrome which presents similar to a patient on thiazide therapy. Bartters present with similar physiology however presents in early childhood where Gitelmans presents in late adolescence to early adulthood. Liddles can be differentiated due to the raised Renin:aldosterone level as this would expected to be reduced in this disorder. Frusemide abuse could present with a similar metabolic picture however no concerns for medication abuse.
questions.stem: A 21 yo male presents to outpatient clinic with asthenia, myalgias and general muscle weakness. He had been noted by his GP to have hypokalaemia (2.5mmol/L) which he has been supplemented for. He was admitted for further investigation and was found despite supplementation to have a potassium of 2.9 mmmol/L. Na and CL were within normal limits. Magnesium was 0.58 mmol/L. He was also noted to have a metabolic alkalosis with a pH of 7.48, pCO2 40 and HCO3 of 32. A Renin: aldosterone level is performed which is noted to be elevated. He denies using any medications or illicit drug use. What is the likely cause of his presentation?

questions.explanation: Normal early morning cortisol does not exclude Cushing's -- all the other tests are recognised screening tests.

options.text: High dose (8 mg) dexamethasone suppression test

questions.stem: A left adrenal lesion measuring 10 x 12mm is incidentally detected in a 62-year-old woman on CT abdomen. The lesion is homogenous, has clear demarcations and has Hounsfield units of 12. The patients has a history of longstanding hypertension, well controlled on ramipril 10 mg and amlodipine 5 mg. She is obese with a BMI of 32 and appears to have mild facial plethora and an interscapular fat pad, but does not have proximal myopathy or striae. You decide to perform a series of investigations to further evaluate the adrenal mass. The following investigations are all likely to aid in diagnosis EXCEPT for:

The above scenario is most in keeping with ongoing ingestion of gluten. In some instances, this can be in the form of cross contamination of products and the patient may not be aware. Collagenous atrophy can present similarly and also results in villous atrophy however serological testing for Coeliacs disease will be negative and there will be no response to gluten free diet. There will also be notable collagen deposition on biopsy.
Pancreatic malabsorption would be typically seen in patient with chronic pancreatic insufficiency in setting of pancreatitis. This would not show villous atrophy and testing with faecal elastase would assist in establishing this diagnosis.
Intestinal Crohn's disease again would present with other macroscopic findings namely crypts and ulceration.

questions.stem: Stem cell mobilisation for the purpose of autologous stem cell transplant:

options.text: Requires the administration of myelosuppressive chemotherapy

questions.explanation: Stem cell mobilisation does not always require myelosuppressive chemotherapy, as it can be achieved with growth factors like G-CSF alone. However, combining chemotherapy with growth factors (chemomobilisation) can result in higher stem cell yields and may be used to reduce tumour burden, although it carries more side effects. In cases where mobilisation is poor with growth factors alone or with a combination of CTX and growth factors, other agents like plerixafor may be used to improve the mobilisation of stem cells.

BAL often shows lymphocytosis in hypersensitivity pneumonitis with a lymphocyte percentage over 30% considered supportive of the diagnosis. This combined with typical findings from a high-resolution CT scan and identified exposure to an inciting antigen can increase diagnostic confidence. Spirometry can suggest a restriction associated with ILD by showing a disproportionate reduction in both FEV1 and FVC but cannot definitively diagnose ILD because it does not measure total lung capacity, which is the gold standard for confirming a restrictive ventilatory defect. HP is an inflammatory condition triggered by an allergic reaction to inhaled organic dusts, not smoking; smoking appears to be protective against developing the disease but can be a risk factor for developing fibrosis in those who already have HP. While studies show promise, the level of evidence for pirfenidone's efficacy in some non-IPF fibrotic diseases is not as strong as for IPF and further research is needed.

questions.explanation: PD peritonitis commonly results from poor or incorrect handling of the PD catheter by the patient. Empirical therapy for PD peritonitis should include gram pos and gram neg cover, as although offending organisms are usually skin commensals, gut-derived organisms are not uncommon. Polymicrobial infection indicates GIT pathology and should prompt investigation for a bowel source of infection. The strongest risk factor for fungal PD peritonitis is recent antibiotic use, so antifungal prophylaxis should be given whenever antibiotics are administered.

She comes back after 6 weeks of treatment with adjuvant tamoxifen. She complains of persistent hot flushes, which are very debilitating. She is keen to persevere with tamoxifen, however would like to try something to help with vasomotor symptoms.
Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?

options.text: Hormone replacement therapy with low dose oestradiol

questions.explanation: The safest treatments for vasomotor menopausal symptoms with a history of breast cancer include nonhormonal medications like SSRIs. Both venlafaxine and paroxetine can be effective for vasomotor menopausal symptoms, but studies suggest paroxetine may offer a greater overall reduction in hot flashes, while venlafaxine may provide faster symptom relief but with more side effects. Hormone therapy generally not recommended due to the risk of stimulating any remaining cancer cells, especially in oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancers.

questions.stem: What regards to the use of trikafta (elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor) which of the following is true?

options.text: Only benefits patients with the F508del mutation
Increases the mean sweat chloride concentration

questions.explanation:
The triple drug combination of elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) is an important therapy for people with CF (PwCF) and at least one F508del mutation and for those with any other CFTR gene mutation that is responsive, based on in vitro and/or clinical trial data. Trikafta is taken orally and should be taken with fat-containing foods to improve absorption. Trikafta's three drugs work together to target defects in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) protein. Elexacaftor and tezacaftor act as "correctors," improving the protein's processing and transport to the cell surface. Ivacaftor acts as a "potentiator," binding to the CFTR protein at the cell surface and opening the chloride channel to improve its function. The combined


effect is increased quantity and function of the CFTR protein, which helps regulate the flow of chloride ions and water in the body. Trikafta significantly decreases sweat chloride concentrations in people with cystic fibrosis (CF), as it helps correct the underlying defect in the CFTR protein. Clinical trials have shown that mean sweat chloride levels can decrease by approximately 41­58 mmol/L after 24 weeks of treatment, often falling to about half of the initial levels.

questions.stem: A large-scale study compared the incidence of lung cancer between 2500 coal miners who worked above ground and 2500 coal miners who worked below ground who were matched in terms of their age and duration of occupation. Those who worked below ground were found to have an increased risk of cancer and was inferred as a causal factor. It was later discovered however that those miners who worked underground were also more likely to smoke. This phenomenon can be described as;
questions.explanation: A confounder is a variable that is associated with study exposure that can influence the study outcome. In this study it is unclear if the underground mining or increased smoking exposure or both have contributed to the outcome of the study. Selection bias appears less likely as there is two well matched groups. Measurement and misclassification bias also less likely as there is a clear exposure and documented outcome.

questions.explanation: The `funny' (pacemaker, I(f)) current is a mixed sodium/potassium inward current. Ivabradine inhibits the hyperpolarization-activated cyclic nucleotide-gated (HCN) channels, which are responsible for the If current in the SA node.

questions.explanation: Alport syndrome is a genetic disorder that typically does not recur in a transplanted kidney. Diseases that do commonly recur after a transplant include IgA nephropathy, FSGS, MPGN.

questions.stem: A 25 year-old female presents with intellectual disability and epilepsy. You organise genetic testing with a chromosome microarray. Which of these will be detected by the chromosome microarray?

options.text: Splice-site variant in the PTEN gene
Nonsense variant in the ARID1B gene

questions.explanation: Chromosome microarrays detect a gain (microduplication) or loss (microdeletion) in genetic information within a chromosome. Microarrays do not sequence the DNA code of the gene and hence cannot pick up single nucleotide changes eg. missense variant/nonsense variant/frameshift variant etc. Microarrays also cannot detect triplet repeat expansion disorders like Fragile X syndrome, Huntington's disease, myotonic dystrophy etc.

questions.stem: What cell is primarily affected in leucocyte adhesion deficiency?
questions.explanation: The primary cell affected is the neutrophil. Patient will typically present with an elevated WBC in the absence of infection. Leucocyte adhesion deficiency is a rare inherited immunodeficiency which results in immune cells not being able to reach sites of tissue injury or inflammation. There are a number of subtypes but all effect different steps of immune phagocytes attachment to the endothelium allowing for migration into the tissues.

questions.stem: The following statement regarding confidence intervals is false:

options.text: The intervals are larger with smaller sample size
They indicate the presence or otherwise of a statistical difference between two group
A 95% confidence interval means that 95% of all observed values fall within that interval

In an odds ratio, if the 95% confidence interval includes unity then no significant difference may
apply

questions.explanation: A 95% confidence interval does not mean that 95% of all observed values fall within that specific interval. A confidence interval is a range of values used to estimate an unknown population parameter (like the mean or proportion). For any single, specific interval that has been constructed, you can be 95% confident that it contains the true population parameter. The observed data points (the values in your sample) are used to calculate the interval itself. The interval is about estimating the true underlying population value, not about the spread of individual data points within your sample.

Describes a silent mutation where despite change there is no change to protein that is coded. D. This describes a nonsense mutation whereby alteration results in a stop codon resulting premature termination of the protein.

questions.explanation: The primary purpose of irradiating red blood cells is to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD), a rare but often fatal complication that can occur when transfused donor Tlymphocytes attack the patient's cells. Irradiation inactivates these lymphocytes, preventing them from multiplying and causing the disease. This is crucial for immunocompromised patients who may not be able to reject the donor cells.

questions.stem: Which of the following processes DO NOT occur in germinal centres of lymph nodes?
Teriparatide is used in the management of osteoporosis. Which of the following best describes its mechanism of action?

options.text: Competitive antagonist of the parathyroid hormone receptor

questions.explanation:
Teriparatide's mechanism of action (MOA) is that of an anabolic agent that stimulates new bone formation by preferentially stimulating osteoblasts (bone-building cells) over osteoclasts (boneresorbing cells). This action is similar to endogenous parathyroid hormone (PTH) but, with intermittent use, leads to a net increase in bone mass, density, and strength, which reduces fracture risk. It also influences calcium and phosphate homeostasis, causing a transient increase in serum calcium and a transient decrease in serum phosphate. In disease states associated with excess parathyroid hormone (PTH) in the blood, such as primary hyperparathyroidism, increased osteoclast activity, and accelerated bone resorption it may seem paradoxical that parathyroid hormone or its analogues can be a treatment for osteoporosis, a disease associated with accelerated bone loss. Ultimately, the difference in PTH effects on the bone stems from differences in the dose and pattern of exposure of bone to PTH. When under continuous exposure to PTH (eg, hyperparathyroidism), bone undergoes resorption more than formation, while intermittent exposure to low-dose PTH (like daily teriparatide administration) induces bone formation more than resorption. Although the underlying molecular mechanisms undergirding this variation in effect (ie,


catabolic effect with sustained exposure vs anabolic effect with intermittent exposure) are largely unknown, some involved molecular signalling mechanisms have been elucidated.

What is her risk of having a child affected with DMD?

Therefore, Monica has a 1 in 2 chance of being a carrier and a 1 in 4 chance of passing on a faulty DMD gene to each of her children. Only her sons would be at risk of being affected, so there is a 1 in 8 chance of an affected son.

Fever may not be present in neutropenic patients, particularly if the patient is elderly or taking corticosteroids. For the greatest survival benefit, start antibiotics as early as possible. A 46-year-old woman presents 2 hours after the onset of acute left arm weakness. CT perfusion imaging reveals an acute thrombus in her middle cerebral artery, and she undergoes thrombolysis and endovascular clot retrieval.
Subsequent duplex ultrasound imaging of both carotid arteries reveals no stenosis, and no atrial fibrillation is identified on cardiac monitoring. On day 4 of her admission, the following blood test results are returned:

Lupus anticoagulant: Beta2 glycoprotein IgG: Anti-cardiolipin IgG:

positive 122 U/mL 84 U/mL

[<20 U/mL] [<40 U/mL]

Which of the following is the most appropriate agent to prevent subsequent thrombotic events?
questions.explanation:
There is a high probability that this patient has antiphospholipid syndrome (APLS). The presence of a venous or arterial thrombotic event, plus one or more of a positive lupus anticoagulant, beta-2 glycloprotein IgG &/or anti-cardiolipin IgG (confirmed with a repeat positive result 3 months apart) satisfies the criteria for APLS.
Patients with APLS are at high risk for recurrent venous and arterial thromboembolic events, and this risk is highest in patients who test positive on all three assays ("triple positive APLS"). Regardless of the site of thrombosis, anticoagulant therapy provides superior secondary thromboprophylaxis rather than antiplatelets alone.
In triple positive APLS patients, the use of rivaroxaban was found in a randomised controlled trial to be inferior to warfarin in preventing recurrent thrombosis, particularly arterial thromboses.

questions.stem: All of the following are potential treatments in hereditary angioedema, except:

A 27-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of dyspnoea and malaise. She is experiencing haemoptysis, headaches, and fever. Her bloods show a WCC of 15.0 x 10^6 cells/L and CRP is 125 mg/L. She is otherwise fit and well. She and her partner recently moved home to a building where she states the water piping wasn't quite ready yet.
What is the most likely causative organism of this presentation?
questions.explanation:
Legionella pneumophila is found in natural water supplies and soil. It is also found in many recirculating and water supply systems. It causes haemoptysis and pleuritic chest pain. This patient likely contracted the infection from her water piping. Table from TG 2019 Community acquired pneumonia
Legionella species Legionella species are acquired from environmental sources. Patients may present with nonrespiratory symptoms, including confusion and diarrhoea. Hyponatraemia may be present. Risk factors for Legionella disease include chronic lung disease, smoking, diabetes, end-stage kidney disease, malignancy, and immune compromise, including chronic corticosteroid use. Of the 424 confirmed cases of Legionella pneumonia in Australia in 2014, Legionella pneumophila caused 46% (98% of which were serogroup 1) and Legionella longbeachae caused 39%. L. pneumophila serogroup 1 is the serogroup most commonly associated with outbreaks, whereas L. longbeachae causes sporadic infections (eg after exposure to potting mix). L. pneumophila causes more severe CAP, whereas L. longbeachae usually causes less severe CAP.

questions.stem:
A 35-year-old man with known T2 incomplete spinal cord injury, following a motor vehicle accident 3 years earlier, complains of sudden new onset severe 8/10 throbbing headache, bilateral and frontally predominant, associated with facial flushing and sweatiness, blurred vision and nasal congestion, apparently unprecipitated. On examination, BP 150-90, HR 45 regular, afebrile. Pupils constricted, minimally reactive to light. No new focal neurological signs.
What is the most likely contributor to this presentation?
questions.explanation:
Autonomic dysreflexia in spinal cord injury. From Lakra et al Relatively common in people with spinal cord injuries above the level of T6. Three times more common in those with complete spinal cord injury than in those with incomplete injury (91% versus 27%) noxious stimulus sends signals via afferent sensory nerves to the spinal cord. This triggers a sympathetic response from T6 to L2 that is pathologically exaggerated, due to intrinsic cord hyper excitability below the lesion and lack of descending modulation - akin to motor spasticity - with sympathetic response causing massive vasoconstriction of the muscular, splanchnic and cutaneous vascular beds the increase in blood pressure stimulates the carotid and aortic baroreceptors, increasing brainstem parasympathetic outflow, with resulting descending drive causing relative bradycardia and peripheral vasodilatation. The vagal drive cannot pass below the level of the lesion, such that unopposed sympathetic activity below that level can lead to lifethreatening rising blood pressure. Patients with spinal injuries below T10 rarely experience autonomic dysreflexia, as the splanchnic innervation remains intact and allows compensatory parasympathetic dilatation of the splanchnic vascular bed. Ordinarily dysreflexia is defined as an acute increase in systolic blood pressure above 20 mmHg from baseline with headache: patients with spinal cord injury above T6 tend to have systolic blood pressure between 90 and 110 mmHg, so sometimes the degree of seemingly moderate hypertension does not seem to match the symptoms. Most common causes bladder over distension, accounting for 75 to 85% of episodes stop the second most common causes over distension of the bowel. Common causes can be remembered as: bladder (infection, retention, stones, blocked catheter), bowel (constipation, impaction), boils (skin damage), bones (fractures), back passage (haemorrhoid or fissure), babies (pregnancy, sexual intercourse, breastfeeding).

What is the most important risk factor for ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation:
Family history of ovarian cancer · Early menarche (before age 12) has been associated with a slightly increased risk of ovarian cancer in some, but not all, studies · A patient who has one first-degree relative with ovarian cancer has an approximate 5% risk of ovarian cancer, a 3.5% risk if the patient has one second-degree relative, and a 7% risk if the patient has two affected relative. Much of this risk may be due to pathogenic variants in BRCA and, to a lesser extent, in other genes associated with ovarian cancer (eg, RAD51C, RAD51D, BRCA1-interacting protein 1 [BRIP1], as well as the mismatch repair genes associated with Lynch syndrome). It is estimated that genetic mutations account for up to one-quarter of ovarian cancer cases. · Infertility treatment do not appear to be independent risk factors for ovarian cancer. · Parous patients appear to be at decreased risk for ovarian cancer compared with nulliparous patients, and the risk appears to decrease with increasing parity. In one study, risk of ovarian cancer decreased by 8 % for each additional pregnancy.
In two systematic reviews, high body mass index appeared to modestly increase ovarian cancer risk (odds ratios 1.3 and 1.1), but another systematic review concluded available data were limited and inconsistent, precluding a clear assessment of risk.
Vd = 2L/kg * 100kg = 50L. Clearance = (Vd*0.693)/t1/2. Clearance is the volume of plasma cleared of drug per unit time.
