A 45-year-old male presents to outpatients with active ileocolonic inflammatory Crohn's disease despite 6 months of optimized azathioprine therapy and a previous course of prednisolone 2 months ago. He is passing four loose stools per day and has an elevated faecal calprotectin of 400 µg/g (<50 µg/g). He is particularly troubled by arthropathy. He reports 8 weeks of pain, 45 minutes of early morning stiffness and subtle swelling of his right knee, left ankle and left hip. Examination confirms mild synovitis but with full range of motion and retained weight-bearing ability. He is afebrile and otherwise well. Rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, ANA, dsDNA, ENA and HLA-B27 are negative.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next management step?



5
questions.explanation: This patient has a type I enteropathic arthritis. Type I is an acute pauci-articular inflammatory arthropathy, affecting fewer than five large usually lower limb weightbearing joints and is usually associated with active IBD. Type II is polyarticular, often symmetrical, affects a larger number of peripheral joints, and is independent of IBD activity. This patient requires step up in his maintenance therapy due to active, steroiddependent luminal Crohn's disease despite optimised azathioprine. Whilst either infliximab or ustekinumab are reasonable options, the presence of convincing extraintestinal manifestations and the more rapid onset of infliximab makes this the most appropriate choice. Whilst methotrexate has some evidence in peripheral arthopathy, it is unlikely to adequately address his luminal disease given he has failed optimised thiopurine.

A 45-year-old woman experiences relapse of diffuse large B cell lymphoma (DLBCL) 18 months after being treated to complete remission with 6 cycles of R-CHOP chemotherapy. Following 2 cycles of the 2nd line chemotherapy regimen R-ICE, a PET scan shows persistent disease in a conglomerate of retroperitoneal lymph nodes. She then receives combination chemotherapy with fludarabine and cyclophosphamide, followed by an infusion of axicabtagene ciloleucel.
24hrs after infusion, she develops a fever of 39.2 C and is admitted for investigation and is treated with IV piperacillin/tazobactam. 24hrs after admission she develops hypotension (SBP 85mmHg) and hypoxia (SaO2 85%). The hypotension is refractory to resuscitation with IV fluids and her SaO2 rises to 96% with the application of 6L/min of O2 via face mask. Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral infiltrates.
Alongside supportive care, what agent should next be administered to treat the most likely underlying diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
This patient most likely has cytokine release syndrome (CRS) which occurs in ~40-90% of patients with lymphoma following treatment with a CD19 targeting CAR T cell therapy. Fever >38.0 C is a universal feature of CRS with a median time of onset of 48hrs post infusion. Whilst all organ systems can be affected, the overall severity CRS is determined by the presence and severity of hypotension and hypoxia.
Alongside supportive care, the interleukein-6 inhibitor tocilizumab is the first line treatment for CRS and is highly efficacious in preventing the progression to severe CRS, particularly when instituted early.
Whilst corticosteroids can be used for severe and refractory CRS, their use can be associated with impaired anti-tumour CAR T cell efficacy and are thus used in highly selected patients only.



4





Whilst highly pre-treated lymphoma patients are at risk for PJP, it would not typically be associated with hypotension and its incidence is much lower than that of CRS (particularly with near-universal primary prophylaxis)

Therefore, the correct answer is a positive IgM hepatitis A antibody. https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/hepatitis-a

From Up to date: Grading strength -- The most common convention for grading muscle strength is the 0 to 5 Medical Research Council scale:
0 = no contraction 1 = visible muscle twitch but no movement of the joint 2 = weak contraction insufficient to overcome gravity 3 = weak contraction able to overcome gravity but no additional resistance 4 = weak contraction able to overcome some resistance but not full resistance 5 = normal; able to overcome full resistance
The most compelling feature of this scale is its reproducibility; an examiner is unlikely to assign a score of 1 to a muscle that another examiner graded 3 or stronger. A major limitation of the scale is that it is insensitive to subtle differences in strength. In particular, grade 4 covers a wide range, so that in most clinical situations the scale does not allow precise differentiation of the severity of weakness from one muscle to the next. Similarly, it is not a sensitive tool for documenting moderate changes in strength over time.

In eAg-negative chronic Hepatitis B, if the HBV DNA VL is > 20 000 IU/ml and the ALT is >2x ULN, treatment is indicated with consensus agreement from AASLD, APASL and EASL. questions.explanation: SDHD is subject to maternal imprinting; this means the copy you inherit from your mother is silenced. A man who carries a SDHD mutation will still have a 50% chance of passing on the mutation to each of his children (sons or daughters); his copy will not be imprinted and so any of his children that inherit the SDHD mutation would be at risk of developing paragaglioma.

questions.explanation:
This question tests knowledge of the criteria with which to classify patients with plasma cell dyscrasias into one of three categories (monoclonal gammopathy of uncertain significance (MGUS); smouldering myeloma; symptomatic multiple myeloma), and the indications for commencement of therapy in these disorders.
With the results initially available, this patient has a diagnosis of smouldering myeloma ­ they have >10% bone marrow infiltration but no CRAB features.
Combination therapy is not indicated for the treatment of MGUS or smouldering myeloma (treatment of smouldering myeloma with single agent therapy is highly controversial, but not standard practice), and thus the key to this question is identifying the investigation that will "upstage" this patient from smouldering myeloma to symptomatic myeloma.
Whilst this patient does not have CRAB features, the International Myeloma Working Group (IMWG) includes the following criteria as "Myeloma Defining Events": · >60% clonal plasma cells on bone marrow examination · Serum involved/uninvolved free light chain ration >100 · >1 focal lesion on MRI that is >5mm in size
Thus, MRI spine is the investigation that will allow definitive classification of this patient. Whilst cytogenetic and molecular studies can help predict the outcome of patients treated with VRd, they do not influence the classification of patients with plasma cell dyscrasias or the decision to institute active therapy.

questions.explanation:
Neurological complications after first dose of COVID-19 vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 infection. Patone M et al, Nat Med. 2021;27(12):2144. Epub 2021 Oct 25.
Emerging reports of rare neurological complications associated with COVID-19 infection and vaccinations are leading to regulatory, clinical and public health concerns. We undertook a self-controlled case series study to investigate hospital admissions from neurological complications in the 28days after a first dose of ChAdOx1nCoV-19 (n=20,417,752) or BNT162b2 (n=12,134,782), and after a SARSCoV-2-positive test (n=2,005,280). There was an increased risk of Guillain-Barré syndrome (incidence rate ratio (IRR), 2.90; 95% confidence interval (CI): 2.15-3.92 at 15-21days after vaccination) and Bell's palsy (IRR, 1.29; 95% CI: 1.08-1.56 at 1521days) with ChAdOx1nCoV-19 (AstraZeneca). There was an increased risk of hemorrhagic stroke (IRR, 1.38; 95% CI: 1.12-1.71 at 15-21days) with BNT162b2 (Pfizer). An independent Scottish cohort provided further support for the association between ChAdOx1nCoV and Guillain-Barré syndrome (IRR, 2.32; 95% CI: 1.08-5.02 at 1-28days). There was a substantially higher risk of all neurological outcomes in the 28days after a positive SARS-CoV-2 test including Guillain-Barré syndrome (IRR, 5.25; 95% CI: 3.00-9.18). Overall, we estimated 38 excess cases of Guillain-Barré syndrome per 10million people receiving ChAdOx1nCoV-19 and 145 excess cases per 10million people after a positive SARS-CoV-2 test. In summary, although we find an increased risk of neurological complications in those who received COVID-19 vaccines, the risk of these complications is greater following a positive SARS-CoV-2 test.

questions.stem:
Juan, 30, has a new diagnosis of dilated cardiomyopathy.
A cardiac MRI will be most useful for which of the following?

options.text: Ascertaining the aetiology of the cardiomyopathy
Identifying if he needs an upfront ICD emergently
Guiding which order guideline-directed medical therapy should be commenced in

questions.explanation: A cardiac MRI gives enormous information regarding aetiology of cardiomyopathy, depending on location of scar (delayed gadolinium enhancement) or inflammatory changes. It is often diagnostic, and almost always also helps rule out relevant differentials even when it doesn't definitively rule in an aetiology. MRI findings would not determine need for upfront ICD ­ this would depend on clinical history, for example a VT arrest. If no ventricular arrhythmia history, GDMT would always be started before ICD placed. LVEDP is determined at coronary angiography. GDMT is always commenced as per protocol, with nuances according to clinical history (for example beta-blocker intolerance, renal failure) not MRI findings.

A 24-year-old Asian man presents with acute onset weakness after eating a large pizza the night prior. In the emergency department he appears anxious, heart rate is 90 bpm, and he has symmetrical lower limb weakness [3/5]. Serum potassium is 2.1 mmol/L [3.55.0].
What is the most important next step?
questions.explanation: History, presentation, and ethnicity are typical for thyrotoxic periodic paralysis. In TTP, thyroid hormone and adrenergic stimulation stimulate the Na-K-ATPase, contributing to intermittent hypokalaemia. Carbohydrate load and exercise can be precipitators. Treatment includes cautious potassium supplementation to prevent rebound hyperkalaemia (no total body deficit), beta-blockade and restoring euthyroidism.

A couple presents to find a cause for their 18-year-old son's intellectual impairment. They wish to determine if their intellectually normal daughter is at risk of having a child similarly affected to their son. Chromosome microarray is performed on a blood sample from the son and a microduplication at Xp13 is found.
What investigation should be performed next to best interpret the microarray finding and predict the risk of recurrence for the couple's daughter?



1

questions.explanation: B and C are cognitive screens and will likely be impaired in delirium but not often used to assess for delirium per se. GCS may also be impacted by delirium but not specific for causes of impaired LOC.

JAK2. The mostly likely diagnosis in this patient is primary myelofibrosis. Her peripheral blood film is leucoerythroblastic (containing blasts and nucleated red cells) and the marrow demonstrates marked fibrosis. This condition is one of several myeloproliferative neoplasms that include polycythaemia vera and essential thrombocytosis. These disorders are characterised by a high prevalence of the JAK2 V617F variant. This mutation is observed in 95% of polycythaemia vera patients, and ~60% in both essential thrombocytosis and primary myelofibrosis. Ruxolitinib is a targeted inhibitor of JAK2 than can be used to decrease symptoms and decrease spleen size.
questions.stem:
A 50-year-old woman is reviewed in haematology clinic after being referred for splenomegaly with symptoms of abdominal discomfort. Her FBE showed a Hb 90, WCC 12, and platelets of 90. The blood film showed mild to moderate red blood cell teardrops, occasional nucleated red cells, and some rare blasts <1%. Biochemistry demonstrated increased LDH twice the upper limit of normal. She undergoes a bone marrow biopsy which demonstrated significant fibrosis and abnormal megakaryocytes.
Pathogenic variants (mutations) in which of the following genes is the most likely to be found?
What is the mechanism of action of the SARS-CoV-2 antiviral nirmatrelvir/ritonavir?

options.text: Inhibits fusion of viral and host phospholipid bilayers
Inhibits complementary DNA synthesis from a viral RNA template

questions.explanation:
Option A: Molnupiravir and remdesivir ­ nucleoside analogues that are incorporated by the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase into the new RNA strand, ultimately halting further replication



1





Option B: Paxlovid (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir) is a boosted protease inhibitor that targets the protease enzyme responsible for cutting the long polypeptide strand that is translated from the virus RNA into smaller, functional proteins ­ stuck in long form, cannot carry out functions necessary for viral replication C and D ­ not current mechanisms of action for COVID-19 antivirals
https://www.covid19treatmentguidelines.nih.gov/therapies/antiviral-therapy/ritonavirboosted-nirmatrelvir--paxlovid-/ https://www.covid19treatmentguidelines.nih.gov/therapies/antiviral-therapy/molnupiravir/ https://www.covid19treatmentguidelines.nih.gov/therapies/antiviral-therapy/remdesivir/

questions.stem:
A 20-year-old female, with consanguineous parents, is admitted with her second presentation of Strep. pneumoniae pneumonia within the last 12 months. She has a history of systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed at age 15. Her lupus is currently inactive with hydroxychloroquine treatment. Functional complement assays are performed.
Which of the following results are in keeping with her likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This patient likely has C2 deficiency (autosomal recessive) with recurrent pyogenic infections and early onset SLE. C2 deficiency affects the classical complement pathway resulting in reduced CH50 and normal AH50. Her lupus is inactive ­ if she had active lupus this could result in low C3 and C4 leading to reduced AH50 and reduced CH50 (due to consumption).
Electrodiagnostic findings in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis: Variation with region of onset and utility of thoracic paraspinal muscle examination Sanghani et al, Muscle & Nerve. 2024;69:172­178.
questions.stem: In bulbar onset motor neuron disease, the sensitivity of electromyography (EMG) in detecting craniobulbar active denervation in tongue is around...

options.text: Tumour testing showing loss of MLH1 expression with a positive BRAF V600E mutation
Diagnosis of a left sided colorectal cancer
First degree family history of papillary thyroid cancer diagnosed at the age of 50
Diagnosis of colorectal cancer at the age of 37

questions.explanation:
The diagnosis of Lynch syndrome is autosomal dominant, with clinical diagnosis suspected when a patient history and family history fulfill the Amsterdam criteria. The Bethesda guidelines were developed later to identify more patients to test for microsatellite instability (MSI), but currently it is a standard practice to test all patients with newly diagnosed colorectal cancer for MSI 6.
Why not A - Detection of a somatic BRAF V600E mutation is closely associated with MLH1 hypermethylation, which is the most common cause of deficient mismatch repair, and the presence of either of these alterations in a tumor indicates a sporadic origin and rules out Lynch syndrome Why not B ­ Lynch syndrome is more associated with synchronous colorectal cancer, metachronous colorectal cancer or other lynch syndrome associated tumors. The side of colorectal cancer is not related to Lynch syndrome. Why not C ­ thyroid cancer is not a Lynch syndrome associated tumors
questions.stem:
A 37-year-old man presents with abdominal pain and a 6kg weight loss over the past 4 months. His father was diagnosed with papillary thyroid cancer at the age of 50. He is married with no children. Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis revealed thickening of left ascending colon with high suspicion of malignancy. Colonoscope revealed an ulcerating mass, with biopsy confirming a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma.
Which of the following features suggest a stronger association with Lynch syndrome?

options.text: Take a history and examination looking for a source of chronic inflammation

questions.explanation: Chronic inflammation is an important and common cause of erythropoietin resistance, here suggested by the high ferritin but low TSat and Hb. Finding and addressing the cause is the correct answer. Iron deficiency is unlikely with normal MCV and high ferritin despite the low TSAT. Increasing erythropoietin dose is unlikely to help until the cause of inflammation is corrected.

A 55-year-old gentleman with no significant past medical history is admitted under the Respiratory service with a 48-hour history of fevers, productive cough and chest pain. Initial bloods demonstrate CRP of 150, WCC of 18.5 and neutrophils of 14.2. Sputum has been sent for routine microscopy and culture. Preliminary gram stain shows large numbers of squamous epithelial cells and some gram-positive cocci. A chest x-ray and CT chest are organised with representative images below.



3





The investigation that would most likely change the acute management (< 24 hours) of the patient is...
questions.explanation:
The diagnosis is most likely empyema. Further microbiological investigations may assist in identifying a pathogen and rationalising antibiotic therapy, but would take time to return, may not be positive (eg: sputum MCS) and would not change acute management. A pleural fluid pH (if < 7.2) would mean an ICC is indicated and would certainly change the acute management of the patient (eg: large volume thoracocentesis for complicated parapneumonic effusion vs ICC drainage for empyema).



2

questions.stem:
While axial pain and stiffness remains the main clinical feature in patients with axial spondylarthritis a variety of other clinical manifestations are increasingly recognised.
Which extra-axial manifestation would be most likely seen in a patient with ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation:
Rationale: All of these clinical features are seen in patients with ankylosing spondylitis however, the most common is enthesitis seen in nearly 30% of patients. Anterior uveitis is seen in around 23% of patients, IBD in around 7%, peripheral enthesitis in around 29% and finally skin psoriasis in around 10%.
Reference: Robinson PC, van der Linden S, Khan MA, Taylor WJ. Nat Rev Rheumatol. 2021;17(2):109-118.

questions.stem:
A 78-year-old man from a low socioeconomic group presents with progressively worsening dysphagia to solid foods. He is a current heavy smoker, has a preference for hot beverages and regularly chews areca nuts wrapped in betel leaves. He has lost 20kg and also reports haematemesis and retrosternal chest pain. He is of a low BMI and does not have a history of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease.
What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Barrett's oesophagus with progression to adenocarcinoma of the oesophagus

Squamous cell carcinoma of the oesophagus



5

questions.explanation:
GORD is associated with Barrett's oesophagus and adenocarcinoma of the oesophagus. Smoking, alcohol, hot liquids, areca nuts and N-nitroso compounds are associated with squamous cell carcinoma.



3

questions.stem:
An autosomal recessive condition has an incidence of 1 in 90,000.
What is the carrier frequency in the general population?
questions.explanation: Incidence = 1 in 90,000 Mutated allele = q, wildtype allele = p. So, q x q (q2) = 1 in 90,000, so q = 1 in 300. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium states that the frequency of carriers (pq) is double the frequency of wildtype and mutated alleles (p: 2pq: q). So, the carrier frequency is double that of q = 1/300 x 2 = 1 in 150.

questions.stem: What is the most common type of hip fracture in the elderly?
questions.explanation: Femoral neck and intertrochanteric are the most common (~ 90% fall into these 2 categories). Femoral head is quite rare and usually the result of a high-velocity event.

Protamine. Enoxaparin functions by inhibiting factor Xa more so than thrombin. In contrast to protamine reversal of unfractionated heparin, only about 60% of the anticoagulant activity is reversed by protamine in the setting of low molecular weight heparin. Nonetheless, protamine is the most appropriate agent if reversal of bleeding is required (e.g. life-threatening bleeding, not stabilised by fluids and transfusions). Indarucizumab is used for dabigatran reversal. Prothrombinex is used for reversal of warfarin and direct factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban).

What is the mechanism of action of the antiviral zanamivir for treatment of influenza virus infection?
questions.explanation: Neuraminidase inhibitors include oseltamivir, peramivir and zanamivir, that act by inhibiting the neuraminidase enzyme to prevent viral budding and release of daughter virions from the host cell to infect other cells. Peramivir is available in intravenous formulation. Other options: A ­ M2 inhibitors include amantadine, rimantadine ­ effective only against influenza A, block the M2 H+ channel leading to incomplete viral uncoating and disassembly. C- ie remdesivir, molnupiravir ­ not used for influenza. D ­ ribavirin, some evidence of benefit in RSV in immunocompromised patients to prevent progression to LRTI (in particular HSCT, lung transplant recipients).

C8 radiculopathy and medial cord plexopathy give sensory distribution extending to medial forearm, in distribution of medial cutaneous nerve of forearm. Ulnar neuropathy at elbow gives deficit as described, including the dorsal hand, and proximal medial hand. Sparing of the dorsal surface of ulnar dermatome (dorsal medial hand and two fingers) points to Guyon canal syndrome. This occurs because this region is innervated by the dorsal ulnar cutaneous branch which comes off about 5 cm proximal to the Guyon canal entrance. The hypothenar eminence is also commonly spared because the palmar ulnar cutaneous branch ­ arising proximal to the wrist - is not affected.

A 40-year-old man presents with 1 week of progressive dependent oedema and is found to have proteinuria 4+ on dipstick, with no blood. Serum creatinine is 140umol/L, eGFR 69ml/minute. BMI is 42, BP is 150/90. Renal ultrasound shows 2 kidneys, each 10.5 cm in length with normal appearances. A kidney biopsy is performed, and of the 20 glomeruli sampled, 4 show segmental sclerosis.
What feature would support a diagnosis of primary FSGS, rather than secondary?

options.text: A positive blood test for PLA2R
The presence of gross ankle oedema

questions.explanation: Consideration of Obesity associated FSGS is warranted in this man given his BMI of 42, however oedema is very uncommon in FSGS secondary to obesity. ACR 1000 and Cholesterol 9.0 are features of the nephrotic syndrome but do not differentiate between primary and secondary types. PLA2R positivity is seen in primary membranous nephropathy, not FSGS.

questions.stem:
An 80-year-old ex-smoking male with a 50 pack year history attends clinic for follow up of severe COPD. He is stable on a combined LABA/LAMA inhaler. He has not had any exacerbations of COPD in the last 12 months. He is mildly breathless on exertion. Examination records resting oxygen saturations of 87%. His chest is clear to auscultation, with globally reduced breath sounds. There are no clinical features of cor pulmonale. An ABG collected on room air demonstrates the following results.



7





FiO2 pH PaCO2 PaO2 HCO3 Base excess Na K Cl Ca Glucose Lactate Hb FCOHb FMetHb

0.21 7.45 50 53 35 6 137 4.1 105 1.2 5.0 1.5 135 1.5% 0.8%

Normal range
7.35-7.45 35-45mmHg 75-105mmHg 22-28mmol/L -3 to +3 135-145mmol/L 3.7-4.7mmol/L 101-110mmol/L 1.15-1.3mmol/L 3.5-5.4mmol/L 0-2mmol/L 120-150g/L 0.3-1.8% 0.4-1.2%

What is the next most appropriate management option?
questions.explanation:
Addition of an ICS could be considered if he had suffered from frequent exacerbations (> 2 moderate or 1 severe exacerbation). ICS is therefore not currently indicated. As he is breathless on exertion, reducing his bronchodilator therapy is not indicated. PaO2 is 53 which would mean he qualifies for the prescription of long-term oxygen therapy. This has benefits (albeit based on historical studies) in terms of improving survival in patients with COPD who adhere to 18 hours use. The addition of morphine PRN and Palliative Care referral is very reasonable, but not the next most appropriate management option given severe hypoxaemic respiratory failure.



6





Ref: Papi A, Vestbo J, Fabbri L, et al. Extrafine inhaled triple therapy versus dual bronchodilator therapy in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (TRIBUTE): a doubleblind, parallel group, randomised controlled trial. Lancet 2018; 391(10125): 1076-84.
Pascoe S, Locantore N, Dransfield MT, Barnes NC, Pavord ID. Blood eosinophil counts, exacerbations, and response to the addition of inhaled fluticasone furoate to vilanterol in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease: a secondary analysis of data from two parallel randomised controlled trials. The Lancet Respiratory medicine 2015; 3(6): 435-42. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4738443/

The usual dose of a drug given orally is 50mg once daily for people with normal renal function. Its oral bioavailability is 0.80 and its fraction excreted unchanged is 0.50.
In a person with renal impairment whose eGFR has halved, what is the ideal renallyadjusted dose for once daily oral dosing?
questions.explanation:
50% of the usual dose that is renally excreted (25mg) needs to be halved. Clearance of the other 50% (25mg) is unaffected. Hence the renally-adjusted dose = (25mg*50%) + 25mg = 37.5mg.



7

questions.explanation: 0%. Haemoglobin H (HbH) disease is an alpha thalassemia disorder that generally results from deletion of three out of the four alpha gene loci. In contrast, alpha thalassemia trait is caused by deletion of two alpha genes (in cis or trans) while hydrops fetalis is a lifethreatening disorder caused deletion of all four loci. In general, HbH is nontransfusion dependent but is characterised by haemolysis and occasional periods requiring RBC transfusions during stress. The genotype of HbH disease is a deletional --/- (note that nondeletional forms also exist). Alpha thalassemia trait can be caused by either -/- (homozygous two gene deletion) or --/ (heterozygous two gene deletion) genotypes where the two alpha gene deletions can occur in trans or cis, respectively. In this scenario, both the man and woman have the alpha thalassemia trait in trans. The genotype of the child will therefore always be -/-, meaning the child will have a 100% risk of alpha thalassemia trait but 0% risk of hydrops fetalis or HbH disease.

A 48-year-old male presents 2 days after return from Bali with fevers, photophobia and headache. He elects to stay home until he develops myalgia 5 days after leaving. On examination, he looks well, without fever, headache or any neurological signs. His GCS is 15. He also notices some mild bilateral hand swelling and joint pain. Pertinent investigations include FBE: haemoglobin 159 g/L, platelets 61 x 10^0/L (normal 150-450), lymphocytes 0.8 x 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.6 x 10^9/L, COVID-19 and other respiratory virus PCR negative, blood cultures no growth, CK 846 U/L (normal 45-250). LFTs ALT 85 U/L (normal 5-40), AST 143 U/L (normal 10-40), bilirubin normal. CRP is 1 mg/L. Urea, electrolytes and creatinine are normal. Blood film reveals marked neutropenia and occasional atypical lymphocytes with moderately reduced platelets. On further history, he did have some mosquito bites but did not go swimming and only stayed in his resort.
What is your next best test for diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This patient has quick onset fever after returning from overseas. Longer incubation periods are usually associated with other infections including malaria or typhoid fever. Dengue should be high on your list of differentials in this setting (fever within 7-10 days of returning home) and can cause a viral induced myositis. The blood film can be very useful in helping to work out etiology of infectious diseases ­ this suggests viral infection. Commonly, LFTs are mildly deranged with dengue (D). Other options ­ (B) there is negligible risk of malaria transmission in the resort areas of Bali, the patient's headache and fever has improved, if persistent, of course a lumbar puncture should be considered (C). https://wwwnc.cdc.gov/travel/yellowbook/2020/preparing-international-travelers/yellowfever-vaccine-and-malaria-prophylaxis-information-by-country/indonesia#seldyfm948

options.text: Thick and thin blood film x 3 and malaria ICT

questions.stem: What is a primary role for antibodies in resistance to bacterial infection?
questions.explanation:
One of the key functions of antibodies is to opsonise bacteria to aid phagocytosis. While antibodies also function through antibody-mediated cellular cytotoxicity, this is primarily for virally infected cells, tumour cells and parasites rather than bacteria.



8

options.text: Activation of the alternative complement pathway

Opsonisation for increased uptake by phagocytes

questions.explanation:
REF Up to date: evaluation and management of the first seizure in adults
Acute symptomatic seizure -- Acute symptomatic seizure refers to a seizure that occurs at the time of a systemic insult or in close temporal association with a documented brain insult. Such insults include metabolic derangements, drug or alcohol withdrawal, and acute neurologic disorders such as stroke, encephalitis, or acute head injury. The time window within which a seizure may be considered acute symptomatic has not been clearly defined and may vary based on the type of insult. One consensus panel recommendation suggests the following ranges: Within one week of stroke, traumatic brain injury, anoxic encephalopathy, or intracranial surgery At first identification of subdural hematoma During the active phase of a central nervous system infection Within 24 hours of a severe metabolic derangement In population-based studies, acute symptomatic seizures make up to 40 percent of first seizures. Acute symptomatic seizures may recur during the index illness but generally carry a low risk for future epilepsy compared with unprovoked seizures. That said, some patients will go on to develop remote symptomatic seizures or epilepsy related to a prior stroke, hemorrhage, or head injury.
Unprovoked seizure -- Unprovoked seizure refers to a seizure of unknown etiology as well as one that occurs in relation to a preexisting brain lesion or progressive nervous system disorder. Unprovoked seizures that are determined to be due to an underlying brain lesion or disorder are also referred to as remote symptomatic seizures. They carry a higher risk of future epilepsy compared with acute symptomatic seizures.
Epilepsy -- Epilepsy is defined as when any of the following exist [7]: At least two unprovoked seizures occurring more than 24 hours apart. One unprovoked seizure and a probability of further seizures similar to the general recurrence risk after two unprovoked seizures (eg, 60 percent) occurring over the next 10 years. This may be the case with remote structural lesions such as stroke, central nervous system infection, or certain types of traumatic brain injury. Diagnosis of an epilepsy syndrome. The second criterion was added by the International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) working group in 2014 and emphasizes the importance of neuroimaging and electroencephalography (EEG) in the evaluation of patients with a first-time seizure, as some of these patients will meet criteria for epilepsy at the time of a first seizure.
Alcohol intoxication or withdrawal and drugs of abuse should not be overlooked as a potential cause of seizure. Of note, however, a history of alcohol does not preclude other causes of seizure. In a study of 259 patients with first seizure suspected to be due to alcohol withdrawal, head computed tomography (CT) identified 16 patients (6 percent) with a clinically significant lesion (eg, subdural hematoma), and the presence of altered mental status or focal deficits did not correlate with abnormal head CT results

options.text: First seizure on day 6 post right middle cerebral artery ischaemic infarction

One unprovoked seizure and a probability of further seizures similar to the general
recurrence risk after two unprovoked seizures (80 percent) occurring over the next 10 years
Second seizure in 12 months in a 16-year-old male, each in the setting of alcohol intoxication and sleep deprivation
Two unprovoked seizures occurring 3 days apart

Which of the following is a contra-indication to kidney transplantation?
questions.explanation: Kidney transplantation is contra-indicated in those with cancer except for early-stage non-melanoma skin cancer, early-stage prostate cancer and superficial bladder cancer. For other cancers, transplantation should not be undertaken until potentially curative therapy has been completed and remission achieved, with consensus between transplant clinicians and oncologists that cure is likely, and that transplantation is justified. Obesity and substance abuse are relative contraindications and should be assessed on a case-by-case basis, but do not necessarily preclude transplantation. COPD with FEV1 < 1.0 is associated with a high risk of mortality post-Tx and is a contraindication to kidney alone transplant.

There is currently limited data on safety of orexin antagonists in patients with OSA, however lemborexant appears to be safe and well tolerated in patients with severe OSA. RLS and parasomnia disorders are not contraindications. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4702197/ https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37677076/

While axial involvement is seen in all patients with ankylosing spondylitis, peripheral joint involvement can also occur.
Which peripheral joint is most commonly affected in patients with ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation:
Although axial involvement is seen in all patients with ankylosing spondylitis, peripheral arthritis is also common in around 30% of patients. The hip is the most common joint affected and is a marker of worse disease / a poorer prognosis.
Reference: Dr Michael Gingold's DeltaMed Lectures Golder V and Schachna L. Ankylosing spondylitis: An update. Australian Family Physician 2013; 42(11): 780 ­ 784.

Mavacamten is a first-in-class drug that acts via myosin inhibition to reduce LVOT gradient in obstructive HCM. As such, it is technically cardiotoxic and frequent echocardiograms are required at initiation of mavacamten. LV dysfunction is an absolute contraindication to commencing mavacamten, and if LV dysfunction develops on mavacamten it must be ceased.

Mixing studies will distinguish whether the problem is due to factor deficiency (prolonged APTT will correct on mixing) or due to an inhibitor (specific factor inhibitor or lupus anticoagulant). The additional tests demonstrates that the patient does not have a specific factor deficiency (low factor activity levels). The most likely reason for prolonged APTT is a lupus anticoagulant which is not uncommon in patients with malignancy. These patients do not have an increased risk in bleeding or thrombosis (above that of malignancy) and should receive normal pharmacologic thromboprophylaxis.

questions.stem: In an individual with an average risk of cancer, screening of which of the following cancers has been shown to significantly decrease mortality rate?
questions.explanation:
In ovarian cancer ­ a large, randomized trial of women (N=50623) age 50-74 years who did not have increased risk of familial ovarian cancer found that screening with transvaginal ultrasound did not reduce mortality24.
In lung cancer ­ a systematic review and meta-analysis of 7 randomized controlled trials (N=84558) in patients with smoking history of more than 15 pack years revealed a reduction in lung cancer mortality of 17% (RR 0.83, 95% CI 0.76-0.91), with relative reduction of overall mortality of 4% (RR =0.96, 95% CI 0.92-1.00), by screening using low dose CT25. However, there is no evidence in screening an average risk patient without smoking history.
In prostate cancer, 3 largest randomized controlled trials conducted to assess the effect of PSA screening on prostate cancer among men 55 to 69 years of age found at most a small benefit in reducing prostate cancer mortality. Screening should be a shared decision-making between clinicians and patient after assessing the risk and benefits 26. (Reference for reading: NEJM review article on "Screening for Prostate Cancer" https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMcp2209151)
In colorectal cancer, randomized controlled trials of FOBT has shown to reduce colorectal cancer incidence and identifying earlier staged lesions 27. (Reference for reading: AJGP "Colorectal cancer screening in Australia: an update" https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2018/december/colorectal-cancer-screening-inaustralia)

(Adapted from AJGP "Colorectal cancer screening in Australia: an update")



12

questions.stem: The risk of acute kidney transplant rejection is greater...

options.text: With a negative (versus positive) virtual crossmatch
After induction with thymoglobulin rather than basiliximab
In recipients who have had a previous kidney transplant

questions.explanation: Having had a previous transplant typically induces development of anti-HLA antibodies, and PRA is typically high. Having been "sensitized" by the previous organ, the patient will be more likely to develop acute rejection to a subsequent transplant as compared to a patient receiving their first kidney transplant. A negative virtual crossmatch indicates the absence of donor specific antibodies prior to transplant, and hence lower risk of rejection. Thymoglobulin is a more potent induction agent than basiliximab. Acute rejection is most commonly experienced during the first 3 months after transplantation.

FiO2 pH PaCO2 PaO2 HCO3 Base excess Na K Cl Ca Glucose Lactate Hb FCOHb FMetHb

0.21 7.43 40 65 26 +2 137 4.2 106 1.1 4.5 0.9 149 1.2% 0.9%

Normal range
7.35-7.45 35-45mmHg 75-105mmHg 22-28mmol/L -3 to +3 135-145mmol/L 3.7-4.7mmol/L 101-110mmol/L 1.15-1.3mmol/L 3.5-5.4mmol/L 0-2mmol/L 120-150g/L 0.3-1.8% 0.4-1.2%

The most likely cause for his breathlessness is...
questions.explanation:
The A-a gradient is calculated according to the following formula: A-a gradient = PAO2 ­ PaO2
PAO2 is estimated from the alveolar gas equation. PAO2 = 150 ­ (PaCO2 x 1.25)
Therefore, the A-a gradient is calculated by... A-a gradient = [150 ­ (PaCO2 x 1.25)] ­ PaO2
The expected A-a gradient for age is calculated according to the following formula... A-a gradient (for age) = (Age + 10) / 4
The A-a gradient is elevated (35) compared to the A-a gradient expected for age (19). The only cause of a wide A-a gradient hypoxaemia in the list above is COPD and emphysema. Metabolic acidosis can immediately be excluded based on the ABG.

Please see the reference below.
Reference: Pugh D, Karabayas M, Basu N. et. al. Large vessel vasculitis. Nature Reviews Disease Primers 2021; 7: 93.

questions.stem:
The following pedigree is obtained from a family with Huntington's disease.

Individual A is 40 years old and has developed symptoms of chorea, depression, personality change and some cognitive impairment. His father started to develop symptoms at the age of 50 and his grandmother developed symptoms at the age of 65. Individual B is also 40 years old and remains asymptomatic, but the diagnosis was confirmed on genetic testing. His mother also has the disease but did not develop symptoms til the age of 60.



16





Which of the following best explains why individual B is asymptomatic compared to individual A?
questions.explanation:
Paternal transmission of a CAG repeat expansion in HD appears to be more unstable that maternal transmission. This means that the offspring of a male CAG repeat expansion carrier are at greater risk of inheriting a further expanded allele resulting in the potential for earlier onset of disease. This phenomenon is known as anticipation. An inherited the expansion from his father, whereas B inherited his expansion from his mother, so A's expansion may well have expanded further to explain his more severe disease and B is likely to have inherited a similar size expansion to his mother and present with less severe disease. HD is not a polygenic disease, although polymorphisms in other genes may modify expression. This remains unknown to date. Homozygous CAG expansions are not thought to cause any earlier onset of disease than heterozygotes. Based on the CAG repeat size, we have no way of predicting if individual B will develop symptoms in the next 12 months.



14

options.text: As a polygenetic disease, individual A is likely to have inherited other genes which modify the expression of the Huntington gene
Individual A is likely to be a homozygote while individual B is likely be a heterozygote
Individual B is likely to develop symptoms within the next 12 months and progress at the same rate as individual A
Intergenerational instability is much greater with paternal inheritance

questions.stem:
A 97-year-old man with end-stage Alzheimer dementia has been brought into hospital with reduced level of consciousness. He usually resides in a nursing home, is bedbound and non-verbal.
Thorough examination reveals a large painful sacral pressure ulcer, with likely underlying osteomyelitis. Blood tests reveal multiorgan failure. As he has no surrogate decision-maker, the treating team decide to commence an Endof-life care pathway, with morphine and midazolam charted as needed.
Which of the following ethical principles has been applied in this patient's current care?
questions.explanation: A and D do not apply when there is lack of capacity (and no given care directives or surrogate decision maker). Maleficence is not an ethical principle. NON-maleficence would also be applicable here and the physician's obligation and intention to relieve suffering overrides the foreseen but unintended harmful effects or outcome of (in this case) administering palliative care.

questions.stem:
A 70-year-old man is admitted to the cardiothoracic unit for a bioprosthetic valve replacement. Three days later he developed a right sided iliofemoral DVT and is commenced on unfractionated heparin. His platelet count begins to fall relative to the normal platelet count of 250 x10^9 cells/L he had at the end of surgery. His day 5 results are shown below.
Platelet: 30 x10^9 cells/L INR: 1.1 PT: 13 seconds (12-15) APTT: 70 seconds (25-37) Fibrinogen: 2 g/L (1.5-4)
His 4T score indicates a high probability and HIT testing is sent off.
What is the most appropriate anticoagulant to change the patient to?
questions.explanation: The immediate management is to stop the unfractionated heparin and change anticoagulant therapy. In the acute perioperative period, an infusional anticoagulant with a short life such as bivalirudin is preferred. Another option would be argatroban. Enoxaparin should be avoided due to being a heparin. Warfarin should not be used in the acute period as it can worsen thrombosis and cause skin necrosis (initial fall in protein C). However, warfarin is an option after the platelets have normalised if other anticoagulants are not appropriate. Rivaroxaban can also be used for HITT; however, given the immediate perioperative period in this scenario, a shorter acting agent would be preferred.

Latent TB section in TG, 2020 Other options A ­ she will need specialist follow up if LTBI treatment is started, B and C ­ not until D is performed. If she does not have active TB, either option is probably fine ­ chronic kidney disease can increase risk of TB reactivation, if she is to be immunosuppressed for a kidney transplant then treatment of LTBI becomes more important. There is no recommended dose adjustment for kidney dysfunction for either medication ­ however, it does require close monitoring for AEs (https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJM197307122890208). Equally, you could watch and wait if she does not meet other criteria for consideration of LTBI treatment (recent converter in last 2 years, close contact with smear + pulmonary TB case, HCW, currently immunocompromised).

A 50-year-old male has an out of hospital cardiac arrest following a bee sting requiring 5 minutes of CPR. He is reviewed in ICU where he is intubated, on vasopressor support and piperacillin/tazobactam for hospital-acquired pneumonia. A serum tryptase measured 48 hours post-admission is elevated at 150ug/L (normal <11.1ug/L). His renal function is normal. On examination the following rash is noted on his trunk.

What is the most likely explanation for his raised tryptase?
questions.explanation: Mastocytosis is the most likely cause for this patient's raised tryptase, which has remained very elevated 48 hours post-anaphylaxis. The rash is also typical of urticaria pigmentosa as is the presentation of anaphylaxis. Tryptase elevation post-mast cell degranulation in IgE-mediated reactions is transient and should normalise by 24 hours (particularly in the context of normal renal function). CPR can increase serum tryptase levels, but it would be unusual for such high levels to persist after 5 minutes of CPR.

options.text: Organise an urgent biopsy of the lesion

questions.explanation:
Spinal metastases are common in cancer, with breast, prostate and lung cancers being the most frequent causes of metastasis to vertebral column. Treatment of malignant spinal cord compression would be either radiotherapy or surgical decompression but are partly palliative. Surgery (laminectomy) is the most rapid method for relief of acute spinal cord compression and is necessary if there is spinal instability, followed by post operation radiation. However, in patients with poor underlying performance status, careful discussion is often required to determine if patient is fit for surgery and the expected neurology recovery. Option for surgery is not included in the options to avoid confusion. In this case of a likely metastatic lung cancer with spinal cord compression, poor performance status/ unlikely surgical candidate due to significant co-morbidities, urgent palliative radiation is recommended.
Figure above adapted from Ropper et al. "Acute Spinal Cord Compression", NEJM review article 201737)

A chest x-ray showed a large spiculated mass in the left upper lobe. An urgent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine was performed, revealed the following findings.

What would be the most appropriate next step in managing this case?

Delayed graft function (DGF) is defined by the requirement for dialysis within 1 week of kidney transplantation due to inadequate function of the transplanted kidney.
Regarding DGF, which of the following statements is correct?



19
questions.explanation: Use of Plasmalyte, a balanced solution containing physiological concentrations of both chloride and potassium, was associated with a 25% reduction in the incidence of DGF as compared to normal saline in a recent multicenter trial conducted in ANZ (Collins M, et al, Lancet 2023). DGF is rare in living donor kidney transplantation, but affects 3040% of deceased donor recipients and >50% if the kidney is from a donor after circulatory determination of death (DCDD). Although DGF is associated with increased risks of acute rejection, prolonged hospitalization, higher costs and reduced long-term outcomes, the vast majority of patients with DGF (>95%) regain transplant function and remain free from dialysis at 12 months.

options.text: DGF is more frequently seen following living donor transplantation than deceased donor transplantation
DGF is less likely to occur if balanced crystalloids are used for peri-operative IV fluid therapy, rather than normal saline
Fluids containing potassium should be avoided peri-operatively to reduce the risk of hyperkalaemia
25% of patients with DGF will experience transplant failure within 1 year of transplant surgery

questions.stem:
A chest x-ray requested for investigation of cough in an 85-year-old gentleman is shown below.

These findings are most likely in keeping with previous exposure to which of the following?
questions.explanation:
This chest x-ray demonstrates multiple calcified irregular pleural plaques, in keeping with previous asbestos exposure. Silica exposure is usually associated with fibrosis (and in some cases, progressive massive fibrosis). Aspergillus and wood fibre exposure do not lead to development of pleural plaques. Pleural plaques can also occur in cases of previous tuberculosis, haemothorax, talc pleurodesis, early mesothelioma, myeloma and rib fractures.
Ref: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5873627/#:~:text=Although%20bilateral %20calcified%20pleural%20plaques,mesothelioma%2C%20myeloma%20and%20rib%2 0fractures. https://radiopaedia.org/articles/pleural-plaque

questions.stem:
Symmetrical deforming polyarthropathy is one of the hallmark features of untreated rheumatoid arthritis.
Which of the following x-ray changes would be most likely to be seen in rheumatoid arthritis?
A. Distal interphalangeal joint sparing B. Juxta articular erosions with a sclerotic margin C. Resorption of the distal phalangeal tips D. Subchondral sclerosis at the MCPs

Extended MCQ
Answer Options for Q69 & Q70
questions.explanation: The classic x-rays changes seen in rheumatoid arthritis are juxta-articular osteopaenia, peri-articular erosions, soft tissue swelling leading to joint space narrowing. The DIPs are usually spared in rheumatoid arthritis but involved in psoriatic arthritis, gout, scleroderma and osteoarthritis. Juxta-articular erosions with a sclerotic margin are seen in gout. Subchondral sclerosis is a feature of osteoarthritis. Reference: Michelle Leech's DeltaMed lectures.

questions.explanation: Despite beneficial effects of testosterone on bone density, a recent RCT (PMID: 38231621) has reported that testosterone increases fracture risk compared to placebo. Fractures occurred at sites associated with trauma (e.g., ribs) and are most likely due to pleiotropic effects on testosterone on behaviour. Therefore, men at high fracture risk should be treated with osteoporotic drug therapy irrespective of whether they receive testosterone treatment or not. Testosterone has been reported to be neutral for cardiovascular events (no increased risk relative to placebo), to increase haematocrit (and reduce anaemia risk), and to reduce risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus in high-risk men.

Which of the below management strategies is the most appropriate for this patient?
questions.explanation: Octreotide is preferred as terlipressin can cause vasoconstriction and is contraindicated in ischaemic heart disease and should be used with caution in patients with cardiac arrhythmias, cerebral vascular disease, peripheral vascular disease or uncontrolled HTN. It can also cause bronchospasm and dyspnoea, therefore may worsen asthma/COPD.

Resuscitation to restore haemodynamic stability, tranexamic acid, ceftriaxone and
terlipressin.
Resuscitation to restore haemodynamic stability, lactulose and terlipressin.

questions.stem:
A 28-year-old female presents with a history of 2 early miscarriages. Investigation reveals that both miscarriages had Trisomy 21. She undergoes normal chromosome testing via banded karyotype.
What investigation should be performed next, to best investigate the cause of recurrent pregnancies with Trisomy 21?
questions.explanation: The most likely cause here is one of the parents carrying a balanced chromosome translocation involving chromosome 21 that predisposes to recurrent trisomy 21. Karyotype has excluded a translocation in the female. Microarray will not detect a balanced translocation. Whole genome sequencing will not detect a translocation either. What's missing here is whether the father carries a balanced translocation. This is best assessed via karyotype in him.

options.text: Microarray analysis in the female to ensure she carries no chromosomal abnormality that predisposes her to recurrent pregnancies with Trisomy 21
Microarray analysis in the male partner to ensure he carries no chromosomal abnormality that predisposes her to recurrent pregnancies with Trisomy 21
Banded karyotype in the male partner to exclude him carrying a balanced chromosomal translocation involving chromosome 21
Whole genome sequencing in both parents to exclude an underlying genetic cause for their recurrent miscarriages
