A 65-year-old lady with metastatic melanoma (BRAF negative) attends the oncology outpatient clinic for
review, her current treatment consists of the anti CTLA4 agent ipilimumab. She reports a change in her
bowel habits over the past week, now passing loose motions up to twelve times daily, with some
associated abdominal pain not responding to her current low dose morphine. She feels reluctant to go
outside, and almost cancelled today's appointment. After deciding she needs admission to hospital,
which is the best course of action?
questions.explanation:
From the eviQ guidelines diarrhoea >7 stools/day over baseline or interfering with ADLs is classified as
grade 3 diarrhoea and requires hospital admission for IV methylprednisolone. It is recommended that
steroids are only started in the absence of bowel perforation.

options.text: Exclude bowel perforation and start IV methylprednisolone
Exclude bowel perforation and start PO steroids
Cease ipilimumab and manage symptomatically with IV fluids and loperamide
Exclude bowel perforation and start PO metronidazole

questions.stem:
A 35-year-old woman presents with a headache that has persisted for several days. She has a history of
hypertension and is taking amlodipine 10 mg daily. She has not had hot flashes or any recent change in
weight. Her blood pressure is 170/95 mm Hg in the right arm and 169/96 mm Hg in the left arm; she has
a heart rate of 75 beats per minute and a BMI of 24. Radial and femoral pulses appear to be
synchronous, and she has normal pedal pulses. An electrocardiogram demonstrates sinus rhythm with
left ventricular hypertrophy.
Baseline laboratory testing yields the following results:

                                                          Patient value Reference range

Haemoglobin (g/L)                        132   120\xAD160

Haematocrit (%)                          38.0  36.0\xAD46.0

Potassium (mmol/L)                       3.5   3.5\xAD5.0

Creatinine (umol/L)                      97    53\xAD97

Glucose (mg/dL)                          5.3   3.9\xAD5.6

Bicarbonate (mmol/L)                     29    21\xAD30

Plasma renin activity (ng/mL per hour) 12.00   <0.6\xAD4.3

Thyroid-stimulating hormone (mIU/L)      2.3   0.5\xAD4.7

Urinalysis is negative for protein.

Which one of the following conditions is the most likely cause of this patient's hypertension?
questions.explanation:
In a young woman with new or difficult-to-control hypertension, fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) should
be considered as a cause of secondary hypertension. Significant renal artery stenosis leads to
hypoperfusion of the kidney, which activates the renin\xADangiotensin\xADaldosterone system, leads to
retention of sodium and water, and counters blood pressure control. When both kidneys are involved,
affected patients may present with acutely worsening renal function or flash pulmonary oedema from
volume overload. Imaging may show asymmetric kidney size. Diagnostic imaging of FMD may include
duplex ultrasonography, CT, or MRI, although the gold standard is renal artery angiography. The serum
creatinine level is at the upper limit of normal, which makes a primary renal parenchymal disease less
likely.
The patient's normal glucose level makes Cushing syndrome unlikely. Cushing syndrome is associated
with a suppressed plasma renin activity.
Type 4 renal tubular acidosis (hypoaldosteronism) is characterized by hyperkalaemia and is associated
with low plasma renin activity.
Coarctation of the aorta is unlikely in a patient who has synchronous radial and femoral pulses. The
classic findings of coarctation of the aorta are systolic hypertension in the upper extremities, diminished
or delayed femoral pulses (radial\xADfemoral delay), and low or unobtainable arterial blood pressure in the
lower extremities.

options.text: Coarctation of the aorta Page 3 of 43

questions.stem:
A 32-year-old man develops, swollen knees and a left ankle effusion 5 days after an episode of diarrhoea
with some blood in his stool. He is in pain and unable to walk. His CRP is 86.
Which of the following is TRUE?

options.text: Stool culture is unlikely to aid the diagnosis
Prednisone 30mg is the best initial form of management
Risk of recurrence or persistence is predicted by presence of HLA-B27 gene
Aspiration of the knees is necessary to exclude a septic arthritis

questions.explanation:
Reactive arthritis (ReA) occurs in about 5:1000 cases of gastroenteritis caused by a variety of organisms
(most commonly Salmonella and Shigella). Stool culture is very helpful in confirming current infection
and hence ReA. Risk of recurrence is greatly increased by being HLA B27 positive. Prednisone is not
indicated. Treatment is with NSAIDs and intra-articular corticosteroid injection. Aspiration is not needed
for diagnosis, as this is not a history for septic arthritis.

questions.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents with a one-week history of tender skin lesions on her lower legs. During
the interview, she notes a 2-year history of intermittent abdominal pain and diarrhea that has worsened
acutely. She has been told that she has irritable bowel syndrome and has been treated with
antispasmodics, but her symptoms have not improved. Her recent bowel movements have contained
scant amounts of blood.

Her vital signs and cardiopulmonary examination are normal. Her abdomen has hyperactive bowel
sounds and tenderness that is most prominent in the right lower quadrant; there is no rebound or
guarding. Skin examination reveals tender erythematous plaques and nodules on her shins (figure).


 Which one of the following diagnoses is most likely for this patient?
questions.explanation:
Erythema nodosum is a panniculitis characterized by tender erythematous to reddish-brown
subcutaneous nodules on the anterior shins. Given the symptoms described in this case, this patient
likely has inflammatory bowel disease\xADassociated erythema nodosum.
Erythema nodosum is a reaction pattern that may also occur in association with other conditions,
including sarcoidosis, infections, and lymphoma, or with particular medications. The most commonly
reported infectious aetiology is streptococcal infection, but erythema nodosum may also be seen with
many other infections, including tuberculosis and other bacterial, fungal, and viral infections. Oral
contraceptives are also rare causes of erythema nodosum.
Polyarteritis nodosa, a vasculitis of small to medium-sized arteries, may manifest as cutaneous nodules,
but features of purpura or livedo reticularis will often be present, indicating a more active vascular
injury pattern. Polyarteritis nodosa may be limited to the skin or associated with vasculitis of internal
organs.
Pyoderma gangrenosum is an autoimmune ulcerative process characterized by progressive cutaneous
ulceration featuring an active, inflamed, undermined border with deeply necrotic centres.
Pancreatic panniculitis may manifest similarly to erythema nodosum, as tender red nodules on the legs;
however, there is often an oily substance draining from the nodules, and the patient would have
underlying pancreatitis or pancreatic malignancy. The history in this case does not suggest a pancreatic
aetiology.

questions.stem:
A patient with a prosthetic aortic valve with no allergies is undergoing a dental tooth extraction. Which,
    if any, antibiotic should be given?
questions.explanation:
The eTG recommends pre-procedural antibiotic prophylaxis be given for patients with any of the following:
          prosthetic cardiac valve, including transcatheter-implanted prosthesis or homograft
          prosthetic material used for cardiac valve repair, such as annuloplasty rings and chords
          previous infective endocarditis
          congenital heart disease but only if it involves:
                     unrepaired cyanotic defects, including palliative shunts and conduits
                     repaired defects with residual defects at or adjacent to the site of a prosthetic patch or device
                         (which inhibit endothelialisation)
          rheumatic heart disease
    Procedures for which endocarditis prophylaxis is recommended (for patients with above cardiac conditions):
          Dental procedures --only those involving manipulation of the gingival or periapical tissue or
              perforation of the oral mucosa (eg extraction, implant placement, biopsy, removal of soft tissue or
              bone, subgingival scaling and root planing, replanting avulsed teeth).
          Dermatological or musculoskeletal procedures --only those involving infected skin, skin structures or
              musculoskeletal tissues.
          Respiratory tract or ear, nose and throat procedures --only for tonsillectomy or adenoidectomy, or
              invasive respiratory tract or ear, nose and throat procedures to treat an established infection (eg
              drainage of abscess).
          Genitourinary and gastrointestinal tract procedures--only if surgical antibiotic prophylaxis is required
              or for patients with an established infection

questions.stem:
A 52-year-old male with a history of Crohn's disease has been on adalimumb for the last 12
    months with good response. He now presents with a scaly rash on his scalp. What is the
    most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
Psoriatic skin lesions can be induced by anti-TNF-alpha therapy. The pathogenesis may involve unopposed
    production of interferon-alpha by skin plasmacytoid dendritic cells as a result of changes in cytokine balance.
    Tinea capitis primarily occurs in children. The most common clinical findings are single or multiple scaly
    patches with alopecia and patches of alopecia with black dots at follicular orifices that represent broken hairs.
    Other presentations include widespread scaling with subtle hair loss, a boggy oedematous painful plaque
    called a kerion, and favus, which characteristically exhibits multiple cup-shaped yellow crusts.
    Monilia is due to candida albicans and is more often seen in mucous membranes- it is not associated with
    treatment with anti-TNF therapy but can be seen in patients treated with JAK inhibitors.
    Erythema (chronicum) migrans is due to Lyme disease which does not occur on the scalp or in Australasia!

questions.stem:
A 70-year-old man is having difficulty walking 3 days after a right hip replacement. Your intern notices
    he is catching his right toes on the ground and proactively performs a thorough neurological
    examination. Tone is normal and there is no clonus. There is grade 4/5 weakness of right ankle
    dorsiflexion and toe extension, with normal power in other muscle groups. Knee jerks are present
    symmetrically but right ankle jerk is lost. Sensation is altered on the sole and dorsum of the right foot.
    What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
This lesion would localise best to the right sciatic nerve. Sciatic nerve palsy is a rare complication of hip
    arthroplasty (~0.5%).
    L5 damage would cause foot drop but not the loss of ankle jerk which is innervated via S1/tibial nerve.
    Tibial neuropathy would cause loss of ankle jerk, but weakness of plantarflexion (not dorsiflexion).
    The femoral nerve innervates knee extensors and knee jerk.

questions.stem:
A 68-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and a post-bronchodilator FEV1 that is
    44% of predicted has had two exacerbations in the past year requiring hospitalization and treatment
    with systemic glucocorticoids. He is minimally symptomatic between exacerbations. His comorbidities
    include tobacco use, coronary artery disease, prolongation of the corrected QT interval, and
    hypertension treated with chlorthalidone and potassium supplementation. He is currently taking regular
    inhaled tiotropium and inhaled salbutamol as required, with good compliance and proper technique. In
    addition to recommending smoking cessation, providing appropriate vaccinations, and referring to
    pulmonary rehabilitation, which one of the following pharmacologic therapies should be initiated?
questions.explanation:
For all symptomatic patients with COPD:
             o follow a stepwise approach to pharmacological treatment until adequate control of
                 breathlessness, improved functional capacity, and control of exacerbation frequency is
                 achieved.
             o use short-acting inhaled bronchodilator therapy for short-term relief of breathlessness.
     For patients receiving short-acting bronchodilators who have persistent
    troublesome dyspnoea, add a long-acting muscarinic antagonist or long-acting
    beta2-agonist (or both in combination if monotherapy is not adequate) for
    regular use.
     LAMA / LABA fixed dose combinations in a single inhaler (glycopyrronium / indacaterol,
        umeclidinium / vilanterol, tiotropium / olodaterol, aclidinium / formoterol) are available for patients
        who remain symptomatic despite monotherapy with either alone.
     Triple therapy (ICS / LABA / LAMA) should be limited to patients with repeated exacerbations and
        more severe COPD symptoms that cannot be adequately managed by dual therapy. SR HE
     Avoid long-term (> 2 weeks) use of systemic corticosteroids.
    Inhaled bronchodilators are preferred to oral bronchodilators, such as theophylline. Theophylline is not
    recommended unless other bronchodilators for long-term treatment are unavailable or unaffordable.
Page 9 of 43

options.text: Inhaled fluticasone, umeclidinium and vilanterol Page 8 of 43

questions.explanation:
Absolute indications to start dialysis include uremic pericarditis or pleuritis and progressive uraemic
encephalopathy. Common symptoms that provide indication for dialysis initiation include declining
nutritional status, fatigue and malaise and mild cognitive impairment. Other indications are persistent or
difficult-to-treat volume overload and laboratory abnormalities refractory to medical treatment
including acidosis, hyperkalaemia, and hyperphosphatemia.
questions.stem:
A 65 year old patient with progressive chronic renal failure attends for a routine clinic visit. He still works
full time. Which of the following is the strongest indication for commencing dialysis?

questions.explanation:
Explanation: Anion gap = (136+3.5) - (114+17) = 8.5 i.e. normal.
    DKA and starvation ketosis would typically demonstrate raised anion gap.
    Vomiting usually presents with a metabolic alkalosis due to loss of stomach acid and chloride deficit.
    Chloride deficit drives bicarbonate reabsorption in the kidney, maintaining the alkalosis.
    This hyperchloraemic acidosis suggests gastrointestinal losses and would be consistent with diarrhoea,
    perhaps due to laxative abuse.

Which of the following is the most likely cause of her blood gas picture?

questions.explanation: Page 12 of 43

options.text: Faecal immunochemical test 2 yearly from age 40

questions.explanation:
Cholinesterase inhibitors are not advised in those with bradycardia or conduction defects as they may
exacerbate these leading to a more profound bradycardia, heart block, or syncope. It is recommended
that an ECG is performed prior to initiating treatment, and heart rate is monitored each month during
titration.
questions.stem:
Which of the following investigation are advised prior to commencing treatment for mild dementia with
a cholinesterase inhibitor, such as donepezil?

questions.explanation:
The core features of borderline personality disorder include emotion dysregulation, impulsivity, and
    interpersonal dysfunction. DSM-5 diagnostic criteria = Pervasive instability of social relationships, self-
    image, and emotions; marked impulsivity beginning in early adulthood and present in at least five of the
    following contexts:
     Frantic efforts to avoid real or imagined abandonment, reflecting intolerance to being alone
     Unstable and intense relationships marked by abrupt and extreme shifts between idealization and
        devaluation
     Identity disturbance, seen in an unstable self-image or sense of self
     Impulsivity that is potentially self-damaging in at least two of the following areas - spending, sex,
        substance abuse, reckless driving, binge eating
     Recurrent suicidal gestures or threats, or self-mutilation
     Marked mood reactivity
     Chronic feelings of emptiness
     Frequent displays of inappropriate or intense anger
     Stress-related paranoid ideation or severe dissociative symptoms.
questions.stem:
A 23-year-old male is admitted to the general medical ward for treatment and observation
    following paracetamol overdose after a fight with his girlfriend. His history includes multiple
    presentations to the emergency department after self-harm with razors and other cutting implements,
    overdoses of prescription and over-the-counter medications, as well as injuries sustained while
    inebriated or under the influence of drugs. He describes a pattern of emotional swings in relationships
    and the majority of his self-harm attempts have been precipitated by relationship stressors. He forms
    intense relationships with ED staff and keeps a "black list" of doctors who are not allowed to treat him
    because of previous interactions.
    Which psychiatric disorder diagnosis best fits this clinical description?

A patient undergoing chemotherapy for pancreatic cancer presents with decreased GCS and pinpoint
    pupils. His medications include methadone 30mg BD for spinal metastases, metoprolol for BP control,
    ondansetron PRN for nausea, levomepromazine PRN for nausea, fluconazole for oral thrush.
    What is the likely cause of his narcosis?
questions.explanation:
Fluconazole is a cytochrome P450 enzyme inhibitor (CYP2C9 and CYP3A4). Methadone undergoes
    demethylation by CYP450 enzymes (primarily 3A4 and 2D6). Inhibition of CYP450 will lead to decreased
Page 14 of 43
     clearance of methadone and thus increased plasma levels leading to narcosis. The other concern here
    will be numerous drugs which prolong QTc.

options.text: Reduced intraluminal opioid metabolism in the small intestine due to pancreatic insufficiency
Enhancement of p-glycoprotein mediated opioid absorption by metoprolol
Decreased protein binding of methadone by direct competition with levomepromazine

questions.stem:
A 34 year old man has a 12 month history of back pain, which wakes him at night and he has stiffness in
    the morning lasting several hours. He has had 3 months of physiotherapy for his back and tried 2
    different NSAIDs without significant benefit. A lumbar spine plain film demonstrates bilateral erosion
    and sclerosis on either side of the joint. He is HLA-B27 positive.
    What is the next most appropriate therapy?
questions.explanation:
This patient has Ankylosing Spondylitis. The first line management of this is physiotherapy and NSAIDs,
    which they have trialled without improvement. Therefore the next therapeutic option is a TNF alpha
    inhibitor such as adalimumab. An IL-17 monoclonal antibody such as Secukinumab is another
    alternative.

questions.stem:
A 35 year old patient with multiple sclerosis has been on treatment with natalizumab for 18
    months, and presents now to clinic complaining of progressively worsening difficulties with
    speech.
    An MRI is shown below.

    Lumbar puncture is positive for JC virus PCR.
    What is the most appropriate next step of management?

options.text: Start IV hydrocortisone and stop natalizumab
Start IV valganciclovir and stop natalizumab
Continue natalizumab, repeat MRI brain in 4 weeks

questions.explanation:
Natalizumab treatment for multiple sclerosis is a risk factor for developing progressive multifocal
    leukoencephalopathy (PML). Therefore, natalizumab should be immediately discontinued in patients
    who develop PML. High dose glucocorticoid therapy is then recommended as empirical therapy
    especially in the context of neurological deterioration. However, PML responds poorly to treatment and
    is usually lethal. Rituximab is also associated with this condition which was first widely reported in
    patients with AIDS.
Page 16 of 43

questions.stem:
A 56 year old man with a monoclonal gammopathy (MGUS) is suspected of progressing to Multiple
    Myeloma (MM). Which of the following is FALSE?

options.text: Abnormal free light chain ratio is associated with higher risk of progression from MGUS to MM.
Serum calcium levels are unhelpful in predicting MM.
High IgA levels predicts progression to MM
Most multiple myeloma patients will benefit from Bisphosphonate therapy.

questions.explanation:
Higher risk of progression from MGUS to MM is associated with IgA, and abnormal free light chain ratio.
    High serum calcium, anaemia and elevated creatinine all suggest potential transformation to MM. All
    myeloma patients should receive bisphosphonates, as they have anti-myeloma effects and reduce
    serum calcium even if no lytic lesions are seen.
    QUESTION 24.
    A 45-year-old Queenslander is diagnosed with metastatic melanoma. Biopsy demonstrates a BRAF
    V600E mutation. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
        A. BRAF inhibitor alone
        B. Pembrolizumab
        C. BRAF inhibitor plus MEK inhibitor in combination
        D. Dacarbazine
    EXPLANATION:
    This man has metastatic melanoma with a BRAF mutation. This mutation causes activation of the MAP-
    Kinase pathway, causing upregulation of cell proliferation and survival. Therefore appropriate first line
    treatment is with a BRAF inhibitor to target this mutation and pathway. However this needs to be given
    in combination with a MEK inhibitor as over 80% of patients on a BRAF inhibitor develop resistance. The
    main mechanism of resistance is via MAP Kinase reactivation via MEK. Therefore a MEK inhibitor is given
    in combination to delay the timing of resistance development, and improve overall survival. Studies
    have shown that there is increased response rate, median survival, and overall survival with a BRAF
    inhibitor compared to dacarbazine, therefore D is not correct.

Valproic acid OD can result in severe cerebral oedema and hyperammonaemia. Paracetamol OD is
    common and can cause cerebral oedema if fulminant liver failure occurs, but this patient has only a mild
    elevation on ALT, making this extremely unlikely. The other drugs would not explain this clinical
    scenario.
Page 18 of 43

A 28-year-old woman with a complex psychiatric history is brought
    to the emergency department after a possible drug overdose over 6
    hours earlier. A range of medications was found at her home. On
    examination she is drowsy and uncooperative. There is no evidence
    of trauma and no focal neurological deficits. She is afebrile. Over
    the next four hours she becomes progressively obtunded and is
    admitted to the ICU for intubation. A CT brain is performed:
    Blood tests are as follows:
     Hb 9.8, WCC 11.2, Platelets 111
     Na+ 148mmol/L, K+ 4.9mmol/L, Urea 11.9mmol/L,

        Creatinine 128\xB5mol/L
     Ammonia 248
     ALT 100, ALP 110, GGT 90, Bilirubin 38, Albumin 26
    Which is the most likely agent she has taken as a deliberate self-poisoning?
Which of the following is a feature of a left sided Horner's syndrome?

options.text: Increased sweating of the left side of the face

questions.explanation: Features of Horner's syndrome: myosis, ptosis, anhidrosis

The patients has varicella and should be placed on airborne and contact precautions. Airborne droplet
    nuclei are particles of respiratory secretions <5 microns. Droplet nuclei can remain suspended in the air
    for extended periods and thus can be a source of inhalation exposure for susceptible individuals.
    Airborne precautions are warranted for the care of patients with suspected or confirmed tuberculosis,
    measles and varicella. Patients on airborne isolation precautions should be placed in a private room with
    negative air pressure that has a minimum of 6 to 12 air changes per hour. Doors to the isolation rooms
Page 19 of 43
     must remain closed, and all individuals who enter must wear a respirator with a filtering capacity of 95
    percent that allows a tight seal over the nose and mouth. In the setting of herpes zoster, airborne and
    contact precautions are warranted for all patients with disseminated zoster and for
    immunocompromised patients with localised zoster. Immunocompetent patients with localised zoster
    that can be contained/covered may be managed with standard precautions. Susceptible health care
    workers should not provide direct care to these patients if immune caregivers are available.

Tacrolimus is associated with a higher frequency of hyperglycaemia and diabetes compared to
    cyclosporin. Neurological complications are also more common in this group. Cyclosporin has a higher
    frequency of hypertension, hirsutism, gum hypertrophy, and dyslipidaemia.

questions.explanation:
Although neither sensitive nor specific, common complaints and signs of GPA and MPA include fatigue,
    fever, weight loss, mouth ulcers, arthralgias, rhinosinusitis, cough and dyspnoea, urinary abnormalities
    (an active urine sediment) with or without renal insufficiency, purpura, and neurologic dysfunction.
    Among patients who have GPA with ANCA, 80 to 90 percent have PR3-ANCA. The remainder of ANCA-
    positive GPA patients have MPO-ANCA.

Start with working out the total daily dose of morphine: 140 + 40 = 180
    180mg oral morphine is equivalent to 90mg IV (PO:IV is 2:1)
    So for every 4 hours it is 90mg/6=15mg
questions.stem:
A 70-year-old gentleman with metastatic bowel cancer develops nausea and vomiting. He is found to
    have a bowel obstruction and admitted. He is made nil by mouth. He usually takes long-acting morphine
    70 mg twice a day, and 2mls of morphine liquid 10 mg/ml for breakthrough pain, usually twice each day.
    The bowel obstruction has not exacerbated his pain.
    The best estimate of the equivalent parenteral four hourly dose of morphine is:

questions.explanation:
This patient has abnormalities of her posterior columns (vibration and proprioception) along with
    corticospinal tract signs. These are seen with subacute combined degeneration of the cord as seen in
    Vitamin B12 deficiency. Ankle jerks are often absent as peripheral nerve damage from the loss of dorsal
    root ganglion cells prevent a full-blown upper motor neuron picture. Metformin is associated with
    vitamin B12 deficiency, which is likely to have been exacerbated when this patient became vegan.
    Diabetic neuropathy is predominately a peripheral sensory neuropathy and usually occurs with
    prolonged microvascular disease. Tabes dorsalis as its name suggest, involves the dorsal (posterior)
    columns, with sparing of the corticospinal tracts. Friedreich's ataxia is an autosomal recessive
    degenerative condition that can also affect the posterior columns and the corticospinal tracts. However,
    it is usually manifest by the time patients are in their early 20s or 30s.

    reflexes, bilaterally extensor plantar reflexes, and loss of position and vibration senses in all extremities.
    She can walk with assistance and Romberg's sign is positive. Her memory and other cognitive functions

A 32-year-old man has been referred to the infectious diseases clinic with a new diagnosis of HIV. His
    CD4 count is 160. He is started on highly active anti-retroviral therapy. There is no current evidence of
    opportunistic infection. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE prophylactic therapy for opportunistic

options.text: None \xAD he does not require any prophylactic therapy at present.

Co-trimoxazole and fluconazole     EXPLANATION     Commonly seen infections at various CD4 levels:      CD4 Count 200 \xAD 500: Herpes zoster shingles, pneumococcal pneumonia, oral candidiasis, TB      CD4 Count 50 \xAD 200: PJP, CNS toxoplasmosis, Cryptococcus, Kaposi's sarcoma, Primary CNS          lymphoma related to EBV, non-Hodgkin's lymphoma. Initiate primary prophylaxis with cotrimoxazole (PGP          + Toxoplasmosis) Page 22 of 43       CD4 count < 100: Fluconazole prophylaxis for Cryptococcus      CD4 count < 50: Disseminated MAC, CMV retinitis, Cryptosporidiosis. Primary prophylaxis with          azithromycin 1g weekly.      Continue prophylaxis until CD4 count is recovered above the levels above for at least 3 - 6 months          (depending on the organism).

questions.explanation:
The diagnosis of eosinophilic esophagitis is based upon symptoms, endoscopic appearance, and
    histological findings. Eosinophilic oesophagitis should be suspected in patients with chronic symptoms
    of oesophageal dysfunction (e.g. dysphagia, food impaction, food refusal, abdominal pain, heartburn,
    regurgitation, chest pain, odynophagia). A history of atopic comorbidities (e.g. asthma, atopic
    dermatitis, or immediate food-type allergies) and a family history of eosinophilic esophagitis or
    dysphagia, should raise the index of suspicion.

questions.stem:
A 55-year-old male smoker presents with worsening cough, breathlessness and pleuritic left sided chest
    pain for 1 week, managed unsuccessfully by the GP with oral antibiotics. He appears well and is
    hemodynamically stable. A temperature of 38.1 degrees is recorded. ED CXR shows moderate pleural
    effusion. He is commenced on IV amoxicillin and a pleural aspirate is performed and the results are
    shown below:
     pH 7.1
     Glucose 2.0
     Protein 7.3
     Gram stain; no organisms seen
     Culture pending
    What is the next most appropriate step in his management?

options.text: Use of central venous catheter at first dialysis

questions.explanation:
Reduced BMI (less than 18) is associated with increased mortality. Increased BMI does not demonstrate
    a statistically significant additional risk when compared with normal BMI. Other factors that increase
    mortality include poor nutrition, hypoalbuminaemia, diabetic nephropathy (compared with non-diabetic
    causes of ESKD), age, unprepared access at first dialysis (i.e. use of central line), referral to nephrologist
    < 3months from dialysis initiation, comorbidities.
questions.stem:
Regarding survival in renal replacement therapy, which of the following is NOT associated with
    increased mortality

questions.explanation:
Fluoropyrimidine-related cardiotoxicity is an uncommon but potentially lethal side effect of this class of
    chemotherapy. At present, FU is the second most common chemotherapeutic agent associated with
    cardiotoxicity, after anthracyclines. The most common clinical manifestation of fluorouracil cardiotoxicity is
    chest pain, which can be either nonspecific or anginal and is often but not inevitably associated with
    electrocardiographic (ECG) changes. Symptoms may occur at rest or be effort related. Fluoropyrimidine
    cardiotoxicity tends to occur most commonly during the first cycle of administration. The median time to
    initiation of symptoms is 12 hours following infusion initiation with a range between 3 and 18 hours, although
    in animal studies, median time to symptom onset has been more variable and has been noted as late as 48
    hours into the infusion. Symptoms may develop during later cycles.
questions.stem:
A 67-year-old male receiving FOLFOX chemotherapy for colorectal cancer develops central
    chest pain shortly after his first infusion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

questions.explanation:
Jaccoud's arthropathy occurs in up to 5 to 10 percent of patients with Sj\xF6gren's syndrome or SLE and
can also occur in sarcoidosis. The joint deformities of Jaccoud's arthropathy are not caused by
destruction of joints but by loosening and lengthening of periarticular structures and tendons. The ulnar
drift or swan neck deformities caused by this disorder resemble RA superficially but can be distinguished
by the fact that they are "correctable" on physical examination: fingers with these deformities can be
moved manually back into normal alignment. In addition, radiographs in Jaccoud's arthropathy rarely
reveal the cartilage loss, erosions, or cysts that are typical of longstanding RA. Jaccoud's arthropathy can
also occur following acute rheumatic fever.
questions.stem:
A 42-year-old woman is reviewed with painful hands, mouth ulcers and Raynaud's phenomenon. On
    examination, she has ulnar deviation but normal range of motion and function. Plain x-ray reveals no
    erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40 year old female who has never smoked has been referred to respiratory clinic after discovery of a
    lung nodule that was seen on a routine chest X-ray. CT of the chest shows a 7mm left lower lobe nodule
    with smooth borders. She is asymptomatic in terms of respiratory symptoms. What is the most
    appropriate next step?
questions.explanation:
For a 6 to 8 mm solid nodule, a CT should be performed at 6 to 12 months. In a high-risk patient, or if
    size stability of the nodule is uncertain, another CT should be performed at 18 to 24 months. For a low-
    risk patient with a nodule that is clearly unchanged, follow-up can stop at 6 to 12 months. Modern CT
    surveillance protocols with contiguous thin slice (1 mm section) imaging allow for confident assessment
    of size stability even on a single follow-up examination.

questions.stem: The underlying mechanism for clozapine-induced cardiomyopathy is related to which cell type?
questions.explanation:
Studies have estimated the risk of clozapine-induced myocarditis to be 1 in 500 to 1 in 10,000. The true risk is
    likely closer to the 1 in 500 rate. Most cases of myocarditis occur early in the course of clozapine treatment, a
    median of about 16 days following the commencement of the medication. Post-mortem examination has most
    commonly revealed damaged myocytes and eosinophilic infiltration suggesting a type I IgE-mediated acute
    hypersensitivity reaction. Other contributing mechanisms may include cytokine release and high levels of
    catecholamines. A diagnosis of myocarditis should be followed by prompt discontinuation of clozapine and
    medical follow-up with the patient's primary care clinician for supportive treatment. This course will usually lead
    to spontaneous resolution. Subsequent use of clozapine in cases with clear clozapine-induced myocarditis leads to
    recurrence of myocarditis in most cases when the drug is restarted.

questions.explanation:
A type I error (also referred to as an "alpha error") is incorrectly concluding that there is a statistically
    significant difference in a dataset; the probability of making a type I error is called "alpha." A typical
    value for alpha is 0.05. Thus, a p<0.05 leads to a decision to reject the null hypothesis, although lower
    values for claiming statistical significance have been proposed.

questions.explanation:
The SAAG identifies the presence of portal hypertension. It is calculated by subtracting the ascitic fluid
    albumin value from the serum albumin value, which should be obtained the same day. The SAAG
    generally does not need to be repeated after the initial measurement. High SAAG (11 g/L or 1.1 g/dL):
    Cirrhosis, Alcoholic Hepatitis, Heart failure, massive hepatic metastases, heart failure/constrictive
    pericarditis, Budd-Chiari syndrome, Portal vein thrombosis, Idiopathic portal fibrosis. Low SSAG (<11 g/L
    or < 1.1 g/dL): Peritoneal carcinomatosis, peritoneal tuberculosis, pancreatitis, serositis, nephrotic
    syndrome.

questions.stem:
The BEST EXPLANATION for the condition represented by following pedigree is that the condition is
    subject to:
questions.explanation:
For some genes, only the allele inherited from a particular parent is expressed in the offspring. A gene
    that is expressed only from the paternally inherited chromosome is maternally imprinted (the maternal
    allele is inactivated); a gene that is expressed only from the maternally inherited chromosome is
    paternally imprinted (the paternal allele is inactivated). When such a gene is mutated, only individuals in
    whom the mutated allele is expressed will have the disease, whereas individuals whose mutated copy is
    imprinted will be healthy. As can be seen in the family pedigree above, whenever the mutation passes
    through the maternal germline, the offspring are non-manifesting carriers. Her sons will have affected
    children, whereas her daughters will again silently transmit the mutation to the next generation.
    Overall, there should be an equal number of affected individuals and non-manifesting carriers in each
    generation. Also, where there is male to male transmission of a disease conditions with X-linkage,
    paternal imprinting can also be excluded.

Bleeding from DIC/increased fibrinolysis is seen with acute promyelocytic leukaemia (APML), and is the
reason this subtype of AML used to hold the worst prognosis. The landscape has been changed with
targeted treatment; however, it remains an important cause of early mortality. APML cells have a
balanced translocation t(15;17); this translocation is the site of the retinoic acid receptor. The other
translocations here are associated with Burkitt's lymphoma t(8;14); CML t(9;22) and a distinct subgroup
of AML t(8;21).

questions.explanation: A. Incorrect. Hyperkalaemia can cause peaked T waves, however they are usually widespread and not confined to one territory as in this case. ECG features of hyperkalaemia include widespread peaked T waves, Bradyarrhythmias, PR prolongation, conduction block (LBBB, RBBB, fascicular block), QRS widening with bizarre QRS morphology. B. Incorrect. There are no features of LVH on this ECG. equivalent ECG pattern that presents without obvious ST segment elevation. This patient is suffering an acute occlusive myocardial infarction to the LAD artery and requires immediate reperfusion therapy.ECG criteria are tall, prominent symmetrical T waves in the precordial leads, upsloping ST segment depression >1mm in the precordial leads. D. Incorrect. ST segment elevation on ECG accurately localizes the culprit vessel. Non ST segment myocardial infaction does not accurately localize the culprit vessel with the exception of De Winters T wave and Wellens ECG patterns.

Disease + Disease - Test + 0.99 0.49 1.48 Test - 0.01 48.51 48.52 1 49 50 PPV = those with the disease who tested positive / all who tested positive = TP/TP+FP= 0.99 / 1.48 x 100 (to get %) = 67% (rounded)

questions.explanation: To calculate the chance of B having the condition you need to determine the chance of his mother and of his father being carries. B's father's chance can be calculated using the given incidence. Incidence of AR condition = Carrier frequency2 x 4 Carrier frequency = 1:30 Assuming B's father is from the general population his chance of being a carrier is therefore 1:30 B's mother's sibling has the condition therefore she has a 2:3 chance of being a carrier. She would have had 1 in 4 chance having the disease 2 in 4 chance of being a carrier and a 1 in 4 chance of not being a carrier. We know she doesn't have the condition so she is either a carrier or not a carrier. Therefore she has a 2:3 chance of being a carrier and a 1:3 chance of not being a carrier. Therefore the chance of both B's parents being carrier is 2/3 x 1/30 and is 2/90. If both B's parents are carrier he would then have a 1:4 chance of inherting the disease. Therefore the chance of B having the condition is 2/90 x 1/4 and is 2/360 which equals 1/180

Reassure patient that DFS is a benign ANA variant of non-pathologic significance

questions.explanation: isolated DFS ANA positivity is increasingly recognised as being mostly a benign ANA variant of non-pathologic significance in the absence of positive ENA and clinical concerns of connective tissue disease. Non-isolated DFS ANA or with positive ENA, a strong family history for autoimmune disease, or clinical symptoms/signs for autoimmunity still warrants further workup and/or monitoring. Further workup for other causes of fatigue and arthralgia such as OSA, hypothyroidism, DM, haemochromatosis should be considered in this case, despite the reassuring DFS ANA.

For meropenem question, MRSA is resistant to all beta-lactams (except ceftaroline, which is an anti-MRSA cephalosporin). Enterococcus faecium is usually resistant to all beta lactams also. Generally, less than 10% are sensitive to ampicillin, but they are generally all resistant to cephalosporins and carbapenems. In VRE, even less are ampicillin sensitive.S. maltophilia is intrinsically resistant to carbapenems due to carbapenemase production. (Which is why they are quite frequent in ICU patients who get lots of meropenem.) Best to treat this bug with Cotrimoxazole. Carbapenems are the drug of choice for severe ESBL infections as they are not so easily destroyed by the ESBL.
questions.stem:
Meropenem is most likely to effectively treat a blood-stream infection caused by which one of the
following?

options.text: Continue hydrocortisone and measure early-morning cortisol in 3 months
Restart prednisolone, 4 mg daily, and measure early-morning cortisol in 3 months
Stop twice-daily hydrocortisone and advise stress dosing only

questions.explanation: The borderline result of the cosyntropin stimulation test suggests that the patient has sufficient adrenal cortisol reserve in baseline conditions and is unlikely to require regular daily glucocorticoid replacement. The patient should be educated about hydrocortisone administration during major stress.
questions.stem:
A 63-year-old woman taking oral prednisolone for temporal giant-cell arteritis has been tapering the
dosage over a 3-year period, with the aim of discontinuing treatment. While taking prednisolone, 4
mg daily, early-morning cortisol is measured 24 hours after the last dose and is documented to be
223 nmol/L. The patient's regimen is switched from prednisolone to twice-daily hydrocortisone (10
mg upon awakening + 5 mg at lunchtime), and a short 250-mcg cosyntropin-stimulation test is
arranged. Cortisol 30 minutes after cosyntropin administration is 411 nmol/L.

Which of the following is the best next step in this patient's management?

options.text: Discontinuation of metformin and commencement of Dulaglutide 1.5mg subcut weekly
Commencement of insulin therapy starting with optisulin 10 units pre bed
Discontinuation of metformin and commencement of Semaglutide starting at 0.25 mg subcut weekly and increasing slowly over 8 weeks to 1 mg weekly
Discontinuation of metformin and commencement of Empagliflozin 10mg daily

questions.explanation: Metformin should be ceased when eGFR< 30. Empagliflozin cannot be used with this low eGFR (currently but this may change in the future) and canagliflozin showed increased amputation risk so at this time we tend to avoid a SGLT2 inhibitor in patients with previous amputation. Also a risk of euglycaemic DKA if pancreatic function poor and heavy drinker. Semaglutide should not be used in patients with proliferative retinopathy as treatment can initially worsen retinopathy. Dulaglutide has weight loss, CVS and renal benefits and can be used with a eGFR >15
questions.stem:
A 58-year old gentleman with type 2 diabetes of 12 years duration attends diabetes clinic for further
review. He has multiple micro and macrovascular complications including proliferative retinopathy
(requiring intravitreal injections), peripheral neuropathy, ischaemic heart disease, chronic kidney
disease (eGFR of 28) and a previous toe amputation. He drinks 3 standard drinks per day.
Body mass index was 27. HbA1c was 10%. He is on Metformin 500 mg BD. Besides advice on lifestyle
changes, which medication adjustments would be most appropriate:

An 80yo male presents to emergency complaining of recent onset of blisters on his skin. The initial
lesion was on the patient's buccal mucosa but rapidly progressed to include the scalp and trunk. He
noted also the lesions seem to appear in any areas of trauma or pressure. When the blisters
rupture, there is significant pain, but they heal without scarring. The patient has a past medical
history of hypertension and diabetes for which he is taking hydrochlorothiazide, ramipril and
glybenclamide and no other new medications for years.
What is the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This is Pemphigus vulgaris that affects primarily the elderly population, marked by acantholysis with a loss of cohesion between epidermal cells with the formation of epidermal blisters. Mild pressure or trauma may elicit the development of new areas of blistering, known as Nikolsky's sign. Lesions typically begin on the oral mucosa but advance to include face, scalp, trunk and axilla. Pathologically, it is marked by IgG on keratinocytes most commonly directed against desmogleins. Morbidity and mortality arise from secondary infection. Bullous pemphigoid is an autoimmune disease that creates tense blisters over lower abdomen, groin, flexor surfaces.

Multiple endocrine neoplasia (MEN) syndrome is defined as a disorder with neoplasms affecting two or more hormonal tissues in several members of the family. The most common of these is MEN type 1 (MEN1), which is caused by the gene coding the nuclear protein called Menin. MEN1 is associated with tumors or hyperplasia of the parathyroid, pancreas, pituitary, adrenal cortex, and foregut and/or subcutaneous or visceral lipomas. The most common and earliest manifestation is hyperparathyroidism with symptomatic hypercalcemia. This most commonly occurs in the late teenage years, and 93%\xAD100% of mutation carriers develop this complication. Gastrinomas, insulinomas, and prolactinomas are less common and tend to occur in the 20s, 30s, and 40s. Pheochromocytoma may occur in MEN1 but is more commonly found in MEN type 2A (MEN2A) or von Hippel-Lindau syndrome.
questions.stem:
An 18-year-old woman is evaluated at her primary care physician's office for a routine physical. She
is presently healthy. Her family history is notable for a father and two aunts with multiple endocrine
neoplasia type 1 (MEN1), and the patient has undergone genetic testing and carries theMEN1gene.

Which of the following is the first and most common presentation for individuals with this genetic
mutation?

In relation to patients with Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), which of the
following statements is TRUE?

options.text: Dapagliflozin reduces mortality and worsening heart failure only in patients with diabetes.
Dapagliflozin should not be used in patients with Type 1 Diabetes.
Dapagliflozin can be used safely in patients with any level of renal function, including in patients
Dapagliflozin should be started where possible in all patients with HFrEF, including those who are

questions.explanation: Mirabegron is a B3 agonist that acts to relax bladder muscle during the storage phase of micturition and increase bladder capacity. There is no evidence that it is more effective than solifenacin or oxybutynin, but it is an alternative option due to its different side effect profile (i.e. it is not an anti-cholinergic medication). Hypertension is the most common and concerning side effect of mirabegron. It is not associated with dry mouth, which is the most common side effect that results in discontinuation of anti-cholinergic medications.
questions.stem:
An 80-year-old woman with mild Alzheimer's disease is from home alone, independent for mobility
and personal activities of daily living but requiring assistance from her daughter for domestic tasks.
She has urge urinary incontinence and has previously trialled oxybutynin and solifenacin with
modest benefit, but ceased due to side effects. She has now commenced mirabegron 25 mg daily.

What is the greatest benefit of mirabegron as compared with oxybutynin and solifenacin?

A 61-year-old man has been experiencing tiredness, low mood, low libido, and inability to put on
muscle bulk when exercising for the last 2 years. He does not experience early-morning erections
but can maintain erections during intercourse. He has high cholesterol treated with a statin,
hypertension treated with a calcium channel blocker, and type 2 diabetes treated with metformin.
His BMI is 33.7 kg/m2.

Laboratory test results:
Serum LH = 2.5 IU/L [2.0-10.0 IU/L]
Serum FSH = 3.3 IU/L [2.0-10.0 IU/L]
Total testosterone (9 AM, fasting) = 5.6 nmol/L and 5.8 nmol/L [6.0-28.0 nmol/L]
SHBG = 12 nmol/L [20-60 nmol/L]
Hematocrit = 0.42 [0.40 to 0.54]

The best next step in management should be:
questions.explanation: Low serum testosterone is common. However, only 2% of men aged 40 to 90 years fulfilled the criteria for late-onset hypogonadism defined by European Male Ageing Study as: (1) low libido, loss of morning erections and loss of spontaneous erections; (2) low testosterone defined as total testosterone less than 8 nmol/L or a combination of testosterone <11 nmol/L and calculated free testosterone < 220 pmol/L. This man has a low SHBG level, so his calculated free testosterone is normal. His comorbidity may be contributing to his symptoms, and weight loss should be offered as an alternative to testosterone therapy.

options.text: Commence allopurinol 100mg daily with a plan to up-titrate for a target serum uric acid
Commence febuxostat aiming for a target serum uric acid < 0.36mmol/L

questions.explanation: This patient has crystal proven gout with 3 flares in the past 12 months. He should be started on urate lowering therapy. There is an elevated rate of allopurinol hypersensitivity reaction in patients with HLA-B*801 genotype. This is allele is found most commonly in people from Han Chinese, Korean and Thai descent (rate of ~ 7%). People of these ethnicities should be tested for HLA-B*5801 prior to commencing urate lowering therapy and if positive, allopurinol should be avoided and febuxostat should be used as the first line urate lowering agent (answer D).
questions.stem:
You are asked to see a 65 year-old man of Chinese descent who over the past 12 months has had 3
hospital admissions for flares of gout. On the most recent occasion, 5mL fluid was aspirated from his
right knee with urate crystals identified on microscopy. He has no tophi. The discharge paperwork
notes that he has tested positive for the HLA-B*5801 allele. He has normal kidney function and is
currently symptom free on colchicine 500mcg BD. He wishes to start long-term preventative
therapy. What do you advise:
Which of the following treatments has the best evidence base for managing frailty in elderly men?

questions.explanation: Theory: nephrotic syndrome => proteinuria, hypoalbuminaemia. Total concentration can drop in the case of phenytoin whilst unbound concentration stays the same. There is an increased fraction unbound \xAD the ratio of unbound concentration to total concentration. The unbound concentration is pharmacologically active. Increasing the dose based on the total phenytoin level would lead to toxicity, hence a free phenytoin level should be measured instead.

options.text: Double the daily oral dose of phenytoin
Give an IV bolus of phenytoin and continue the daily oral dose
Increase the dosing interval to BD
Continue the current dose of phenytoin

questions.stem:
A patient on oral phenytoin that is seizure free for 6 months also develops nephrotic syndrome.
Their total phenytoin level is 30 umol/L (therapeutic reference range 40-80 umol/L). What is the best
next step?

An 18 year old previously well patient presents with a 3 month history of abdominal pain and weight
loss. They report no change to their diet or exercise regime. The patient reports that their stools
have become looser and that they seem to stain their underwear despite enthusiastic wiping. On
examination of the abdomen they have central tenderness with active bowel sounds.

On perianal examination you note a small non-tender sinus at 3 o'clock which is discharging a small
amount of mucous. You perform a colonoscopy which reveals aphthous ulceration of the terminal
ileum.

What is the most appropriate management of this patients condition?

options.text: Oral Mercaptopurine with dose adjusted based on TPMT
IV Infliximab in combination with azathioprine
Oral Prednisolone in combination with oral ciprofloxacin
Subcut Adalimumab with dose adjusted based on trough level

questions.explanation: Patient has fistulising Crohn's. The likely fistula is minor and doesn't appear infected or complex based on a lack of symptoms. A. not correct as MCP isn't effective against fistulising disease. Also dose is adjusted based on TGN rather than TPMT B. Correct as combination therapy is desired C. Steroids and ciprofloxacin might be used earlier but are not maintenance therapy. Anti TNF therapy is a better choice. D. the second best option however without the azathioprine not as good. levels adjusted based on trough has less convincing data than IV infliximab. Source: eTG, PBS, ECCO guideline

Tumour lysis syndrome. A. Calcium phosphate stones form in alkaline urine so alkalinization will increase the likelihood of stone precipitation in these patients. B. Allopurinol is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor and decreases UA production, but has no effect on existing uric acid. C. Rasburicase converts uric acid to allantoin and can rapidly lower serum UA. D. RRT may be required in situations of severe acute kidney injury, acidaemia or hyperkalaemia.

options.text: Urine alkalinization increases the risk of calcium phosphate precipitation
Allopurinol will metabolize pre-existing uric acid in the blood
A single dose of rasburicase can rapidly lower the serum uric acid level
Kidney replacement therapy may be needed for patients with severe kidney injury and

questions.stem:
A 45-year-old female with relapse AML and a WCC of 129 (74% blasts) is admitted for treatment
with hydroxyurea. One day after treatment, her creatinine increases from 100umol/L to
238umol/L.

On examination, her blood pressure was 113/76mmHg and pulse rate is 110bpm. She appears
unwell.

Her blood tests show:
Na 139mmol/L
HCO3 10mmol/L
Creatinine 327umol/L
Urea 25mmol/L
Phosphate 2.39mmol/L
Uric acid 55mmol/L
WCC 34
Hb 99g/L
Platelets 13
LDH 4500 U/L
Urinalysis 1+ blood 1+ protein, no casts

Which one of the following statements about the treatment of patients with this condition is
FALSE?

options.text: Refer to palliative care because she has advanced HCC

questions.explanation: She has metastatic HCC therefore not a candidate for liver transplantation. She is clinically well with compensated cirrhosis (Child A) therefore further treatment to control the progression of HCC should be considered, rather than palliative care in the first instance. Currently 3 medications are available for systemic palliative treatment for advanced HCC in Australia. The combination of atezolizumab & bevacizumab showed better progression-free survival than sorafenib and lenvatinib therefore should be considered as first line treatment.
questions.stem:
A 60 year old woman presented with one month of abdominal pain and CT scan showed multifocal
liver tumours typical of hepatocellular carcinoma, portal vein thrombus and lung metastases. She
has long standing mildly abnormal LFTs from fatty liver and background of diabetes mellitus, obesity,
hypercholesterolaemia and hypertension.

She is otherwise well and fulfills the criteria of Child Pugh A. The most appropriate treatment for her
is:

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulphate and delivery within 24 hours
Close observation with daily monitoring of fetal welfare

questions.explanation: This is a case of severe hypertension in late pregnancy, without features of preeclampsia or fetal compromise. The most appropriate intervention is treatment of the maternal blood pressure. Intravenous labetalol would be preferred in this setting as it is fast-acting and safe in pregnancy. Oral methyldopa may be safely used in pregnancy but has a slow onset of action and is therefore will not be useful in management of severe hypertension.
questions.stem:
A 35 year old woman who is currently 35 weeks gestation in her first pregnancy presents to the
antenatal clinic with persistent headache. Her pregnancy has been unremarkable to date.
On examination, her blood pressure is found to be 190/100. Heart rate is normal at 80 beats per
minute. There is no right upper quadrant tenderness or clonus on examination.
Further testing reveals a normal full blood count, biochemistry and liver function tests. Urine
protein:creatinine ratio is not elevated.

CTG shows normal fetal heart rate variability. Duplex ultrasound shows normal uterine artery
Dopplers with fetal growth on the 50th centile.

The most appropriate next step is:

options.text: Liver cirrhosis secondary to non-alcoholic steatohepatitis

questions.explanation: This patient has low SAAG ascites (Serum albumin (34) \xAD ascites albumin (27) = 7). This is consistent with malignant ascites and other causes of low SAAG. All other causes are of high SAAG.
questions.stem:
A 69-year-old male presents to the emergency department with increasing abdominal distension
over the last four weeks. He also reports shortness of breath and mild abdominal discomfort. On
examination he has a distended abdomen which is tense but not tender. There is shifting dullness.
He is afebrile. He has mild pitting oedema of the lower limbs. He has a past medical history of poorly
controlled Type 2 diabetes, obesity, ischaemic heart disease and chronic kidney disease. He also has
a history of prostate cancer diagnosed 4 years ago, for which he had a transurethral resection of the
prostate (TURP). His last PSA 6 months ago was 0.02ug/L.

On further questioning he tells you that he drinks alcohol, 2-3 standard drinks, 3 out of 7 nights a
week and has done so for many years. He also has a few tattoos.

After an abdominal x-ray ruled out any bowel distension, he had a diagnostic paracentesis.

The results are as follows:

Serum tests:
Albumin: 34g/L
Total Protein: 74 g/L
Creatinine: 330umol/L
ALT: 26U/L
AST: 39U/L
GGT: 64U/L
ALP: 70U/L

Ascitic fluid analysis:
Glucose: 7.1 mmol/L
Protein: 42g/L
Albumin: 27g/L
LDH: 786U/L

Which of the follow is the most likely cause for his ascites?

Which of the following is the earliest finding visible on plain X-Ray of a patient with rheumatoid
arthritis?

Robert Smith is 75 years old with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of lung, with extensive bone
metastases. His bone pain, previously problematic, is well controlled with MS Contin 60mg twice
daily, and he has been at this dose for 4 weeks. He presents with a history of nausea and vomiting,
followed by now reduced level of consciousness.

Blood investigations show eGFR 75ml/min, Corrected calcium 2.35, unremarkable liver function
tests. Full blood count shows WCC 15 (12 neutrophils). CT brain demonstrates extensive intracranial
metastases, with midline shift. There does not appear to be any signs of distress.

What is the most appropriate way to manage his baseline analgesia?

options.text: Cease MS Contin and chart morphine 2.5 mg 4th hourly prn
Cease MS Contin and commence a subcutaneous infusion with 20mg morphine over 24 hours
Cease MS Contin and commence a subcutaneous infusion with 40mg morphine over 24 hours
Cease MS Contin and commence a subcutaneous infusion with 60mg morphine over 24 hours

questions.explanation: His pain is currently controlled so there is no indication for dose increasing. To convert from oral morphine to subcutaneous equivalent, the oral dose is divided by 3 (based on bioavailablity comparisons). Thus 60mg bd PO = 120mg PO =40mg subcutaneously.
Pancreatitis occurs in around 1 in 2,000 pregnancies. Most cases of pancreatitis in pregnancy are gallstone related
questions.stem:
A 29-year-old woman who is 30 weeks pregnant is admitted to the Emergency Department with
central abdominal pain. Initial blood tests show the following: Amylase 1,438 u/dl

What is the most likely cause of this presentation?

questions.explanation: Explanation: see Appelbaum P., Assessment of Patients' Competence to Consent to Treatment. NEJM 2007; 357:18. 1834 \xAD 40.

options.text: For a patient to have capacity, they must be able to understand and retain information about a
Capacity requires that a patient be able to communicate their decision in some way
Capacity is assessed through cognitive testing
The ability to weigh information in order to make a decision, is an important factor in

questions.stem: Capacity is a legal term. Which of the following statements is INCORRECT?
When should a patient be referred for further review regarding narcolepsy?
questions.explanation: This is a question on the symptoms of narcolepsy. 1 -3 may be symptoms of narcolepsy, therefore 4 is correct.

options.text: Abnormal behaviours or sensations during sleep
Longstanding tiredness in spite of adequate sleep time at night and adequate treatment of any
Weakness in their legs when they feel strong emotions

questions.explanation: Clarithromycin is a strong inhibitor of CYP3A4 and potentiates the toxic effects of simvastatin. Other drugs that can cause a similar reaction include azole systemic antifungal agents and ritonavir.
questions.stem:
A 65-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with four days of progressive proximal
muscle pain and weakness. She was seen by her General Practitioner a week ago for management of
dyspnoea and cough productive of purulent sputum. She was started on a course of clarithromycin
(500 mg twice daily) to treat an infective exacerbation of longstanding chronic obstructive
pulmonary disease (COPD). She has a past medical history of atrial fibrillation and ischaemic heart
disease with regular medications including apixaban (5 mg daily), metoprolol (50 mg twice daily) and
simvastatin (40 mg daily.

On examination, she is noted to have significant muscle tenderness as well as weakness (3/5) to the
proximal muscles of her upper and lower limbs. There is no observable rash and cardiorespiratory
examination is unremarkable other than a finding of quiet breath sounds.

Investigations reveal:
Creatinine kinase 8750 U/L (normal <211)
Creatinine 124 micromol/L (normal 45 \xAD 90)
Haemoglobin 160 g/L (normal 115 \xAD 165)
Neutrophils 2.6 x109/L (normal 2.0 \xAD 7.5)
Platelets 306 x109/L (normal 150 \xAD 400)
CXR: hyperinflated lung fields without consolidation

The MOST LIKELY diagnosis is:

options.text: Check if she is on an ACE inhibitor
Check whether the previous hand swelling responded to antihistamine
Administer adrenaline and determine whether there is evidence for a response

questions.stem:
A 16-year old girl presents to the emergency department with acute onset asymmetrical swelling of
her lips associated with crampy abdominal pain. There is no airway compromise or hypotension. She
has had intermittent episodes of hand swelling for the last 2 years. There has not been any history of
pruritis or urticaria.

Which of the following options is the LEAST important priority to pursue when it comes to
diagnosis?

A 75yo male with a background of hypertension and AF is attending hospital for an elective open
cholecystectomy. He is currently on Warfarin with an INR of 2.3, what is the most appropriate
management of his perioperative anticoagulation?
questions.explanation: Perioperative Bridging Anticoagulation in Patients with Atrial Fibrillation NEJM 2015; 373: 823-833

options.text: Continue warfarin until day of surgery and give prothrombinex on the morning of surgery,
Cease warfarin 5 days prior to surgery, restart 24 hours after surgery
Cease warfarin 5 days prior to surgery, commence enoxaparin 3 days prior to surgery, restart
Cease warfarin 48 hours prior to surgery, administer vitamin K 24 hours prior to surgery, restart

questions.explanation: A - this might be useful for CIDP (symmetrical but length-independent sensorimotor demyelinating neuropathy) or MS (upper motor neuron signs - for limbs, often transverse myelopathy pattern) but presentation not suggestive of either. B - might cause sensory neuropathy but typically large fibre and dorsal columns (joint position sensation) more affected than spinothalamic tract. C - anti-MAG antibody is associated with distal acquired demyelinating (DADS) neuropathy which is symmetric, lenth-dependent, often with upper limb tremor. It should show an axonal not demyelinating pattern. D - amyloidosis typically causes carpal tunnel syndrome early then small fibre neuropathy (burning feet + postural hypotension symptoms), lethargy, background
questions.stem:
A 60-year old is referred with burning pain in his feet now progressing to his knees. This keeps him
up at night and he has also been progressively tired during the day, and gets intermittent light-
headedness on standing. He has a background of carpal tunnel syndrome treated with surgical
release 2 years ago, and congestive cardiac failure managed by his GP. He is taking olmesartan 40
mg daily and frusemide 40 mg daily. He does not drink alcohol or smoke. His HbA1c is 5.0. He has no
family history of neurological disease or malignancy.

On examination of his lower limbs, he has normal tone, power, and reflexes aside from absent ankle
jerks. He has intact joint position sensation but reduced pinprick sensation to the knees. NCS of the
lower limbs shows a mild length-dependent neuropathy with reduced sensory nerve amplitudes
distally; motor studies are normal.

Which of the following tests most likely to be ABNORMAL, in support of a cause to this
presentation?

options.text: She should be offered treatment with molnupiravir even though she is asymptomatic.
She has COVID-19 reinfection and should be isolated.
The result is likely to represent infection 24 days ago and she does not need isolation.
The motor vehicle accident is likely due to long COVID-19 syndrome.

questions.stem:
A 72 year old woman is admitted to hospital after a motor vehicle accident when she sustained a
fractured femur. She had developed cough, fever and headache 24 days before admission. She was
tested for COVID-19 by PCR and was positive 21 days before admission.

She had fully recovered fully by day 4 of illness (20 days before admission). She has no cough, sore
throat, fever or other symptoms of COVID-19 today. A COVID-19 PCR test taken yesterday is
positive with a cycle threshold value of 38.

Which is the best answer?

Janice is an 82 year old female who presents to hospital with 3 days of confusion and new urinary
incontinence. Janice lives with her husband, John who reports that she has had disrupted sleep, has
been unable to follow simple instructions and has been talking about her deceased Mother as
though she is still alive for the last few days.

Blood tests and a urine microscopy and culture is sent, the results are presented below. Her past
medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus on oral hypoglycaemic agents,
hypertension, stage 3b chronic kidney disease, osteoarthritis, anxiety, previous bilateral total knee
replacement and right cataract surgery. Janice is treated appropriately during her hospitalisation
and after 4 days she returns to her baseline and is discharged home.

         Specimen: Urine
         Leucocytes > 100 x 10^6/L
         Erythrocytes < 10 x 10^6/L
         Squamous epithelial cells < 10 x 10^6/L
         Culture: >10^8/L of E.coli, sensitive to Cefalexin, Amoxicillin, Penicillin, Trimethoprim

She is followed up in memory clinic 6 weeks after discharge from hospital. Her husband reports that
over the last 18 months she has become more forgetful. Her husband now needs to manage the
finances and supervise her cooking as she has burnt food on the stove several times. Janice can no
longer do the grocery shopping on her own as she gets disorientated at the local shops and once got
lost trying to get home. Her husband prompts her to take her medications and reminds of her
appointments. She is often repetitive in her conversations.

She completes an MMSE in the clinic appointment, her break down in scores is presented below:
         Orientation 5/10
         Registration 3/3
         Attention and calculation 5/5
         Recall 0/3
         Language and visuoconstruction 7/9
         Total score 19/30

What is Janice's most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Short term memory (recall) and orientation deficits are the hallmark cognitive deficits in Alzheimer's dementia and her history is in keeping with her MMSE score. Her attention is normal so she does not have a delirium, although she did when she was in hospital. There is no history of previous cerebrovascular accidents/disease making vascular dementia incorrect. She does not have clinical features of Lew body dementia, such as visual hallucinations, and visuospatial and executive cognitive deficits.

options.text: Commence Piperacillin/Tazobactam plus Gentamicin plus Vancomycin
Commence Piperacillin/Tazobactam plus Vancomycin plus Filgrastim

questions.stem:
A 28-year-old man has a neutrophil count of 0.01 x 109 /L [2.0-7.5] following induction
chemotherapy for acute myeloid leukaemia. It has been 10 days since the chemotherapy was
completed and he has been noted to be febrile with a temperature of 38.2\xB0C. He is clinically well
with stable vital signs. He has a Hickman catheter in situ which has become erythematous along its
subcutaneous tract. Chest X-ray is clear.

After taking appropriate cultures, which of the following is the most appropriate first line of
management?

questions.explanation: ESC Guidelines https://academic.oup.com/eurheartj/article/36/42/2921/2293375#108776023

options.text: Large pericardial effusion (>20 mm on echocardiography)
Lack of response to NSAIDs after at least one week of therapy.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a predictor of poor prognosis in patients with pericarditis?

Which of the following tasks poses difficulty for a patient with semantic variant frontotemporal
degeneration?

A 40-year-old man receives treatment with oral Metronidazole for C. difficile diarrhoea which
resolves his symptoms. He has repeat culture on a formed stool sample one week later, which is still
positive for C. difficile toxin.

What treatment do you give?
questions.explanation: Culture will be positive for several weeks. No symptoms so no treatment required.

questions.stem:
A 34-year-old man is brought to the emergency department because of a tonic-clonic seizure. He
tested positive for HIV three years ago. In the emergency department, he is conscious but confused.
His temperature is 37.2\xB0C (99\xB0F), pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 18/min.

Physical examination shows cachexia, oral thrush, and isolated weakness of lateral gaze on the right.
Laboratory studies show a CD4 count of 35 cells/\xB5L. An MRI of the brain shows multicentric ring-
enhancing lesions in the brain and meninges. The patient undergoes a brain biopsy for diagnostic
histologic confirmation.

From which of the following cell types did the lesions most likely arise?
questions.explanation: Lymphoma in immunocompromised patient with low CD4 count.

options.text: Another course of higher of prednisone at a higher dose.

questions.stem:
A 24 year old man with severe ulcerative colitis has ongoing bloody diarrhoea despite mesalazine 4g
daily for 2 year, azathioprine at 2mg/kg for 6 months and a recent 6 week course of prednisone.

What is the next best treatment?

A 26 year old woman presents with a history of juvenile myoclonic epilepsy since age 16. Her last
generalised tonic clonic seizure was 6 months previously associated with medication non-adherence.
Her current antiseizure medication regime is sodium valproate 700,g bd. She is planning to conceive
within the next 12 months and wants advice re antiseizure medication.

The best advice would be:

options.text: Wean sodium valproate and remain medication free during pregnancy
Transfer to carbamazepine 400mg bd prior to conception
Transfer to levetiracetam 750mg prior to conception

questions.stem:

questions.explanation: CVID is an antibody disorder in which patients have low IgG levels, with low IgA and/or low IgM. This can result in an infective phenotype with patients most commonly experiencing frequent, bacterial, sinus and lower respiratory tract infections with typical organisms. A number of genetic causes have been identified, but it does not tend to be X-linked. A diagnosis of PJP is more suggestive of a combined immunodeficiency. Defective NADPH oxidase is seen in patients with chronic granulomatous disease.

questions.stem: Lung biopsy has the greatest diagnostic yield in which of the following vasculitic syndromes?
questions.explanation: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener) is a small-vessel vasculitis that involves the lung in >80% of cases. One-third of patients with radiographic abnormalities may be asymptomatic. It typically also involves the upper respiratory tract and the kidney. Surgical biopsies of radiographically abnormal pulmonary parenchyma have a diagnostic yield of approximately 90% in patients with granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener). Biopsy may also differentiate vasculitis from infection or malignancy. The yield of bronchoscopic transbronchial biopsy is substantially lower than surgical biopsy. Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis and IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Sch\xF6nlein) are small-vessel vasculitides that typically involve the skin and kidney. Polyarteritis nodosa is a medium- vessel vasculitis that typically involves the mesenteric vessels. Cutaneous vasculitis represents the most common vasculitic feature and can be seen in a broad spectrum of settings including infections, medications, malignancies, and connective tissue diseases.

From the details of the food she ate, allergy to nuts is the likely cause of her anaphylaxis.
Mammalian meat allergy is a possibility in this case with such a delayed presentation.
Exercise, such as dancing has no clear role in anaphylaxis.

questions.explanation: Commentary: A. Tryptase is produced by both Mast Cells and Basophils, though most dynamic tryptase change is due to Mast cells. Trytptase and histamine are released when a mast cell degranulates, generating kinins and anaphylotoxins that help drive the features of anaphylaxis. A baseline serum tryptase is typicall between 1 ans 11.4ng/ml, but rises quickly in anaphylaxis. Unlike histamine, tryptase clears from serum slowly (half life 2 hours) and so measurement can be useful for up to 6 hours after anaphylaxis. A rise in tryptase is considered significant if it is 1.2 x basline +2 ng/ml. Performing a tryptase helps to differentiate anaphylaxis from vasovagal reactions, septic shock, carcinoid syndrome and myocardial shock. B. Nut allergy typically occurs within less than 10 minutes in 50%, a further 23% at10-30 minutes, 14% 30-60 minutes, and 5% only over 2 hours. A delay of 6 hours makes Nut allergy unlikely, though one would still test for it. C. Mammalian meat allergy typically presents hours after meat consumption. The allergen, alpha-gal-1,3 gal is a carbohydrate that needs to be processed in the gut, and bind to a gycolipid or glycoprotein to become allergenic. This takes time so patients can present with recurrent episodes of generalized urticaria, angioedema, gastroenteritis, or anaphylaxis with no immediate cause obvious as the reaction is hours after the meal. The allergy is triggered in susceptible people by previous tick bite. D. Exercise induced anaphylaxis is a specific subset of anaphylaxis most frequently associated with wheat, other grains, nuts and shellfish. Co-factors other than exercise can also make patients more susceptible to a severe reaction including alcohol, NSAIDs, extremes of temperature, premenstrual and ovulatory phases of the menstrual cycle, and sepsis.

There are two recognized clinical forms of myotonic dystrophy, both of which are characterized by autosomal dominant inheritance. Myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) is the most common form and the most likely disorder in this patient. Characteristic clinical features of this disorder include a "hatchet-faced" appearance, due to wasting of the facial muscles, and weakness of the neck muscles. In contrast to the muscular dystrophies (Becker and Duchenne), distal limb muscle weakness is more common in DM1. Palatal, pharyngeal, and tongue involvement are also common and produce the dysarthric voice that is frequently heard. The failure of relaxation after a forced handgrip is characteristic of myotonia. Myotonia can also be elicited by percussion of the thenar eminence. In most individuals, myotonia is present by age 5, but clinical symptoms of weakness that lead to diagnosis may not be present until adulthood. Cardiac conduction abnormalities and heart failure are also common in myotonic dystrophy. Diagnosis can often be made by clinical features alone in an individual with classic symptoms and a positive family history. An electromyography would confirm myotonia. Genetic testing for DM1 would show a characteristic trinucleotide repeat on chromosome 19. Genetic anticipation occurs with an increasing number of repeats and worsening clinical disease over successive generations. Myotonic dystrophy type 2 (DM2) causes proximal muscle weakness primarily and is also known by the name proximal myotonic myopathy. Other features of the disease overlap with DM1. Acid maltase deficiency (glucosidase deficiency, or Pompe disease) has three recognized forms, only one of which has onset in adulthood. In the adult-onset form, respiratory muscle weakness is prominent and often is the presenting symptom. As stated previously, Becker and Duchenne muscular dystrophies present with primarily proximal muscle weakness and are X-linked recessive disorders. Becker muscular dystrophy presents at a later age than Duchenne muscular dystrophy and has a more prolonged course. Otherwise, features are similar to one another.
