questions.explanation: 65% of vaginal cancers 50% of vulva cancers 35% of penile cancers 60% of oropharyngeal cancers Gardasil 9 provides fully vaccinated people with protection against nine types of HPV including: types 16 and 18, the two types that cause the majority of HPV-related cancers the five next most common HPV types associated with cervical cancer (types 31, 33, 45, 52 and 58) two non-cancer-causing HPV types (types 6 and 11), which cause 90% of genital warts The clinical history and MRI findings are classical for glioblastoma multiforme. Pathologically, glioblastomas are typified by palisading necrosis, vascular hyperproliferation and extensive mitoses. Molecular findings are the presence of a TERT promoter (variable), EGFR amplification (variable), wild type expression of IDH (universal) and loss of ATRX (universal). Typical radiological findings include butterfly distribution across the midline, central hypo-enhancement consistent with necrosis, a thick, hyper-enhancing peripheral rim and multifocality. Subependymal extension is not a feature of glioblastoma, and is typical, if not pathognomonic of primary CNS lymphoma. Limited scleroderma is characterised by limited skin involvement which does not extend proximal to the elbows or knees and does not extend beyond the face. Other features of limited scleroderma are longstanding Raynaud's phenomenon, digital ischaemic ulcerations, group 1 pulmonary hypertension and positive anti-centromere antibodies, while ILD, group 3 pulmonary hypertension and scleroderma renal crisis are less likely. Diffuse scleroderma involves more extensive skin involvement and greater association with ILD, group 3 pulmonary hypertension, scleroderma renal crisis and tendon friction rubs, with positive anti-Scl70 antibodies. Thiazide diuretics cause mild hypercalcaemia, a well-known side effect, by DECREASING urine calcium excretion. They also cause natriuresis, kaliuresis, and hyperuricaemia by decreasing urine uric acid excretion. Aeromonas species infections are most associated with freshwater wound exposures. Streptococcus pyogenes is classically associated with coral cuts. Vibrio species infections are most associated with saltwater wound exposures. Mycobacterium marinum is most associated with saltwater and brackish water exposures via inoculation (punctures during cleaning fish tanks are classic). Page 2 of 12

Tirzepatide is a dual incretin, with agonist action on both the Glucagon Like Peptide 1 (GLP1RA) and
glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide receptors. When compared to active GLP1RA therapy
for obesity, with or without diabetes, it is associated with significantly better control of
hyperglycaemia and greater weight loss
When compared to the GLP1RA: Liraglutide 3mg once daily, what mechanism best explains greater
efficacy on glycaemic and metabolic measures of Tirzepatide 5mg once weekly?

options.text: Dual action on GLP1 and GIP receptors
A modified amino acid, fatty acid chain

questions.explanation: The modified 39 amino acid peptide C20 fatty acid moiety means tight binding to albumin and reduced degradation. This is the same process that allows Dulaglutide and Semaglutide to be OW therapies, which are demonstratedly superior to the earlier OD and BD GLP1RA agents (Liraglutide & Exenatide respectively). While Tirzepatide 5mg is superior to Liraglutide, it is not the dosage per se. The enhanced effects on insulin sensitivity are due to the dual action on GLP1 and GIP receptors but at this dosage of Tirzepatide, not the mechanism that best explains the enhanced greater efficacy. The clinical vignette is suggestive of an internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO). The left relative afferent pupillary defect and reduced visual acuity is suggestive of a left optic neuropathy. This would be highly suggestive of a pathology affecting both the optic nerve and brainstem, which in a young female is most likely multiple sclerosis. A pontine stroke should not affect visual acuity. HSV encephalitis causes memory disturbance and encephalopathy. Linda is a 65-year-old lady with non-specific interstitial pneumonia and Sjogren's syndrome. She has
been on treatment with mycophenolate mofetil 1g BD for three years.

that there was increasing fibrosis and pulmonary function test showed a worsening in relative forced
vital capacity percentage predicted of 10%. Blood test showed positive ANA [1:1280 titre, speckled
pattern] and positive SSA-52 Ab of 314 AU/mL and SSB Ab of 96 AU/mL (normal range is <40). You
decide to start her on prednisolone and made plans for a review in three months.

You review her in clinic today, and while she reports some improvement to her shortness of breath,
she also reports some adverse effects to prednisolone. You decide to start weaning the dose
prednisolone and want to modify her remaining immunosuppressive therapy. What is the most
appropriate approach?
questions.explanation: The clinical description is scleroderma (Raynaud's and swollen hands), while the creatinine and HB suggest microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (scleroderma renal crisis). The blood film will show schistocytes and low platelets. Macrocytosis is for B12/folate, spur cells are chronic liver, and RBC agglutination is for haemolysis due to cold agglutinin disease. This patient has features of acute on chronic liver failure with a Coombs negative haemolytic anaemia. Notably, his ALP is low (excess copper can interfere with ALP synthesis). These are features of fulminant Wilson's disease. His alcohol history is not significant enough to cause the degree of clinical and biochemical derangements. His ferritin is elevated but non-specific with borderline elevated transferrin saturation levels. There is progressive fibrosing ILD. Nintedanib is the drug of choice. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa1908681 There is no evidence for changing mycophenolate to azathioprine. Page 7 of 12

limb and right lower limb. His background is significant for hypertension, dyslipidaemia and a 20-

questions.explanation: disease-specific aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibody, which plays a direct role in the pathogenesis. Eculizumab in AQP4-IgG-seropositive NMOSD is an approved treatment option. As per guidelines should aim to get SBP <125 with ACEI/ARB Lateral medullary syndrome (a.k.a. Wallenberg's syndrome) is a brainstem vascular syndrome that can result from occlusion of the PICA or vertebral artery (usually vertebral thrombosis). It is caused by thrombosis more commonly than embolus. Clinical features of lateral medullary syndrome include: - ipsilateral ataxia, vertigo and nystagmus - ipsilateral facial decreased pain and temperature sense - ipsilateral Horner's syndrome - contralateral body decreased pain and temperature sense - hoarseness (helps localise to medulla rather than pons), dysphagia and ipsilateral decreased taste Note: A variable portion of the lateral medulla is also supplied by branches of the vertebral artery. The posterior inferior cerebellar artery supplies - lateral medulla - most of the inferior half of the cerebellum - inferior vermis The anterior inferior cerebellar artery supplies - inferior lateral pons - the middle cerebellar peduncle - a strip of the ventral (anterior) cerebellum The superior cerebellar artery supplies - upper lateral pons - superior cerebellar peduncle - most of the superior half of the cerebellar hemisphere The right posterior cerebral artery (PCA) - supplies: inferior and medial temporal lobes and medial occipital cortex - PCA infarcts typically cause a contralateral homonymous hemianopia This patient has an acute inferior myocardial infarction. Although it is possible that the left circumflex (LCx) arteries has coronary disease, the combination of a greater degree of ST segment elevation in lead III when compared to lead II, and the absence of ST elevation in the lateral leads (I and aVL) both make the right coronary artery (RCA) the culprit artery. The ST depressions in leads V1through V3indicate posterior ST elevation myocardial infarction from the occluded RCA. To further localize the lesion electrocardiographically, one should next obtain a right sided ECG, wherein the precordial leads are flipped to the right side of the chest, simply mirroring their usual left-sided position. The presence of >0.5mm of ST segment elevation in V4R would indicate a proximal right coronary artery occlusion, which carries a much higher mortality than a distal RCA occlusion. Page 8 of 12

questions.explanation: 70% of patients with ectopic ACTH, but <10% of patients with pituitary-dependent Cushing syndrome. ACTH levels will be high, because this is the underlying cause of both types of Cushing syndrome. Corticotropin-releasing hormone is rarely the cause of Cushing syndrome. Unfortunately, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the pituitary gland will not visualize lesions <2 mm; thus occasionally, sampling of the inferior petrosal veins is required, but this is not yet indicated in this patient at this point in the evaluation. Potting mix is a well-known carrier of Legionella longbeachae which can cause a severe pneumonia. The urinary Legionella antigen assay only detects Legionella pneumophila serogroup 1, hence is negative as expected. The other pathogens listed should grow on routine culture (although B. pseudomallei is more easily picked up on selective agar). Subacute cough, constitutional features, and lymph node enlargement and upper lobe consolidation on X-ray chest in a migrant from a country with a high prevalence of tuberculosis are strongly suggestive of active pulmonary and lymph node tuberculosis. This diagnosis may be confirmed or excluded with AFB microscopy and culture and M. tuberculosis PCR testing of lower respiratory tract specimens and lymph node tissue. Lymph node tissue histopathology demonstrating necrotising granulomatous inflammation, with or without visualisation of acid-fast bacilli, is suggestive of tuberculous lymphadenitis. HIV serological testing is indicated in all patients diagnosed with tuberculosis because coinfection is common and active tuberculosis is an AIDS- defining illness. Blood interferon-gamma release assays and the Mantoux/tuberculin skin test may be useful in the diagnosis of latent tuberculosis. However, these tests exhibit only moderate sensitivities and specificities in the diagnosis of active tuberculosis and are not useful in patients with clinical features consistent with active tuberculosis because they neither confirm or exclude the diagnosis. Total daily PO opiate requirement is 140mg. Conversion to parenteral requires 140/2.5 = 56mg parenteral daily. Half life is only 4 hours for parenteral morphine, hence divide by 6 to get 4 hourly dose. 56/6 = 9.66, rounded to 10mg every 4 hours while NBM. Hydromorphone only used if eGFR < 30 and avoided otherwise due to high risk of accidental overdose. The patient presents with secondary syphilis, which is characterised by clinical features of fevers, lymphadenopathy, and/or rash and a RPR titre 8. Although benzathine penicillin 2.4 million units/1.8 g IM single dose is the preferred antibiotic regimen for the treatment of early syphilis, the patient has posterior uveitis with chorioretinitis, which is a manifestation of neurosyphilis. Neurosyphilis may occur during any syphilis stage following Treponema pallidum infection, not just tertiary syphilis, and necessitates treatment with intravenous benzylpenicillin for 15 days. questions.explanation: disturbance ("tripping on rug"), executive dysfunction (issues with financial tasks), (brisk lower limb reflexes) and brain imaging findings (small vessel ischaemia) are supportive for an underlying aetiology of vascular dementia. All of these can be associated with the other 3 types of dementia in certain circumstances, but in combination, vascular dementia is most likely. You would expect other specific clues to point you to the other 3 types of dementia as well (e.g. memory, behaviour and language, visual hallucinations). This question is specifically asking about the safest medication choice for this lady who is having a flare of her ulcerative colitis (endoscopic picture shows patchy ulceration). Although steroids may be needed in the short term, they do have the possibility of significant side effects. Anti-TNF agents such as adalimumab are contraindicated in the setting of heart failure. Tofacitinib has been shown to be associated with an increased risk of infections such as herpes zoster and venous thromboembolism as well as heart disease. However, vedolizumab has been shown to be safe to use with no major increased risk of infections, heart disease or cancer. Sudden collapses caused by stroke, ventricular arrhythmia or massive pulmonary thromboembolism are usually associated with loss of consciousness. Whilst some elderly patients will complain of pre-syncopal symptoms associated with orthostatic hypotension, a greater majority will not be able to clearly describe these symptoms prior to a conscious collapse. Pleural effusions generally lead to very accurate V/Q matching. In the absence of other factors, such as underlying lung or pulmonary vascular disease, they do not cause hypoxia and drainage of an effusion does not improve oxygenation for most patients. The benefits in lung function from draining an effusion are modest with the FVC expected to improve by approximately 200mL for every 1L of fluid drained. Dyspnoea is the most likely to improve with 73-86% of patients reporting benefit. Firstly you need to confirm the hypertension by several readings. The definition of hypertension in pregnancy is 140 and/or 90 on repeated measures. This could be either chronic hypertension (known before pregnancy or noted early in pregnancy), gestational hypertension (new onset after 20 weeks), or indicate preeclampsia. One feature of concern to note in the stem is that the fundal height is low, suggesting possible fetal growth restriction. The SOMANZ guidelines for hypertension in pregnancy suggest that in new onset hypertension after 20 weeks, the following are checked: spot urine PCR, FBC, Creatinine, electrolytes, urate, LFTs, ultrasound assessment of fetal growth and dopplers. questions.explanation: this time. Lethal catatonia occurs in people with schizophrenia or during manic episodes. Neuroleptics might either improve or worsen the symptoms of lethal catatonia. Distinguishing lethal catatonia from neuroleptic malignant syndrome can be difficult, although a detailed history might reveal episodes of catatonia while a patient is not taking neuroleptics. Lethal catatonia also tends to have a prodrome of excitement and agitation prior to the onset of rigidity, while neuroleptic malignant syndrome tends to begin with rigidity. Patient has symptoms and signs consistent with cushings syndrome. This is substantiated by low SHBG, elevated ACTH with cortisol at upper end of normal. Source more likely pituitary than adrenal given elevated ACTH. Investigations for cushings syndrome include: 24 hr urinary cortisol, salivary cortisol and low dose overnight DST Differential is polycystic ovarian syndrome- fracture not consistent and testosterone levels normal. PCOS is a diagnosis of exclusion antihypertensives in treatment-resistant hypertension and is now recommended as 4th line treatment for resistant hypertension. optimal medical therapy initially. If symptoms have stabilised, there is no indication for invasive coronary angiography or revascularisation. A stress test is indicated if recurrent symptoms, to look for ischaemia. Source eTG: This patient likely has Bechet's disease. Most common in ethnic groups along the Silk Road and in ages 20-30 years. Oral and genital ulcers are very common as well as oligo-arthropathy most commonly involving the knees with aseptic inflammatory picture on aspiration. First line treatment is triamcinolone or low dose steroid. Infliximab is used in treatment refractory cases. The alveolar-arterial oxygen (A-a O2) difference can be helpful in distinguishing hypoventilation (elevated partial pressure of carbon dioxide [PCO2]) as a cause of hypoxemia. The A-a O2 difference on room air should be less than 15 mmHg in a young adult and typically increases slightly with age. The A-a O2 difference cannot easily be interpreted when the fraction of inspired oxygen (FiO2) is greater than 0.21. The A-a O2 difference on room air is elevated in situations of hypoxemia due to ventilation- perfusion (V/Q) mismatch, shunt, or diffusion defect. It will be normal when the hypoxemia is solely due to hypoventilation. In this case, the gradient is 7.5 mmHg, consistent with hypoventilation secondary to a presumed narcotic overdose. Asthma and pulmonary embolism typically cause hypoxemia due to V/Q mismatch. Pneumococcal and pneumocystis pneumonias cause hypoxemia for multifactorial reasons, including shunt. Page 11 of 12

The history and stool toxin PCR indicates a likely C diff infection. First line therapy is oral metronidazole. Second line is oral vancomycin. Oral ciprofloxacin can be considered for gastroenteritis caused by enteric pathogens Source: eTG. Teaching points/explanation: Intravenous methylprednisone pulse therapy over 3 consecutive days is the appropriate treatment for acute T cell-mediated rejection. Thymoglobulin (ATG) would be used in steroid-resistant cases, and/or if severe. Plasma exchange and IVIG are treatments that are given for treatment of antibody mediated rejection (ABMR), along with steroids (methylprednisolone). There is mixed evidence for whether Rituximab provides added benefit, it is therefore not `routinely' given for treatment of ABMR. Doing nothing just because the urine output is improving is not appropriate, as the kidney will still function to varying degrees even in the presence of acute rejection- left untreated, it will progress. Acute T-cell rejection is characterized by histological findings of interstitial inflammation and tubulitis. Histologic features which are in keeping with antibody- mediated rejection include glomerulitis and peri-tubular capillaritis. Intimal arteritis may be a feature of both acute T-cell rejection and/or antibody-mediated rejection. Benzodiazpeines are the best initial management for status epilepticus. You should use the benzodiazepine that you are most familiar with, however there is an advantage in using a longer acting agent such as clonazepam. Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy. Typically develop tonic-clonic or absence seizures towards end of first decade with development of myoclonic jerks in the next decade. Symptoms typically worsen with carbamazepine. Obinutuzumab is a recombinant humanised monoclonal antibody that binds to CD20 on B lymphocytes Idarucizumab is a humanised monoclonal antibody fragment (Fab) that binds with dabigatran and its metabolites to form a stable inactive complex, reversing their anticoagulant effect Page 12 of 12

questions.stem: Which statement concerning the atrioventricular bundle is correct?
questions.explanation: Statement (A) is false; the AV bundle comprises Purkinje fibres formed from modified cardiac muscle cells. Statement (B) is true; the atria and ventricles are electrically isolated from each other by the non-conducting connective tissue forming and supporting the atrioventricular valves except where the bundle pierces it.

options.text: It is formed of autonomic nerve processes and is the only electrical connection between the atria and the ventricles.
It is formed of Purkinje fibres and is the only electrical connection between the atria and the ventricles
It is formed of autonomic nerve processes and forms one of several electrical connections between the atria and ventricles.
It is formed of Purkinje fibres and forms one of several electrical connections between the atria and ventricles.

options.text: Postsynaptic Gs protein coupled activation of adenyl cyclase leading to an increase in cAMP leading to an increase in heart rate
Presynaptic Gs protein coupled activation of adenyl cyclase leading to an increase in cAMP leading to the relaxation of smooth muscle in the bronchioles
Presynaptic Gi protein coupled inhibition of adenyl cyclase leading to a reduction in cAMP leading to the relaxation of smooth muscle in the bronchioles

questions.explanation: A. Incorrect. W = closure of mitral valve, X = opening of aortic valve, Y = closure of aortic valve, Z = opening of mitral valve. See PV loop below for LV filling and contraction, isovolumic contraction and relaxation, end-systolic and end-diastolic volume. B. Incorrect. LV systolic failure causes a rightward shift of the PV loop (see image below). In heart failure, there is an increase in end-diastolic and end-systolic volume. Due to reduced inotropy, stroke volume (ie. LV end-diastolic minus LV end-systolic volume) is reduced, so the curve is also narrower. C. Correct. Mitral stenosis reduces LV systolic filling, therefore reduces end-diastolic volume. It causes a milder reduction in end-systolic volume (due to reduced afterload), so overall there is also a reduction in stroke volume. D. Incorrect. Aortic stenosis causes a taller, narrower LV loop, with a rightward shift of end-systolic volume and a lesser rightward shift in LV end-diastolic volume. This is since LV emptying is reduced due to a smaller aortic valve orifice; therefore a large pressure gradient develops within the LV cavity (taller PV loop). LV wall stress is increased, stroke volume is reduced (narrower PV loop) and LV end-systolic volume is increased (rightward shift of ESV).

options.text: Points Y and Z correspond to opening and closure of the aortic valve
Left ventricular systolic failure causes a leftward shift of the PV loop
Mitral stenosis causes a leftward shift of points W and X
Aortic stenosis causes a leftward shift of the PV loop

questions.explanation: Explanation: SCFAs, such as butyrate, propionate, and acetate, are byproducts of microbial fermentation in the gut. These compounds play a crucial role in maintaining gut health by providing energy to colonocytes and exerting anti-inflammatory effects. SCFAs have been associated with the regulation of immune responses and the maintenance of intestinal barrier function. Option a) is incorrect as the majority of gut bacteria are beneficial and contribute to various physiological processes, including digestion and immune regulation. Option b) is incorrect because antibiotic use can significantly impact the composition and diversity of the gut microbiome, potentially leading to dysbiosis. Option d) is incorrect because the gut microbiome undergoes dynamic changes throughout an individual's life, influenced by factors such as diet, age, and environmental exposures.

options.text: The majority of gut bacteria are harmful and contribute to disease
Antibiotic use has minimal impact on the composition of the gut microbiome
Short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) produced by gut bacteria have anti-inflammatory effects.
The gut microbiome remains stable throughout an individual's lifetime.

A 40-year-old man presents with a colorectal adenocarcinoma in the caecum. There is no
family history. Immunohistochemistry (IHC) showed loss of MLH1 and PMS2 staining. Which
of the following statements is MOST correct?
questions.explanation: A family history is irrelevant as he may be adopted, born via sperm donor or there could be mistaken paternity. While high penetrance, it is not near 100% and not all individuals with Lynch Syndrome will go on to develop a cancer. The loss of MLH1 and PMS2 staining indicated the tumour has no working MLH1 protein. If MLH1 protein is absent PMS2 is lost as the PMS2 protein binds to MLH1 and if it is absent PMS2 is just lost. The result indicates that germline testing is indicated. The most likely cause of this is biallelic somatic mutations, especially in older individuals but germline pathogenic variants need to be excluded.

options.text: As he has no family history, he is very unlikely to have Lynch Syndrome
The IHC means he has Lynch Syndrome
IHC means that the tumour is likely to be microsatellite unstable and this may impact his treatment
This indicates there is one somatic mutation in MLH1 and PMS2 gene

questions.stem:
A woman has breast cancer at 27 years of age. BRCA1 and BRCA2 testing was done (in
2004) and no mutation was identified. Which of the following statement is MOST correct?
questions.explanation: Earlier testing would have been performed using sanger sequencing looking at only those two genes. We now have newer, whole exome sequencing and a wider panel of genes known to increase risk of developing breast cancer than we did in 2004. As cost and ease of testing has improved, we would now test for both high and moderate risk genes that are currently known. ATM and CHEK are moderate risk associated with generally older onset breast cancer and are unlikely to have caused breast cancer so young. In the absence of family history or identification of a pathogenic variant in a breast-ovarian cancer risk gene after updated testing, she would not be at significantly increased risk of developing ovarian cancer.

options.text: This excludes a germline genetic cause
CHEK2 or ATM are the likely causes
Repeat genetic testing using a wider panel of genes, including TP53, is warranted
She is at significantly increased risk of ovarian cancer

questions.explanation: ays), leptospirosis manifests as a biphasic illness with an acute/bacteraemic phase (seven to 10 days from symptom onset), characterised by sudden onset of fever, myalgia and headache. Calf tenderness and conjunctival suffusion are characteristic but not always present. Other non-specific symptoms include anorexia, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, dizziness, lethargy, arthralgia, eye pain, photophobia and rashes. This is followed by a late/immune phase (>7 days from symptom onset), when immunologically mediated organ damage occurs, and severe complications include acute renal failure, pulmonary haemorrhage, myocarditis, arrhythmias, shock, liver failure, coagulopathy and neurological complications.2 Weil's disease is the classic triad of jaundice, renal failure and haemorrhage, but these manifestations do not always occur together. Aseptic meningitis, encephalitis, convulsions, Guillain\xADBarr\xE9 syndrome, transverse myelitis and other neurological syndromes have also been reported. Leptospirosis can also present with acute or chronic optic manifestations, including subconjunctival and retinal haemorrhages, optic neuritis and chronic uveitis. In Australia, occupational exposure is the predominant source of infection, particularly in livestock and dairy farmers, abattoir and meat workers, and banana workers (through exposure to rodents). Infection through recreational exposure is also common, mostly from white water rafting, kayaking, ecotourism and other outdoor activities. International travel, particularly to tropical, developing countries, is an important source of occupational and recreational exposure. In tropical, developing countries, including Australia's neighbours in the Asia Pacific region, environmental and climatic drivers are important. RNSH 2023 ID CLIN APP

questions.explanation: Haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH) is a life-threatening syndrome of abnormal immune activation leading to excessive inflammation and tissue destruction, which can be associated with specific genetic disorders, and be triggered by infection or malignancy. In HLH, NK cells and cytotoxic lymphocytes fail to eliminate activate macrophages, and assays to assess NK function (NK cytotoxicity assays, or flow cytometry for NK cell perforin and CD107 alpha/LAMP-1 expression) are low or absent. Cytopenias (anaemia and thrombocytopenia) are common, and inflammatory markers, including ferritin, are elevated. Cytokines such as serum CXCL9 and soluble CD25 (IL-2 receptor alpha) are elevated in HLH, with elevated CXCL9 more common in malignancy-related HLH (Debaugnies et al. J Clin Immuno 2021; 41:658-665). Hence a normal soluble CD25 is the least supportive of a HLH diagnosis.

questions.explanation: "Double hit" and "triple hit" lymphomas are variants of diffuse large B cell lymphomas (DLBCL) that are more aggressive and portend an inferior prognosis to standard DLBCL. Lymphomas that express MYC AND either BCL2 or BCL6 as assessed by IHC, are termed "double expressor" lymphoma. Lymphomas that are found to have EITHER MYC/BCL2 or MYC/BCL6 fusions on FISH are termed double hit. Lymphomas that have BOTH MYC/BCL2 AND MYC/BCL6 fusions on FISH are termed triple hit. Triple hit patients perform worse than double hit patients who perform worse than double expressor patients.

In the context of immunology and internal medicine, which of the following cytokines plays a
crucial role in the pathogenesis of rheumatoid arthritis?
questions.explanation: Explanation: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by inflammation of the joints. TNF- is a pro-inflammatory cytokine that plays a central role in the pathogenesis of RA. It contributes to the recruitment of inflammatory cells, synovial inflammation, and joint destruction in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Targeting TNF- with biologic therapies has become a mainstay in the management of RA. Option a) Interleukin-4 (IL-4) is involved in immune responses but is not a key player in the pathogenesis of rheumatoid arthritis. Option c) Interleukin-10 (IL-10) is an anti-inflammatory cytokine and does not have a prominent role in the pathogenesis of RA. Option d) Interferon-gamma (IFN-) is associated with Th1 responses and is involved in various immune processes, but it is not a central player in the pathogenesis of rheumatoid arthritis.

What is the most common extra-articular manifestation of rheumatoid arthritis?
questions.explanation: Historically, subcutaneous nodules are found in up to 25-30% of RA patients (particularly seropositive). However, given advances in RA treatment with biologics this number has markedly reduced. The most recent large study, the ExtRA cohort from Brazil involved just over 1100 patients. It confirmed this original observation, with SC nodules being the most common (8.3%) with others including sicca syndrome (5.8%), ILD (3.8%) and episcleritis/scleritis (1.1%) (see below). An excellent review on extra-articular manifestations of RA is Confort et al. (2021) in Autoimmunity Reviews.

transcription of IL-2?
questions.explanation: Tacrolimus binds to FK-binding protein to form a complex which inhibits calcineurin. This in turn inhibits NFAT and subsequent IL-2 transcription. Cyclosporine binds to cyclophilin to form a complex which inhibits calcineurin. ATG is a polyclonal lymphocyte depleting agent which binds to CD3 receptors on T-cells. Azathioprine/mycophenolate inhibit de novo purine synthesis and therefore reduce cell cycle substrates which are necessary for T- and B-cell proliferation

questions.stem:
SGLT-2 inhibitor has revolutionised the treatment of patients with diabetic kidney disease.
Which kidney structure is primarily affected by its mechanism?
questions.explanation: By inhibiting sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) in the proximal renal tubules, dapagliflozin or empaglifozin reduces reabsorption of filtered glucose from the tubular lumen and lowers the renal threshold for glucose (RTG). SGLT2 is the main site of filtered glucose reabsorption; reduction of filtered glucose reabsorption and lowering of RTG result in increased urinary excretion of glucose, thereby reducing plasma glucose concentrations.

They are also found in type 1 diabetes, and there is an association between stiff-person syndrome and type 1 diabetes. Anti-Yo antibodies are the most highly specific and frequent antibody in paraneoplastic cerebellar degeneration (PCD). N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR) encephalitis is an autoimmune encephalitis mediated by antibodies against the NR1 subunit of the NMDA receptor, sometimes triggered by associated ovarian teratomas. Caspr2 antibodies are associated with autoimmune encephalitis and autoimmune epilepsy.

Acquired methaemoglobinaemia is LEAST associated with exposure to which of the
following medications?
questions.explanation: Acquired methaemoglobinaemia commonly results from exposure to various exogenous substances and medications. These include topical anaesthetic agents/sprays/creams, dapsone, anti-malarial agents and food or drugs containing nitrates/nitrites. Rasburicase can also precipitate methaemoglobin formation, particularly in patients with glucose-6- phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency. Methylene blue is the treatment for acquired methemoglobinaemia, although in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency, there is a paradoxical risk of precipitating haemolysis, and instead high dose ascorbic acid should be considered.

excreted unchanged in the urine of 0.5. For a 60kg woman, what IV loading dose is required
to achieve an initial plasma concentration of 5 mg/L?
questions.explanation: Loading dose = volume of distribution x required plasma concentration \xAD (0.2 x 60 kg) x 5 = 60 mg

questions.stem:
In a healthy adult, which of the following changes will be seen when approaching maximal
exercise intensity?
questions.explanation: This question uses exercise as an analogy for increased physiological stress such as that seen in acute illness, sepsis, etc. Option A is incorrect. With increasing exercise comes an increasing respiratory rate and clearance of CO2 beyond that which is produced by the exercising muscles. Arterial PCO2 drops due to the high alveolar ventilation. Option B is incorrect. An increase in end-expiratory lung volume signifies a patient is having dynamic hyperinflation, i.e. they are retaining more and more gas within their lungs with every breath \xAD such as what is seen in a patient with severe obstructive airways disease who is tachypnoeic. Option C is incorrect. Similar to option A, with high alveolar ventilation the PCO2 in expired gas decreases as the adult has such high alveolar ventilation. Think of it as the adult diluting the CO2 that is crossing into their alveoli as they have such high ventilation. Option D is correct. With increasing heart rate, regardless of cause, the only phase of the cardiac cycle that can shorten (in any meaningful way) is diastole, i.e. systole as well as the interventricular contraction and relaxation times are effectively fixed. This is important as patients with impaired relaxation (i.e. diastolic dysfunction) will have progressively precipitating pulmonary oedema.

options.text: Rise in end expiratory lung volume

questions.explanation: Beta-lactam resistance in MRSA occurs due to production of a distinct penicillin binding protein \xAD PBP2a \xAD which is encoded for by the gene mecA. Enzymatic hydrolysis of beta-lactams is the mechanism through which the majority of Staph. aureus isolates become resistant to pen/amp/amoxicillin (that is to say, Staph. aureus generally produces a very narrow-spectrum penicillinase). This led to the discovery and use of penicillinase-resistant beta-lactams (e.g. methicillin/oxacillin and now flu/dicloxacillin) which are now first-line therapy for methicillin-sensitive Staph. aureus infections. Beta-lactamases are also a major resistance mechanism in Gram negative bacteria. Efflux pump upregulation and decreased cell wall permeability are more commonly associated with resistance to non-beta-lactam antibiotics e.g. macrolides and lincosamides.

options.text: Enzymatic hydrolysis of beta-lactams via beta-lactamases

A previously healthy 25-year-old male presents with fatigue, chest pain, and shortness of
breath. He recently had an illness characterized by fever, sore throat, and muscle aches.
Laboratory tests show elevated cardiac enzymes and an abnormal electrocardiogram (ECG)
suggestive of myocarditis. Which viral infection is most commonly associated with this
clinical presentation?
questions.explanation: Myocarditis is an inflammatory condition of the heart muscle and can be triggered by various viral infections. Among the options provided, Coxsackievirus, particularly Coxsackievirus B, is one of the most commonly associated viruses with myocarditis in young adults. Coxsackievirus belongs to the enterovirus group and can cause a wide range of symptoms, including fever, sore throat, and muscle aches, which align with the patient's recent illness. While other viruses, such as Cytomegalovirus (CMV), Varicella-zoster virus (VZV), and Adenovirus, can also lead to myocarditis, Coxsackievirus is often considered a more common cause in this age group. However, it's important to note that myocarditis can have various viral and non-viral causes, and a comprehensive evaluation is necessary to determine the specific underlying cause in individual cases.

With respect to the use of SGLT-2 inhibitor drugs in the treatment of heart failure, which of
the following is true in terms of the existing randomised data?
questions.explanation: EMPORER-PRESERVED and DELIVER were two randomised studies addressing the use of SGLT-2 inhibitors in HFPEF. The results were consistent across both studies with both meeting the primary end point for reduction in the primary end point (composite of cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalisation/heart failure event). In both studies, the benefit was significant only for reduced heart failure hospitalisation. In HFREF studies, there was a marginal benefit in term of CV mortality, with the greatest magnitude of benefit seen in reducing hospitalisation. The DELIVER studies reported hypoglycaemic outcomes, with no difference seen between active and placebo diabetes mellitus status.

questions.explanation: The Dutch Lipid Clinic Network diagnostic criteria is one criteria system used to give possible, probable and definitive familial hypercholesterolaemia diagnoses. Fulfilling each criterion gives points which contribute to the likelihood of having the disorder. The highest individual criteria are for: LDL-C greater than or equal to 8.5 mmol/L (8 points), and functional mutation in LDLR, apoB, or PCSK9 genes (8 points). Followed by this is tendinous xanthomata (6 points), LDL-C from 6.5 to 8.4 mmol/L (5 points), arcus cornealis before age 45 (4 points), patients with premature coronary artery disease (men under 55 years, women under 60 years) (2 points). Thus the presence of tendinous xanthomata most strongly increases the likelihood of a FH diagnosis.

Patients with premature coronary artery disease (men <55 years, women aged <60 years)

questions.explanation: The clinical vignette is most compatible with a diagnosis of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, with a young patient presenting with a syncopal episode after physical exertion. The ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy with a lateral strain pattern (although the other typical finding of deep and narrow Q waves in the lateral and inferior leads are absent on this ECG). There are no ST abnormalities suggestive of Brugada syndrome. The typical precordial auscultation finding of HOCM is a systolic ejection murmur heard best at the lower left sternal edge. Dynamic manoeuvres which increase preload (handgrip, squatting) reduce the volume of the murmur, and decreasing preload with Valsalva manoeuvre increases the volume.

replacement, and she has developed a papulovesicular eruption, particularly affecting the

questions.explanation: This patient has miliaria rubra (heat rash) which can occur in hospitalised patients, where a common precipitant for this itchy rash is lying for prolonged periods in a waterproof mattress. The primary treatment is modifying the environment (fans, ice packs, cotton blankets to separate patient from waterproof plastic mattress). This diagnosis is supported by the history and biopsy. Other differentials (eg SJS/TEN) would present differently (tender/painful rather than itchy) and has specific findings on histology (subepidermal blistering and necrosis). Distribution (and histology) not consistent with shingles, we would expect that to be a painful rash. Antihistamines may be of some benefit for itch in skin rashes but will not address the underlying cause

Complete central DI is typically associated with an undetectable copeptin level. Partial central DI is typically associated with a low copeptin level. questions.explanation: Dietary therapy has been shown to be effective for EO, however required major lifestyle change. Elimination diets may be difficult to follow, and there is a risk that the diet may not be nutritionally adequate or be associated with unhealthy eating habits. Therefore, elimination diets require education and supervision by an accredited practising dietitian; 2-food (milk and wheat), 4-food (wheat, egg, dairy, soy), or 6-food (wheat, egg, dairy, diet, foods should be gradually re-introduced to identify trigger foods (on a symptomatic or endoscopic basis, depending on the management philosophy); dairy and wheat are the most common triggers. Limiting specific foods may improve symptoms, but this should only be continued if effective; advise patients to avoid food restrictions that do not improve symptoms. Drug therapy for eosinophilic oesophagitis may include proton pump inhibitors (PPI) with or without topical corticosteroids. Eosinophilic oesophagitis may be seasonal, so symptomatic treatment may only be required at certain times of year (usually during spring or summer). Gastro-oesophageal reflux can be a contributing factor in the pathogenesis of eosinophilic oesophagitis, and PPIs may have additional anti- inflammatory effects. First-line drug therapy is a PPI at the standard dose The use of antifungals has no role in EO.
questions.stem: Options for management of eosinophilic oesophagitis include all the following EXCEPT:

A 50-year-old woman recently diagnosed with Crohn's disease is seen in clinic. Her medical
background includes hypertension, dyslipidaemia and gout. You discuss commencement of
Azathioprine with your patient for the management of her Crohn's disease. Which of the
following medications may need to be ceased prior to starting Azathioprine?
questions.explanation: This question requires knowledge of azathioprine metabolism. In short, azathioprine is a pro-drug which is metabolised to 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP). 6-MP is then metabolised by thiopurine methyltransferase (TPMT) into 6-methyl-mercaptopurine (6-MMP) AND by another enzymatic pathway eventually into 6-thioguanine metabolites (6-TGN). 6-MMP is hepatotoxic in high concentrations, whilst 6-TGN (the therapeutic metabolite) can cause bone marrow suppression in high concentrations. Note that there is a third enzymatic pathway for 6-MP metabolism into an inactive metabolite as well (6-thiouracil) by xanthine oxidase. Allopurinol is an inhibitor of TPMT and xanthine oxidase and when co- administered with azathioprine, can lead to shunting down the 6-TGN pathway which may lead to toxic levels of 6-TGN. There are no reported significant interactions between azathioprine and the other

questions.stem:
A 40-year-old male patient is admitted with recurrent pancreatitis. A CT scan reveals no
pancreatic mass, but evidence of widespread lymphadenopathy. Dedicated MRCP reveals a
stricture in the common bile duct but no stones. He also has a history of swollen cheeks.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: IgG4 disease is associated with autoimmune pancreatitis, and asymptomatic lymphadenopathy occurs in 80% of patients. Lymphoma would not typically be expected to cause pancreatitis.

questions.stem:
Which of the following baseline investigations does NOT need to be routinely performed in
the initial evaluation of class III obesity in a 51-year-old female.
questions.explanation: Although obesity can be linked with Cushing disease, this is not routinely screened for unless other clinical symptoms are present. Morning cortisol is often unaffected in Cushing disease and is not recommended. It is used to screen for adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) which is not linked with obesity.

options.text: Flushing in the skin above the spinal cord injury level

questions.explanation: Hypotension is not the main symptom seen in autonomic dysreflexia. Orthostatic hypotension is a common symptom seen in patients who suffer from autonomic dysreflexia. It may also be a complication of sepsis. A rise in systolic blood pressure 20-40mm Hg above the often-hypotensive baseline may be seen, associated with reflex bradycardia. Tachycardia can occasionally be seen. Hypotension is often seen following pharmacotherapy for hypertension associated with autonomic dysreflexia and following the resolution of the precipitating insult.
questions.stem:
A 36-year-old male with a previous C5 spinal cord injury presents with a blocked indwelling
catheter. Which of the following is NOT commonly seen with autonomic dysreflexia?

questions.explanation: Metformin is implicated in reduced absorption of B12 in the terminal by interfering with calcium binding complex of B12-intrinsic factor to its receptor. Phenytoin has been associated with both folate and B12 deficiency. Nitric oxide inactivates B12 via cobalt oxidation leading to functional B12 deficiency. Pantoprazole reduces acidity within stomach resulting in reduction of B12 release from proteins. Levetiracetam has not been found to be associated with B12 deficiency.
questions.stem: Which of the following medications is NOT associated with B12 deficiency?

options.text: Depressive disorders are significantly less common in medically ill patients compared to the general population

questions.explanation: Depressive disorders are significantly more common in the medically ill compared to the general population (Hence A is false). Depression is associated with worsening of physical symptoms, greater healthcare utilization and poorer treatment adherence. Differential diagnoses include delirium, personality disorders, adjustment disorders and depressive disorders secondary to substances, medications, or another medical condition (Hence B is false). Once a mild to moderate major depressive episode is identified, a stepped care approach should be implemented, starting with general psychoeducation, psychosocial interventions, and ongoing monitoring. For moderate to severe symptoms, or mild symptoms that are not responding to low-intensity interventions, the use of antidepressants or higher intensity psychotherapeutic interventions should be considered. (Hence C is false as there is no clarifier as to the severity of the depression in the stem or response). Psychotherapeutic interventions have demonstrated benefits with small to moderate effect sizes. Antidepressant medications have also demonstrated benefits with moderate effect sizes (Hence D is true).
questions.stem:
A 35-year-old female patient is attending general endocrinology outpatients has a notable
low mood for the last month. Which of the following is MOST CORRECT when considering a
depressive disorder in this lady?

options.text: Most people with OCD respond best to a combination of psychosocial and pharmacological treatment.
Compulsions are repetitive behaviours or mental acts that the person feels must be performed in response to an obsession to prevent discomfort
The person with OCD generally recognises that their behaviour is excessive
Doses of psychoactive medications used to treat OCD are generally the same as those used for treatment of other indications such as mood disorder

questions.explanation: D is false. Doses of SSRIs and SNRIs required for treatment of OCD are generally higher than doses used in other indications. (source: eTG)
questions.stem:
Which of the following statements is FALSE with regard to the diagnosis and management
of obsessive compulsive disorder in adults?

A 36-year-old woman has recurrent presentations with long-standing abdominal pain. She is
extremely worried about cancer. She has had numerous investigations including CT scans,
colonoscopies, and serological markers which have not revealed an organic cause for her
symptoms. Despite this, she is convinced that she has an intra-abdominal malignancy and
refuses to believe the negative investigations. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Somatisation disorder = multiple physical symptoms and the patient refuses to accept reassurance or negative test results Hypochondrial disorder = persistent belief in the presence of an underlying serious

questions.stem:
A 42-year-old male assessed for delirium. On assessment his heart rate was 110, was
normotensive tachypneic to 32 and there was a fever 38.2 degrees. There was a slight
increased tone in the upper limbs and there was no focal sign of pain or infection isolated.
On investigations there was a leucocytosis of 13.2x109/L with a left shift present, mild LFT
derangement and a raised CK to 312. Which medication is most likely to have caused this
presentation?
questions.explanation: This is the common presentation for neuroleptic malignant syndrome which is most commonly caused from first generation antipsychotics. Rocuronium is a cause for malignant hyperthermia which is a different presentation.

This question is about Huntington disease, autosomal dominant inheritance and the phenomenon of anticipation leading to disease. earlier onset of disease) is more likely to occur on paternal transmission of the affected HD allele, than on maternal transmission of the affected allele passing on the affected HD allele is the same for all children, regardless of sex (the chance of passing on the affected allele is 50% for each child) repeats of <26 are in the normal range.

options.text: His son has a greater chance of inheriting the affected HD allele than his daughter
His daughter has a greater chance of inheriting the affected HD allele than his son
This man is likely to have <26 CAG trinucleotide repeats in the HHT gene
If both his children inherit the affected HD allele, his son's affected children are likely to show symptoms earlier than his daughter's affected children

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old man in a nursing home has had depression for many years since the passing
of his wife. He suffers general cognitive and physical decline, trouble swallowing and
recently developed choreiform movements. His father died of a heart attack at 70 years of
age but had started to develop similar motor symptoms and signs. You suspect Huntington
disease. This man has a son and a daughter. If this is Huntington disease, which of the
following is true:

questions.explanation: Fragile X-associated Tremor-Ataxia syndrome (FXTAS) is a late-onset X-linked neurological disorder, with prominent features of tremor, parkinsonism, ataxia and progressive cognitive impairment. The white matter changes in the middle cerebellar peduncles are a major radiological diagnostic criterion for FXTAS. The gene linked to Fragile X syndrome and FXTAS is the FMR1 gene which is X-linked. CGG triplet nucleotide expansions in the FMR1 gene of 55-200 repeats are known as "pre-mutations". Males with FMR1 pre-mutations have a significant risk of developing FXTAS in adult life, symptom onset usually after age 50 years. Women with FMR1 pre- mutations have a smaller increased risk of FXTAS in later life, and female pre-mutation carriers also have a 20% risk of developing premature ovarian failure. Female pre- mutation carriers are at risk of having a child with Fragile X syndrome (full mutation = CGG repeat size > 200 repeats). This is because the triplet repeat can expand dramatically in size from a pre-mutation to a full mutation in one generation. If the child inherits a full mutation and the child is male, the child will have Fragile X syndrome which is an intellectual disability syndrome. Patients with Fragile X syndrome don't develop FXTAS. The occurrence of intellectual disability in a male, premature menopause and a neurodegenerative disorder in the same family, strongly suggests the diagnosis of FXTAS in the proband. FXTAS is commonly misdiagnosed as Parkinson's Disease. Although he displays signs of parkinsonism, the specific family history points to FXTAS as the most likely diagnosis. Wilson's disease is autosomal recessive, and therefore does not fit the family history given. The age of onset tends to be younger than age 40 years. It is not associated with premature ovarian failure or congenital intellectual disability. There are many types of spinocerebellar ataxia (SCA), each with different signs and symptoms, including nystagmus, visual loss, dementia and extrapyramidal symptoms, in addition to ataxia. It is not typically associated with premature ovarian failure or a family history of moderate intellectual disability. In summary, although all the above differentials are potential candidate diagnoses for his presentation, FXTAS is the only condition that ties together all the features described in the patient's family history and the MRI findings.
questions.stem:
A 60-year-old man is seen in outpatient clinic for progressively worsening ataxia over 18
months. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. He reports no visual disturbances, headaches
or unexplained weight loss. His speech is normal. His wife did not report concerns with his
cognition. His mother had late-onset tremor and problems with her balance which led to a
diagnosis of Parkinson's Disease at age 75. His sister had early menopause at age 38 and
she has a son with a moderate intellectual disability. There is no family history of dementia.

On examination, he has limb and gait ataxia, as well as a mild kinetic tremor of the upper
limbs. The remainder of his neurological examination is normal. His MRI brain scan (shown
below) is significant for white matter lesions in the middle cerebellar peduncles.
 What is the most likely diagnosis?

questions.explanation: Rituximab is a monoclonal antibody against CD20, and antigen expressed on the majority of normal and malignant B cells. Myeloma is a cancer of plasma cells which typically don't express CD20, thus Rituximab is not routine used to treatment myeloma. Bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor), Lenalidomide (immunomodulatory imide drug) and therapy in myeloma, either in combination with each other (VRd) or in other combinations (Vd, VCD, Rd).
questions.stem:
Which is of the following agents is NOT routinely used in the treatment of multiple
myeloma?

An 87-year-old male with myelodysplastic syndrome is having a blood transfusion in the
ambulatory care centre. You are called to review him for a fever whilst receiving the
transfusion. His temperature is 38.1C, heart rate 80bpm, blood pressure 130/80mmHg
without an interval change from earlier the same day. He is tachypnoeic and has no oxygen
requirement.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Febrile non-haemolytic transfusion reactions occur during or within 24 hours of a transfusion. They present with a mild fever +/- with chills. Management is to stop the transfusion and treat the fever with an antipyretic. Consider and exclude other causes, especially acute haemolytic reactions, transfusion associated sepsis and transfusion associated acute lung injury. Acute haemolytic reactions still warrant exclusion, and bloods should be sent for DAT, FBC and repeat ABO grouping if there are any concerns regarding the severity of reaction or correct blood for the correct patient.

As the mixing study corrects the abnormality, the diagnosis must be a factor deficiency. Thus, it cannot be the presence of a lupus anticoagulant. Factor 7 affects the extrinsic pathway leading to a prolonged PT. Fibrinogen is involved in the common pathway and thus would result in both a prolonged PT and APTT. Thus, determining the Factor 8 level
questions.stem:
A 40-year-old male has a repeatedly prolonged APTT of 60 seconds. See below for
laboratory results:
PT 12 secs (normal 9-13)
APTT 60 secs (normal 25-37)
APTT Mixing study 35 secs
What is the next best investigation to determine the likely diagnosis?

questions.explanation: JAK2 mutation testing which will be positive in nearly all patients with polycythemia rubra vera.

A 70-year-old man presents with amaurosis fugax. On abdominal examination, the spleen is
palpable 5 cm below the costal margin. Blood tests are shown below:
Hb 210 g/L (Normal Range 120-150)
MCV 90 fL (Normal Range 80-100)
WCC 15.0 x 109/L (Normal Range 4.0-10.0)
Plt 350 x 109/L (Normal Range 150-400)
 Which is the most useful diagnostic test?

questions.explanation: An allergy action plan is recommended for all patients with allergic reaction, particularly in this case when re-exposure to the allergen is likely. This is a priority and should be completed before discharge. As this was not an anaphylactic reaction, Epipen is not required. Desensitisation would be impossible before discharge.

options.text: Allergy action plan from the ASCIA website

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old hobby-beekeeper presented to ED with a large localised reaction to a bee
sting. The swelling responded to regular antihistamine. What should be completed before
his discharge?

A 23-year-old medical student presents with a 12-week history of daily urticaria. The rash
usually resolves within 24 hours with no residual pigmentation or scarring. It is migratory and
can occur at any time of the day. She occasionally experiences lip swelling. She is otherwise
systemically well with no significant family history.
Which one of the following is FALSE?

options.text: Second generation antihistamines are a first-line treatment.
Complement studies are likely to be normal.
Specific IgE and skin prick testing to environmental allergies is recommended.
Anti-IgE therapy is an appropriate second line treatment.

questions.explanation: This patient has chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU) with angioedema. First line treatment is second generation antihistamines, with omalizumab in refractory cases. Usually blood investigations are unremarkable and there is no role for specific IgE or skin testing.

questions.stem:
An 89-year-old male presents with a 3-month history of progressive diffuse painful vesicular
rash over the axilla, oral mucosa, genital and eyes. He describes the vesicles readily
breaking and leaking serous fluid. His past medical history is only significant for hypertension
and reflux. He describes losing ~20 kg over the last 6 months, which he has been attributing
to his poor appetite. He undergoes a biopsy of the underlying skin lesions, in which
 immunofluorescence of the skin lesion reveal IgG antibodies and complement on the cell
surfaces of keratinocytes.

What would be the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Note the history of weight loss and friable blistering lesions, thus would limit to the conditions to linear IgA or Pemphigus vulgaris, from which the histopathology provides the diagnosis as Pemphigus involves coating of the keratinocytes and the immunoglobulins target desmoglein-3

This question describes a patient with calcium pyrophosphate (CPP) arthropathy. This condition, particularly when hook-like osteophytes are present should prompt assessment arthritis (RF and CCP positive disease) would not typically be associated with calcification in the TFC or hook like osteophytes. Anti-nuclear antibody associated arthropathy (e.g., SLE, CTD) is non erosive and x-rays are typically normal for age.
questions.stem:
A 55-year-old Caucasian male office-worker is referred to rheumatology clinic with a 5-year
history of stiffness, swelling and progressive ulnar deviation at his MCP joints and wrists. X-
rays show joint space narrowing through the MCP joints with hook-like osteophytes and
advanced degenerative change in the wrists and calcification in the triangular fibrocartilage.
His C-reactive protein is 8 mg/L (normal range < 5 mg/L). Which test would you expect to be
abnormal?

questions.explanation: Tofacitinib is a JAK inhibitor which has been shown to have efficacy in psoriatic and rheumatoid arthritis. A 2022 study (N Engl J Med 2022; 386:316-326) showed higher rates of major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) and malignancy in Tofacitinib treated patient compared with those taking TNF inhibitors. Tofacitinib should thus be avoided in patients with risk factors for cardiovascular disease and malignancy.
questions.stem:
You are seeing a 73-year-old diabetic woman who has a longstanding history of psoriatic
arthritis. 12 months ago, she had a coronary artery stent placed for stable angina. Her
comorbidities include hypertension and hypercholesterolaemia which are well controlled on
Rampiril and Rosuvastatin. She has been taking methotrexate and sulfasalazine for arthritis
but recently her joints have worsened, and she now has active knees, hips and shoulder with
a C reactive protein of 20 mg/L (normal < 5mg/L). Which of the following medications is NOT
suitable for this patient.

questions.explanation: Anti-P155 is the antibody that has the highest prevalence in dermatomyositis it also has an association with cancer of the stomach breast and gall bladder. Anti-SRP is associated with polymyositis, Anti-Jo1 is associated with inflammatory myopathy and Anti-Smith is associated with SLE
questions.stem:
A 75-year-old female presented with myalgia and symmetric proximal muscle weakness. On
further examination Gottron papules and heliotrope rash were present. Which antibody is
most likely to be linked to this presentation.

questions.explanation: Type 2 RTA results in impaired HC03 reabsorption in the proximal tubule. Causes included hereditary, Fanconi syndrome, vitamin D deficiency, cystinosis, lead nephropathy, amyloidosis and medullary cystic disease.
questions.stem: Fanconi syndrome best presents which type of renal tubular acidosis (RTA)?

questions.explanation: No benefit from Hydrochlorothiazide. No indication to add second antiplatelet in the absence of symptoms or untreated coronary lesion. While is benefit from GLP-1 in terms of weight loss and cardiovascular outcomes in those with t2dm, in this case the patient has evidence of proteinuric kidney disease which takes priority. After addition of SGLT-2 could consider adding this next line (or fineranone if proteinuria persists). In addition glycaemic control is adequate therefore metformin not indicated. DAPA-CKD
questions.stem:
A 65 year old male is being reviewed in your clinic. He has a history of hypertension, type 2
diabetes, previous myocardial infarction, hypercholesterolaemia and osteoarthritis of the left
knee. He is a non-smoker and drinks approximately 4-6 standard units of alcohol per week.
He works as a bus driver. On examination his weight is 104kg with a height of 180cm. Blood
pressure is 138/89mmHg. Chest examination is clear. Heart sounds are dual with no
murmur. He has some peripheral oedema limited to the ankle. Recent pathology
investigations demonstrate a creatinine of 120\xB5mol/L, egfr 54mL/min/1.73m2. Hba1c is
6.8%. Total cholesterol is 4.0mmol/L with LDL of 1.5mmol/L and Trigylcerides of
1.8mmol/L. Urine albumin:creatinine ratio is 45mg/mmol. He currently takes Candesartan
32mg daily, Amlodipine 10mg daily, Metoprolol 25mg BD, Aspirin 100mg, Rosuvastatin
40mg daily, Metformin 1000mg daily, Linagliptin 5mg daily, and Panadol osteo. What is the
next best step in his management to reduce his cardiovascular risk?
What is the most important marker of mortality in the dialysis population?

options.text: Timed up and go test >14 seconds

questions.explanation: Albumin is the most important marker of mortality in the dialysis population, malnutrition increases mortality and albumin is also a very important marker of `wellness' in this population.

questions.stem:
A 35-year-old woman developed bilateral optic neuritis with partial recovery. An MRI scan of
brain was normal. One year later, she developed paraparesis and incontinence. She was
given intravenous methylprednisolone followed by oral prednisolone but continued to
deteriorate. Further investigation results shown below.

ESR                                 7 mm/1st h (<>

antinuclear antibodies              positive

anti-aquaporin 4 (NMO-IgG)          negative
antibodies

MR scan of spine                    high-signal lesion from C6\xAD
                                    T10

CSF:

white cell count                    12/\xB5L (5)

oligoclonal bands                   negative

What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders (answer option C) are defined by the clinical and radiological features, and not by the patient's anti-aquaporin 4 antibody status. The combination of bilateral optic neuritis with a longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis and negative oligoclonal bands is highly suggestive of this diagnosis.
The anatomical distribution of weakness and sensory loss points to a sciatic nerve lesion, a recognised complication of hip replacement.
questions.stem:
A 75-year-old woman complained of weakness of her left leg. Forty-eight hours previously,
she had been given a spinal anaesthetic for a left total hip replacement. The operation notes
and anaesthetic chart suggested no untoward events during the operation. On examination,
neurological signs were confined to her left leg. There was severe weakness of flexion of her
knee, dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, inversion and eversion of her ankle, extension of her big
toe and flexion and extension of her toes. Her knee jerk was normal, but her ankle jerk was
absent. There was diminished sensation to pinprick over her calf, her sole and dorsum of her
foot extending up the anterolateral aspect of her shin to just below the tibial tuberosity. What
is the most likely diagnosis?

A 57-year-old man presented with increasing episodes of abnormal jerky movements of his
right arm and face. The movements generally lasted about 30 seconds and his wife stated
that he did not lose consciousness in the majority of the episodes but that he did not appear
to interact normally. On occasion, he had lost awareness during these episodes and had
been incontinent of urine. In between episodes, his wife thought that he was increasingly
forgetful.

Investigation results are shown below

MR scan of brain                       increased signal in left medial temporal lobe

CSF:

total protein                          0.80 g/L (0.15\xAD0.45)

lymphocyte count                       20/\xB5L (3)

What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The clinical presentation of facio-brachial seizures, with changes in MR scan of brain, is A).

The patient's presentation of acute, unilateral vision loss, pain with eye movement, and a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) suggests optic neuritis. Optic neuritis is often associated with demyelinating conditions like multiple sclerosis and can follow viral infections. Option B, "Central retinal artery occlusion," typically presents with sudden painless vision loss and a cherry-red spot on fundoscopy, not with pain on eye movement. Option C, "Retinal detachment," may cause vision changes but does not typically present with a RAPD or pain on eye movement. Option D, "Papilledema," is associated with increased intracranial pressure and is unlikely to be the cause of the patient's acute vision loss and pain.
questions.stem:
A 25-year-old woman presents with acute, unilateral vision loss, pain with eye movement,
and a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) in one eye. She reports a recent upper
respiratory tract infection. An MRI of the brain reveals a periventricular lesion. What is the
most likely diagnosis for her condition?

A 32-year-old nulliparous woman with a known history of lupus nephritis presents at 29
weeks gestation with hypertension and worsening proteinuria. You are concerned she is
developing pre-eclampsia. However, her lupus nephritis presented in a similar way at
diagnosis. Her baseline blood pressure has previously been well controlled on labetalol
200mg TDS. Pre-pregnancy proteinuria was 40mg/mmol. How best do you distinguish
between new onset pre-eclampsia and a flare of lupus nephritis?
questions.explanation: The sFlt/PlGF ratio <38 excludes pre-eclampsia (N Engl J Med 2016; 374:13-22). The presence of nephrotic range proteinuria and worsening renal function will occur in either disease. Hyaline casts are a normal finding in urine.

options.text: The presence of nephrotic range proteinuria (ACR > 220mg/mmol)
The presence of hyaline casts in the urine

questions.explanation: In chronic lithium toxicity, haemodialysis is recommended if patients have severe neurological symptoms such as seizure or confusion, or serum lithium that is not expected to drop to <1 mmol/L. A lithium conc 2.4 mmol/L is likely to drop below 1 mmol/L if the patient has normal renal function.
questions.stem:
According to the EXTRIP guideline, which of the following is a NOT an indication for
haemodialysis in chronic lithium poisoning?
What is NOT a feature of anticholinergic toxicity?
questions.explanation: Explanation: Answer is miosis. Mydriasis is caused by anticholinergic toxicity.

questions.stem:
A pre-terminal patient receiving oral opioid analgesia for metastatic cancer develops renal
failure and becomes drowsy. Your excellent intern very astutely wants to change the
patient's regular oxycodone to hydromorphone. The patient's current dose is oxycodone
dose is 20mg orally every 4 hours. Which of the following best describes the equivalent and
appropriate fourth hourly hydromorphone dose?
questions.explanation: Oxycodone 20mg orally is equivalent to hydromorphone 1.5-2mg IM/IV/SC. The patient is pre-terminal, so the most reliable route of administration is subcutaneous. Ref: Therapeutic guidelines, Palliative Care. Acute pain in patients prescribed opioids for chronic pain.

questions.stem:
What is the biggest benefit of radial EBUS bronchoscopy + biopsy vs CT guided
transthoracic biopsy?
questions.explanation: Notable reference: Hayama M, Izumo T, Matsumoto Y, Chavez C, Tsuchida T, Sasada S. Complications with Endobronchial Ultrasound with a Guide Sheath for the Diagnosis of Peripheral Pulmonary Lesions. Respiration. 2015;90(2):129-35. Epub 2015 6 19.

questions.stem:
A 22-year-old university student presents with 3 months of excessive daytime sleepiness. He
has a background history of depression for which he has taken sertraline for over 5 years.
He retires to bed at 12pm, has sleep latency at least 2 hours, then wakes at 6am feeling
 unrefreshed and at times with headaches. His partner has observed occasional snoring
episodes. He describes frequent episodes of sleep paralysis. Recently he experienced a
visual hallucination of his deceased grandmother immediately before falling asleep. He
reports sleepiness in the day and has unintentionally dozed off in a lecture. He has noticed a
deterioration in memory, mood and concentration. He does not nap routinely. On
examination he appears well with body mass index 23 kg/m2. He has a narrow palate with
Mallampati score of 2.
What is the most likely cause for this patient's sleepiness?
questions.explanation: The vignette is consistent with Type 1 narcolepsy

questions.stem: Which of the following would NOT be a risk factor for developing pulmonary hypertension?
questions.explanation: Bosetentan is an endothelin receptor antagonist medication used in the treatment of pulmonary hypertension.

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old former shipyard worker with a history of occupational asbestos exposure
presents with gradually worsening exertional dyspnoea, non-productive cough, and chest
discomfort. Pulmonary function tests reveal a restrictive pattern. High-resolution CT scans
show diffuse bilateral interstitial fibrosis with characteristic honeycombing. What is the most
likely diagnosis in this patient?
questions.explanation: Given the patient's history of occupational asbestos exposure and the clinical and radiological findings described, the most likely diagnosis is asbestosis. Asbestosis is characterized by progressive interstitial fibrosis in the lungs, leading to a restrictive pattern on pulmonary function tests and the presence of honeycombing on high- resolution CT scans. Option A, "Mesothelioma," is a malignant cancer of the pleura associated with asbestos exposure, but it typically presents with different symptoms and radiological features. Option B, "Pulmonary fibrosis," is a general term for interstitial lung diseases characterized by fibrosis, but in this context, asbestosis is a more specific and likely diagnosis, given the patient's history of asbestos exposure and clinical presentation. Option D, "Hypersensitivity pneumonitis," is an interstitial lung disease caused by an immune response to inhaled allergens or environmental triggers. While it can cause interstitial fibrosis, it is less likely than asbestosis in this case, considering the patient's history of asbestos exposure.
GnRH Agonist alone may flare bone pains. D is correct \xAD as study shows combination with of ADT and Docetaxel provides survival advantage and likely quicker relief to symptoms.
questions.stem:
Robert is a 71-year-old man with a history of localised prostate cancer treated with
radiotherapy a year ago. He presents with increasing bone pain and tenderness
involving the right chest wall. His prostate specific antigen is 863 ng/mL [<6]. His
bone scan is shown below.

The most effective initial therapy would be:

Which molecular marker is routinely tested before initiating anti-EGFR therapy in metastatic
colorectal cancer patients?
questions.explanation: KRAS mutations are a predictive biomarker for response to anti-EGFR therapies in metastatic colorectal cancer. Patients with KRAS wild-type status are more likely to respond to these therapies compared to those with KRAS mutations.

questions.stem:
A 35yo patient presents with a painless ulcer on their leg, which began as a painless papule
several months ago. There is no history of trauma. This has not improved with empirical
courses of cephalexin and cotrimoxazole via the GP. The appearance of the ulcer is as
follows:

The patient has no significant medical comorbidities. There are no signs of disease
elsewhere in the body. They previously lived in coastal Victoria but have never travelled
overseas. A punch biopsy has been performed from the edge of the ulcer. Histology
demonstrates granulomatous inflammation with fat necrosis, and occasional acid-fast bacilli
are noted. Bacterial, fungal, and mycobacterial cultures are negative after prolonged
incubation. What is the most likely pathogen in this instance?
questions.explanation: The syndrome presented is consistent with Buruli/Bairnsdale/Daintree ulcer, which is caused by Mycobacterium ulcerans. This infection is classically acquired in specific regions of Victoria and Queensland. It is relatively painless, and patients remain clinically well despite the severe appearance of the ulcer. M. ulcerans is extremely slow-growing, hence the yield of culture is low. PCR on a biopsy of tissue or swab of pus has excellent sensitivity/specificity for this condition and is the most common method used for confirmation of the diagnosis. Pseudomonas can be involved in chronic ulcers, however it is easy to culture, doesn't cause granulomatous inflammation, and appears as gram negative rods/bacilli on microscopy. Cutaneous Nocardia infection can cause a solitary nodule that progresses to ulceration. We would expect some clinical response to cotrimoxazole, although the duration of treatment (not stated in question) may have been too short. It is less often associated with granuloma formation, and is more classically described as filamentous, branching, Gram positive/modified AFB positive bacteria on histology. Cutaneous Mycobacterium abscessus can have a similar appearance and is indistinguishable from M. ulcerans on microscopy. However, it is often associated with trauma/inoculation injuries, and as a rapid-growing mycobacteria, it is relatively easy to culture.

Ceftriaxone coversNeisseria meningitidisand susceptible strains ofStreptococcus pneumoniae. As he is elderly, he is at risk ofListeria monocytogenesmeningitis. Listeriae are inherently resistant to ceftriaxone but are susceptible to benzylpenicillin. questions.stem:
The pedigree below represents a family with an X-linked condition. A polymorphic
marker has been identified close to the gene responsible for this condition. The
recombination fraction between the gene and the marker is 5%.

Individual  Genotype
1           A
2           AB
3           AB
4           C
5           AC

What is the best estimate of the individual labelled 5 being a carrier of the disorder?
questions.explanation: This question is asking what is the likelihood that the marker is still associated with the pathogenic allele even after the possibility of recombination. It is important to note that this is an X-linked condition, and there is NO recombination between the X and Y chromosomes. Individual 3 has inherited the affected allele from her father, but there has been no chance of recombination as it passed through his germline. In creating the gamete that produced her daughter (ie individual 5) however, the affected X chromosome has had potential for recombination with the other X chromosome. We're told this happens 5% of the time. So, the chance that the marker has NOT been shuffled away from the pathogenic allele is 95%.

questions.stem: Which finger movement becomes weaker in carpal tunnel syndrome?
questions.explanation: Explanation: Thumb abduction (abductor pollicus brevis) is mediated by the median nerve, which runs through the carpal tunnel. Index finger abduction (first dorsal interosseus) and fifth finger abduction (abductor digiti minimi) is mediated by the ulnar nerve which runs through the cubital tunnel at the elbow and Guyon's canal at the wrist. Index finger extension (extensori indicis proprius) is innervated by the radial nerve before the carpal tunnel.

questions.stem:
A pharmaceutical company is conducting a clinical trial to test the effectiveness of a new
drug for reducing blood pressure. They recruit 500 participants with hypertension and
randomly assign them to two groups: Group A receives the new drug, while Group B
receives a placebo. After eight weeks, blood pressure measurements are recorded for
each participant.
Assuming the data is normally distributed for both groups, which of the following
statistical tests is most appropriate for comparing the mean blood pressure reduction
between the two groups?
questions.explanation: In this scenario, you have two independent groups (Group A and Group B) with continuous data (blood pressure measurements) that you want to compare to determine if there is a statistically significant difference in mean blood pressure reduction between the two groups. Given the data is normally distributed, a parametric test is required. The most appropriate statistical test for this situation is an unpaired t-test. Option A, ANOVA is used when you have more than two groups to compare. Option B, the paired t-test, is used when you have paired or matched data (e.g., before and after measurements for the same individuals), which is not the case here. Option D, the Wilcoxon signed-rank test, is used for non-parametric data and is typically used when you have paired data and cannot assume a normal distribution. However, ANOVA is the more appropriate choice in this context with two independent groups and continuous data.
