The antibiotic susceptibility testing results for a Pseudomonas aeruginosa culture isolate
are shown below.

Piperacillin-tazobactam: Resistant
Ceftazidime: Susceptible
Cefepime: Susceptible
Meropenem: Resistant
Gentamicin: Susceptible
Ciprofloxacin: Resistant

What is the most likely mechanism of -lactam antibiotic resistance in this isolate?
questions.explanation: Porin protein alterations, which prevent the entry of antibiotics through the outer cell membrane, are the most common mechanism of -lactam antibiotic resistance in Pseudomonas aeruginosa. - lactam antibiotic resistance due to porin mutations typically results in sequential acquisition of resistance to individual -lactam agents following exposure to these agents rather than resistance to the entire -lactam antibiotic class. ampC-type -lactamases hydrolyse and convey resistance to 1st-3rd generation cephalosporins (including ceftazidime), monobactams, and older penicillin--lactamase inhibitor combinations (including amoxycillin-clavulanate and piperacillin-tazobactam) but not cefepime or meropenem. Carbapenemases hydrolyse and convey resistance to carbapenems, cephalosporins (including cefepime), and older penicillin-- lactamase inhibitor combinations and, in some types, monobactams. Acquisition of new or modification of existing penicillin-binding proteins is the predominant mechanism of -lactam antibiotic resistance in Gram-positive bacteria (e.g. methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, Enterococcus spp., Streptococcus pneumoniae).

questions.explanation: Explanation as to why others are false A \xAD Neurosyphilis can occur at any stage of syphilis infection. There is a common misconception that neurosyphilis refers only to a dementia-like process, there are actually some differences in presentation with early and late neurosyphilis. Early neurosyphilis tends to present with more acute neurological symptoms including meningitis, ocular and otosyphilis, and meningovascular strokes (infectious arteritis), and manifests within the first few weeks to years of infection. Late neurosyphilis comes on years to decades later, with main presentations being the classic dementing picture, or General Paresis, as well as "Tabes dorsalis"" a syndrome of posterior column sensory ataxia with lancinating pain. B \xAD Syphilis is most transmissible via open lesions in primary chancres, and also with some secondary syphilitic lesions (mucosal lesions and condylomata lata); essentially wet lesions. In contrast, "dry" lesions such as the macular dermal rash seen in the picture provided, seen commonly as a manifestation of secondary syphilis is more immunological in nature and carries a very low risk of transmission by skin contact. C \xAD Non-treponemal tests include Rapid Plasma Reagin and VDRL, which are quantitative measures of antibodies against lipoidal antigens, damaged host cells, and possibly from treponemes. This is non-specific and also changes with stage of the disease. Non- treponemal tests rise in titre during the primary and secondary phases, and fall over the tertiary/latent phase of infection, with some staying serofast (ie, low titre positive ongoing), or spontaneously become negative, even in the absence of treatment. Hence a negative non-treponemal test, if there is a positive treponemal test, can be reflective of either: - Treated syphilis (as treponemal tests remain positive for life) - Very early syphilis (before non-treponemal antibodies are made) - Late latent syphilis (graph taken from RACP lecture on Common STIs \xAD Syphilis 2022)

options.text: Neurosyphilis is a hallmark feature of the tertiary phase of syphilis infection
Syphilis is most transmissible via a primary chancre and widespread secondary syphilis dermal rash (as pictured above)
A negative non-treponemal test precludes syphilis infection

questions.stem:
A 71-year-old woman is referred into the Emergency Department by her General
Practitioner with serum sodium of 122 mmol/L. Her only symptom is lethargy.
The most recent previous pathology results were from 12 months prior when serum
sodium was 131 mmol/L.
Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with metformin (HbA1c
6.7%) and hypertension managed with candesartan and hydrochlorothiazide.

Urinary sodium = 58
Urine osmolality = 550

What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
questions.explanation: SIADH, fluid restriction is the more appropriate treatment There is some evidence for SGLT2 inhibitor use in SIADH, however fluid restriction is still first line

questions.stem:
Which of the following treatments for rheumatoid arthritis should be avoided in patients
with a history of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease or other cardiovascular risk
factors?
questions.explanation: A finding of increased lipid levels and malignancies amongst patients taking tofacitinib, a janus kinase inhibitor for rheumatoid arthritis, test the rate of major adverse cardiovascular events and cancers compared to treatment with tumour necrosis factor inhibitors (etanercept and adalimumab). This study found that the rate of major cardiovascular events was 3.4% compared to 2.5% with a hazard ratio of 1.33 (95%CI 0.91-1.94), and non-inferiority was not established for the co-primary endpoint of cardiovascular events and malignancies. Methotrexate is not known to have any increased Current prescribing information suggest not prescribing tofacitinib in patients at high cardiovascular or malignancy risk unless no other suitable alternative treatments are available. Reference: Ytterberg et al. N Eng J Med 2022, 386:316-326.

questions.stem:
A 32-year-old male presents to your clinic with a recent onset history of intermittent
cough worse at night time. He has recently changed his living environment with a move
into a new home. A chest x-ray organised by his GP was unremarkable.
You perform respiratory function tests the results are below:

The lung function is most in keeping with:

options.text: Variable airflow limitation as evidence by the change in FEV1
Fixed airflow with no change in the persistent reduced FEV1/ FVC post bronchodilator.
Obstructive pathology without variable airflow limitation

questions.explanation: This patient has obstructive spirometry as seen by the FEV1/FVC ratio of 61%. The variable airflow limitation is assessed by bronchodilator responsiveness, in this case the FEV1 increases by 31%. Note the most recent ATS/ERS guidelines published in 2021 in the European Respiratory Journal suggest a threshold of >10% change post-bronchodilator without any need to consider the absolute volume change. This is more in keeping with epidemiological studies of bronchodilator reversibility noting a past bias for people with smaller volume lungs who may exceed 10% but do not meet the absolute volume change threshold.

questions.stem:
A 34-year-old woman with oestrogen receptor positive Stage 2 invasive breast cancer is
commencing adjuvant endocrine therapy.

questions.explanation: Exemestane is an aromatase inhibitor used for the treatment of advanced breast cancer. It has shown benefit in young premenopausal early breast cancer patients, when administered together with ovarian suppression. The 5-year breast-cancer-free rate appears superior to tamoxifen and allows women to remain premenopausal.

On examination, he has a loud ejection systolic murmur, mild bibasal crepitations on

His latest transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) shows a calcified aortic valve, with peak

fraction is 37%.
His ECG is below:

A 33-year-old female is 15-weeks pregnant with her first child. She presents to the
obstetrics clinic for routine follow-up complaining of irritability, diarrhoea, and heat
intolerance. On physical examination you observe a fine tremor with ophthalmoplegia
and a small goitre. Her biochemistry is demonstrated below.

TSH <0.002mIU/L (0.45 \xAD 4.5)
FT3 4.1pmol/L (2.6 \xAD 6.0)
FT4 19pmol/L (9 \xAD 23)
b-hCG 4500+

Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment in the first instance?
questions.explanation: Goal in pregnancy for treatment is to maintain maternal free T4 in the high-normal range on the lowest dose of an antithyroid treatment as possible. Treat with propylthiouracil until 16 weeks then switch to carbimazole as PTU has milder birth defects. Carbimazole promotes the risk of foetal or maternal hypothyroidism. Non-selective cardiac beta blockers such as propranolol should be used sparingly due to the risk of IUGR, foetal bradycardia and hypoglycaemia.

Reference: Rodger MA, Kahn SR, Wells PS et al (2008). `Identifying unprovoked thromboembolism patients at low risk for recurrence who can discontinue anticoagulant therapy'. CMAJ. 356: 417-26.

questions.explanation: - Aim: Understanding SAAG calculations & differentials for high vs low SAAG - A: The patient has risk factors for metabolic syndrome/ NAFLD, however, there is no evidence of cirrhosis on imaging or signs of portal hypertension on imaging. Portal HTN has High SAAG, but this patient has high ascites protein (usually <25 if related to cirrhosis/portal hypertension). - B: Usually SAAG <11 with ascitic protein <25 - C: Cardiac ascites \xAD usually high SAAG; high protein. Also has high BNP. This patient had constrictive pericarditis, which is a diastolic heart failure. A normal echo does not exclude diastolic dysfunction and a high index of suspicion is required for diagnosis of constrictive pericarditis which can be difficult to diagnose. - D: No mention of risk factors for this in this case

Risks of prolonged psychotic disorders from psychedelic therapy appears minimal, however patients with a personal or family history of the use of psychedelic therapy to treat OCD, and no high-quality evidence to suggest benefit in any specific substance-use disorder. Lynch Syndrome is an autosomal dominant hereditary cancer syndrome, characterised
by mismatch repair deficiency.

What are the key molecular features of colorectal cancer that would suggest testing for
germline Lynch syndrome?
questions.explanation: Explanation: Microsatellite instability is a molecular finding that reflects the inability of cells to repair genetic defects (mismatch repair deficiency), the key mutation in Lynch Syndrome. MSI-High tumours account for 15-20% of colorectal cancers and are a good prognosis (B or D). BRAF-v600E mutation is a well known poor prognostic factor in colorectal cancer and is used to distinguish between sporadic and germline mismatch repair deficient tumours. BRAF mutation excludes Lynch syndrome, so the ans is D \xAD BRAF-wild type with MSI-high. References https://journals.lww.com/md- journal/Fulltext/2018/03020/The_significance_of_microsatellite_insta bility_in.7.aspx https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S10408428210012 68 https://www.nature.com/articles/bjc2015160 https://www.jmdjournal.org/article/S1525-1578(11)00314-X/fulltext

options.text: Microsatellite stable (MSI-low), v-raf murine sarcoma viral oncogene homolog B1 (BRAF) mutant

questions.explanation: Six key facts from "Suicide & self-harm" monitoring data - Australian Institute of Health and Welfare (https://www.aihw.gov.au/suicide-self- harm-monitoring/data/suicide-self-harm-monitoring-data) Over 3,000 deaths by suicide occur each year in Australia. In 2021, there were 3,144 deaths by suicide \xAD an average of about 9 deaths per day \xAD with an age-standardised rate of 12.0 per 100,000 population. Males are 3 to 4 times more likely to take their own life than females. In 2021, there were 2,358 male deaths at a rate of 18.2 per 100,000; there were 786 female deaths at a rate of 6.1 per 100,000. In 2021, the number of deaths by suicide was higher for males than females in all reported age groups. Females are more likely to attempt suicide than males or be hospitalised for intentional self-harm than males. Rates of ambulance attendances for suicide attempt and self-injury were higher for females than males. In 2020\xAD21 females made up almost two-thirds (66%) of intentional self-harm hospitalisations. There is no evidence that suspected deaths by suicide have increased in recent years. Published data from the suicide registers in New South Wales, Victoria and Queensland show that the numbers of suspected deaths from suicide in 2020 and 2021 were similar to previous years. Suicide is the leading cause of death for young people. Over one- third of deaths in Australians aged 15\xAD24 were due to suicide in 2021. Suicide rates are more than twice as high in young Indigenous Australians compared to non-Indigenous Australians. In 2017\xAD2021, the age-specific rate of suicide deaths was 3.1 times higher in Indigenous Australians aged 0\xAD24 and 2.9 times higher in those aged 25\xAD44, than non-Indigenous Australians.

options.text: Over 3,000 deaths by suicide occur each year in Australia
Suicide is the leading cause of death for young people
Males are more likely to take their own life than females
There is evidence that suspected deaths by suicide have increased in recent years

You are the medical registrar in a rural hospital. A 30-year-old woman presents with a
two-day history of headaches and low-grade temperatures. She is otherwise well and is
only on vitamin supplements. Her blood pressure is 110/70mmHg.

Her blood tests show:
WCC 11.2 x 10^9/L (Normal Range 4.0-10.0)
Haemoglobin 95 g/L (Normal Range 120-150)
MCV 85fL (Normal Range 80-100)
Platelets 25 x 10^9/L (Normal Range 150-400)
Blood film : occasional schistocytes seen
Bilirubin 40 umol/L (Normal Range <20)
PT 11.4 (Normal Range 9.0-13.0)
INR 1.0
aPTT 35 (Normal Range 25-37)
Creatinine 130 micromol/L (Normal Range 45-90)

Which of the following is the most important emergent management for this patient?
questions.explanation: Most likely clinical scenario is TTP with pentad of fever, anaemia, thrombocytopaenia, renal dysfunction and neurological symptoms. FFP can be transfused as a temporising measure until transfer and plasma exchange. Platelet transfusion is not indicated in the absence of bleeding and would likely be consumed by underlying process. Cryoprecipitate should be given to correct DIC - unlikely with normal coagulation studies. Lumbar puncture is not indicated and is unsafe with current platelet count.

questions.stem: Which antibody is associated with a risk of congenital heart block?
questions.explanation: The antibody response against the p200 epitope, spanning Ro52 amino acids (aa) 200 to 239, is a biomarker of increased maternal risk for the development of cardiac-NL in an offspring.

options.text: Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) to look for previous evidence of optic neuritis
Oligoclonal bands present in the serum and CSF
An second symptomatic clinical attack in the right leg at least 3 months after the first
A further non-enhancing T2 MRI lesion in an MS typical location

questions.explanation: Explanation: A patient who has only had 1 attack needs to meet criteria for dissemination in space and time. A second non- enhancing lesion in an MS typical location (periventricular, juxtacortical/cortical, infratentorial or spinal cord) meets criteria for both of these. The non-enhancement suggests it is an older lesion (dissemination in time) and the presence of the second lesion meets criteria for dissemination in space. The lesion does not need to be symptomatic for a diagnosis of MS. This is not to be mistaken for the PBS criteria for starting MS therapy which requires at least 2 symptomatic clinical attacks within the preceding 2 years. The other ans are incorrect as: The role of OCT to diagnose optic neuritis and therefore MS in the absence of clinical symptoms is not well-establish. Especially as loss of retinal nerve fiber layer thickness is not specific for optic neuritis and could be due to a number of other conditions. Hence, it is not part of McDonald criteria. It should only be used as support evidence in a patient who has a history consistent with optic neuritis but Oligoclonal bands must only be present in the CSF to meet McDonald Criteria. An addition symptomatic clinical attack needs to be in a different CNS site to demonstrate dissemination in space, otherwise it may be simply a flare or abhorrent conduction through the old MS lesion.

A 40 year old man with obesity and type 2 diabetes is found to have a serum urate of
0.53. He has no tophi and denies any episodes of acute joint pain.
The most appropriate treatment is to:

An active 61-year old farmer is brought in by ambulance with confusion to the
emergency department of a rural hospital. His past medical history is significant for
hypertension. There is no history of any bleeding disorder, trauma or recent surgery.
He is found to have a left facial droop, and no movement of the left arm and leg as well
as dysarthria. He was last seen well 5.5 hours prior to presentation but the time of onset
of symptoms is unclear.

CT brain does not show any abnormalities. The CT angiogram shows an occlusion of
the proximal right middle cerebral artery (M1 segment). CT perfusion study shows that
there is 100 mL of penumbra and 15 mL of core infarct.

What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Immediate transfer to a tertiary referral centre for endovascular clot retrieval
Thrombolysis with tPA in the peripheral hospital
Medical management with aspirin and clopidogrel as the patient is outside of window for thrombolysis.
Thrombolysis with tPA in the peripheral hospital and subsequent transfer to tertiary referral centre for endovascular clot retrieval

questions.explanation: There are two hyperacute stroke therapies- thrombolysis with tpA and endovascular clot retrieval (ECR) \xB7 The window for thrombolysis is 4.5 hours but in selected cases on perfusion mapping, if the core infarct is small (less than 70 mL) and there is a significant penumbra (ratio of penumbra:core greater than 1.2), thrombolysis up to 9 hours can be considered. \xB7 Patients with large vessel occlusion (internal carotid artery, basilar artery, middle cerebral artery \xAD M1 segment or proximal M2 segment) should have clot retrieval if presenting within 24 hours. There is evidence to suggest that patients who have thrombolysis (if eligible) prior to ECR, do better if there is any delay to ECR (eg presenting to a rural hospital as opposed to a tertiary ECR centre)

questioning, he reports his knees `buckling' when he argues with family members.

Which of these sleep study findings is most likely in this patient?
(MSLT = multiple sleep latency test; SOREMP = sleep onset REM periods).

options.text: REM latency 90 minutes, MSLT: mean latency 3 minutes, 0 SOREMPs
REM latency 45 minutes, MSLT: mean latency 5 minutes, 2 SOREMPs

questions.stem:
57 year old man with metastatic lung cancer was recently commenced on duloxetine for
pain and mood, ondansetron for nausea and tramadol for analgesia. A week later he
presents to ED with fevers and confusion.
Their observations show he is tachycardic and hypertensive.
On neurological examination he is hyper-reflexic and has spontaneous clonus.

What is the most likely differential?
questions.explanation: Serotonin syndrome is a potentially fatal condition caused by too much serotonin. Most cases occur as a result of combining medications that increase serotonin or taking overdose of a medication that can increase serotonin. It can present with altered mental status, autonomic dysfunction (hypertension, tachycardia, hyperthermia, diaphoresis etc.) and neuromuscular abnormalities (tremor, hyper-reflexia, clonus). The Hunter Toxicity Criteria Decision Rules are a way of diagnosis serotonin syndrome. To fulfil the Hunter Criteria patients must be on a medication that can cause serotonin syndrome and have one of the following: 1) Spontaneous clonus 2) Inducible clonus plus agitation or diaphoresis 3) Ocular clonus plus agitation or diaphoresis 4) Tremor plus hyperreflexia 5) Hypertonia plus temperature above 38\xBAC PLUS ocular clonus or inducible clonus In the case vignette the man is taking duloxetine (a serotonin- noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor), ondansetron (serotonin receptor antagonist) and tramadol (binds opioid receptors and inhibits noradrenaline and serotonin re-uptake) i.e. three medications that can increase serotonin. His symptoms are in keeping with serotonin syndrome. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome and anti-cholinergic toxicity are differential diagnoses for serotonin syndrome. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome is less likely because this man is not taking neuroleptic agents. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome normally causes bradyreflexia rather than hyper-reflexia and myoclonus is rare. Anti-cholinergic toxicity can present with agitation and hyperthermia however tone and reflexes are usually normal. This patient is not on anti-cholinergics. Leptomeningeal disease is a differential however the symptom cluster and the use of serotonergic agents means this is a less likely diagnosis.

questions.stem:
A 20-year-old Persian gentleman has presented to the emergency department with
acute onset abdominal pain and fever. Examination reveals a tender raised
erythematous rash on the dorsum of the right foot as well as a swollen right knee. A
synovial aspirate is performed, the results of which are demonstrated below:

Gram stain: negative
Cell count: WCC 80,000 x 106/L
Polarised light microscopy: No evidence of crystals
Culture: pending

This is his second similar presentation to the emergency department during the past
year. Colchicine is commenced with a view to prevent which of the following?
questions.explanation: This patient presents with Familial Mediterranean Fever (FMF). Colchicine has been demonstrated to decrease the frequency of FMF attacks, and plays an important role in preventing long-term complications including secondary (AA) amyloidosis, as well as small bowel obstruction due to recurrent attacks of peritonitis. Secondary amyloidosis is a major cause of mortality in FMF, commonly manifesting as renal amyloidosis. Amyloid deposition can also occur in the spleen, liver, gastrointestinal tract, heart, thyroid, and testes. Colchicine is not as effective in treating the chronic arthritis associated with FMF, and patients can require additional DMARD or biologic therapy.

questions.stem:
An 86-year-old lady has a history of hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, atrial
fibrillation, cardiac failure, and depression/anxiety. She presents with a fall and a history
of recurrent falls and has sustained a subdural haemorrhage. She has a blood pressure
of 146/90 at rest and a postural drop down to 120/80 on standing.

Which of the following medications poses the greatest risk for falls and should be
ceased in the first instance?:
questions.explanation: Risk for falls (OR): - Anti-depressants (including TCAs) - 1.68 - Neuroleptics/antipsychotics - 1.59 - Benzodiazepines - 1.57 - Sedatives/Hypnotics - 1.47 - Anti-hypertensives - 1.24 - Diuretics - 1.07 - B-blockers - 1.01
In patients with partial (oculomotor) CNIII palsy, imaging to exclude a posterior communicating artery aneurysm is indicated.
questions.stem:
A fifty year old man with a history of poorly controlled diabetes presents with double

On examination, there is partial right sided eyelid ptosis, and weakness of right eye

with normal visual fields to confrontation.

What is the most appropriate initial course of action?

A 32-year-old patient with a history of Sickle Cell Anaemia presents to the emergency
department with severe chest pain, fever, and shortness of breath.
A chest X-ray reveals a new infiltrate.

Which term best describes this patient's clinical presentation?
questions.explanation: The patient's clinical presentation is consistent with Acute Chest Syndrome (ACS), a severe complication of Sickle Cell Anaemia. ACS is characterized by new respiratory symptoms, such as chest pain, fever, and shortness of breath, often accompanied by radiographic evidence of lung infiltrates on imaging studies like chest X-rays. It is considered a medical emergency and can be caused by various factors, including infection, fat embolism, and vaso-occlusion in the pulmonary vasculature. Option B, "Vaso-occlusive Crisis," refers to the painful episodes that occur when sickled red blood cells block blood vessels. While vaso- occlusive crises are common in Sickle Cell Anaemia, the patient's presentation in this case is more suggestive of ACS due to the acute respiratory symptoms. Option C, "Aplastic Crisis," typically involves a severe drop in red blood cell production, leading to anaemia. It is not the most appropriate term for the patient's presentation. Option D, "Haemolytic Crisis," refers to an accelerated breakdown of red blood cells, which can result in anaemia and jaundice. This term is not specific to the patient's clinical presentation of acute respiratory symptoms.

options.text: Previous smallpox vaccination provides lifelong infection against monkeypox.
Monkeypox is indistinguishable from variola and vaccinia viruses by electron microscopy.

questions.explanation: Explanation: Multiple trials demonstrate a low-normal Hb target has better patient level outcomes, with particular attention to CHOIR, symptom burden (dyspnoea, fatigue, increased transfusion rate and transplant sensitisation) while higher Hbs are associated with excess thrombotic events (cardiac events, stroke and vascular access clotting).

denosumab?
questions.explanation: Explanation: there is no older age cut-off for denosumab. (Use in children in Australia is off-label except in skeletally-mature adolescents with giant cell tumour of bone/hypercalcaemia of malignancy.) Anti-resorptive therapy, such as with denosumab, should be considered in all older/post-menopausal people with a minimal trauma fracture. Denosumab may worsen hypocalcaemia, and hypocalcaemia (and related vitamin D deficiency) should be corrected prior to initiation. Prior bisphosphonate therapy is not a contra-indication to denosumab therapy.

options.text: Proceed to BEAM autologous stem cell transplant
Proceed with allogeneic stem cell transplant
Proceed to chimeric antigen receptor therapy (CART)
Proceed to a different salvage protocol such as RDHAC

questions.explanation: Explanation: Failed salvage in a transplant eligible patient should be referred for CART. This has a 40-45% of achieving long term disease free survival.
questions.stem:
A 52 year old male developed diffuse large B cell lymphoma and was managed with
RCHOP chemotherapy. 6 months later he relapsed and PET confirmed stage 3 disease.
He failed to respond to salvage RICE chemotherapy with a PET showing progressive
disease.

The next step in management is:

questions.explanation: Calcitonin is a specific and highly sensitive biomarker for medullary thyroid cancer and C cell disease. In parallel with calcitonin, CEA levels may provide valuable information for risk stratification in patients with medullary thyroid cancer. TSH is assessed to ensure the patient is adequately replaced with thyroxine post-operatively. Thyroglobulin is used to monitor recurrence of disease post- operatively in patients who had papillary thyroid cancer.

hour of breathlessness, with difficulty speaking. She is very concerned she is having an
episode of anaphylaxis secondary to COVID-19 vaccination. She has been using her
Ventolin puffer but has had no response.
There is no hypotension, rash or objective angioedema. Her breathing is noisy, her
chest is clear with a variable inspiratory sound, and her O2 on oximetry is normal.
Her only significant past medical history is mild asthma for which she takes occasional
Ventolin.

The most likely diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Inducible laryngeal obstruction (ILO) describes a transient, reversible narrowing of the larynx in response to external triggers. A number of other terms have been used to describe this condition including vocal cord dysfunction (VCD) and laryngeal dyskinesia. It occurs both in those with asthma but also as an asthma mimic. It can be triggered by exercise, gastropharyngeal reflux, psychological stress and inhaled irritants such as smoke, chemical cleaners and perfumes. It is an increasingly recognised in the differential diagnosis of anaphylaxis. The dominant complaint is breathless, often with throat tightness or fullness and dysphonia with cough. Stridor can be noted on inspiration, expiration or both. Typically there is no response to therapy with inhaled steroids or Ventolin. Anaphylaxis typically occurs within 30 minutes to 4 hours of exposure to the trigger of interest. Rash is present in >90% of patients. The features clinically are not those of anaphylaxis, though a serum tryptase should be performed acutely to help exclude the possibility of another trigger. Careful exclusion of severe asthma should be undertaken. However, the clinical findings and presentation do not favour a diagnosis of acute asthma. Hoarseness as a feature should alert one to an asthma mimic such as ILO. The failure to respond to beta-agonist therapy is also typical of ILO. A panic disorder is another possibility but would not be associated with hoarseness.

questions.explanation: COMPLETE trial addressed role routine revascularisation after STEMI. Previous studies in NSTEMI (CvLPRIT, PRAMI and DANAMI 3 PRIMULTI) demonstrated benefits in terms of need for urgent revascularisation, hospitalisation, cardiac death and re-infarction rates. COMPLETE aimed to achieve complete revascularisation after culprit primary PCI. Study methods included PCI for stenosis >70% and FFR (fractional flow reserve \xAD FFR) guided intervention for stenosis 40-69%. Study demonstrated reduced primary end point of CV death and re- infarction. Consensus guidelines favour an early invasive approach compared to stress testing or medical therapy. In terms of bypass surgery, the benefit is seen in left anterior descending disease in patients with diabetes mellitus.

questions.explanation: Fabry disease is an inherited metabolic disorder due to a pathogenic variant in the GLA gene. Common features of this disorder are hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, progressive renal failure, acroparaesthesia, hypo- or hyperhidrosis, gastrointestinal disturbances (irritable bowel syndrome), and periumbilical and groin angiokeratoma. It is inherited in an X-linked pattern although female carriers can present with mild features. The question is asking about classical Fabry disease, therefore the candidate will need to work out the chance of an affected male fetus. AB's maternal half-brother is affected, and given that AB's maternal grandfather also had features of Fabry disease, the disorder is likely inherited through AB's mother (rather than being a de novo variant in AB's brother). Hence, AB's mother is assumed to be an obligate carrier. The risk calculation for AB's pregnancy is as follows:

questions.explanation: Check point inhibitors are used to treat relapsed Hodgkin lymphoma. The majority of HL cells have increased numbers of chromosome 9p24.1 which codes for PD-L1 antigen on the cell surface. As a result, expression of this antigen is increased on the surface of the tumour cell resulting in immune evasion by inhibition of T cells through binding to PD1 on T cell surface. Pembrolizumab and nivolumab inhibit this immune check point by binding to and blocking to PD-1. This activates T cells to bind to tumour cells but can also cause autoimmune disease such as thyrotoxicosis, skin rash, colitis, pneumonitis, hypophysitis. This patient has clinical features of thyroiditis and the definitive treatment is prednisone and cessation of the check point inhibitor.

Disease X exhibits a prevalence of 10% amongst the general population, 20% amongst

questions.explanation: Pre-test odds = Pre-test probability / (1 \xAD Pre-test probability) = (40/100) / (1-40/100) = (40/100) / (60/100) = 40/60 = 2/3 Post-test odds = Pre-test odds x Positive likelihood ratio = Pre-test odds x Sensitivity/(1 \xAD Specificity) = 2/3 x (30/100)/(1-(80/100)) = 2/3 x (30/100)/((100-80)/100) = 2/3 x 30/20 = 2/3 x 3/2 = 1/1 Post-test probability = Post-test odds / (1 + post-test odds) = (1/1) / (1 + 1/1) = 1/2 = 50%

questions.explanation: The American Psychiatric Association's Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, fifth edition (DSM-5) lists five key features that characterize delirium: Disturbance in attention (reduced ability to direct, focus, sustain, and shift attention) accompanied by reduced awareness of the environment. The disturbance develops over a short period of time (usually hours to days), represents a change from baseline, and tends to fluctuate during the course of the day. An additional disturbance in cognition (memory deficit, disorientation, language, visuospatial ability, or perception). The disturbances are not better explained by another preexisting, evolving, or established neurocognitive disorder, and do not occur in the context of a severely reduced level of arousal, such as coma. findings that the disturbance is caused by a medical condition, substance intoxication or withdrawal, or medication side effect. Additional features that may accompany delirium and confusion, but are not essential, include the following:

demonstrates ST-elevation in the anterior leads that resolves over the next 20 minutes. A
coronary angiogram reveals normal coronary arteries. A transthoracic echocardiogram is
also normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for her condition?

options.text: Dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker or a diuretic
Dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker, renin-angiotensin system inhibitor or a diuretic
Renin-angiotensin system inhibitor or a Dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker

questions.explanation: occur more commonly in younger female and is characterised by transient ST elevation. The normal coronary arteries and echo findings are consistent with this diagnosis. This is treated with calcium channel blockers. Beta blockers can worsen vasospasm. Atorvastatin and antiplatelet therapy are treatment for coronary artery disease, not coronary artery spasm. The patient has albuminuric diabetic kidney disease and hypertension. This should be treated with a renin-angiotensin system inhibitor at maximally tolerated dose to treat albuminuria and blood pressure, and slow progression of chronic kidney disease. For more information, please see the KDIGO guideline 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline for Diabetes Management in Chronic Kidney Disease.
questions.stem:
Mrs RA is a 65-year-old lady. On clinical assessment her blood pressure is 150/90 mm Hg,
and she appears euvolaemic. Her estimated glomerular filtration rate is 32 ml/min/1.73m2
and her HbA1c is 8% (64mmol/mol). Her urine albumin to creatinine ratio is 50 mg/mmol.
What is the first line pharmacological agent for the management of her blood pressure?
Please choose the most correct answer.
Which of the following is true for women with established hypothyroidism in pregnancy?

options.text: Thyroxine should be withheld until TSH results are available
TSH should not be used to assessing thyroid function in the first trimester, due to the TSH receptor stimulation effects of beta hCG
Thyroxine dose is typically increased by 30-50% from early in the first trimester

questions.explanation: Thyroxine requirements typically increase in pregnancy and early dose increase is recommended. Universal iodine supplementation is recommended, even for patients with thyroid established. TSH can be used to assess thyroid function in the first trimester, but trimester- specific reference ranges should be used.

questions.stem:
Which of the following monoclonal antibodies is most likely to cause reactivation of hepatitis
B virus infection?
questions.explanation: Hepatitis B reactivation is most strongly associated with use of anti- CD20 therapies (eg rituximab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab). Glucocorticoids, and TNF inhibitors are also associated with HBV reactivation, but at lower rates than in anti-CD20 therapies.

questions.stem:
Which of the following hereditary disorders is NOT associated with increased risk of renal
cancers?
questions.explanation: A: Von Hippel-Lindau disease \xAD leads to excessive HIF1 promoting carcinogenesis (70% lifetime risk) B: Li Fraumeni \xAD increased risk of breast, sarcomas, brain cancer, leukaemias, medulloblastoma, adrenocortical cancers C: Tuberous sclerosis \xAD 5% risk of RCC, usually angiomyolipomas D: Birt-Hogg-Dube syndrome \xAD AD inherited folliculin (FLCN) mutation. Triad of pulmonary cysts (pneumothorax), fibrofolliculomas, RCC (30% risk of oncocytomas and chromophobic)

Beer's potomania is classically associated with excessive beer drinking low in solutes. Similarly this patient is drinking excessive amounts of wine and fluid with what can be presumed to be low protein and solute intake resulting in low urinary sodium and urinary osmolality. Primary polydipsia is the next closest answer based on the results, however given she has LFT derangements consistent with alcohol intake and also history of excessive alcohol intake, beer's potomania is the more correct answer. SIADH is incorrect due to urinary sodium less than 10, and urine osmolality also very low as well. Surreptitious diuretic use would involve urinary osmolality above 100. Hypoervolaemic hyponatraemia is incorrect as you would expect the urinary osmolality to be > 100 indicating a degree of urinary concentration reflecting the production of ADH. Further reading: Adrogu\xE9, H. J. , Tucker, B. M. & Madias, N. E. (2022). Diagnosis and Management of Hyponatremia. JAMA, 328 (3), 280-291. doi: 10.1001/jama.2022.11176.
questions.stem:
A 48yo female presents with 3 days of nausea, vomiting, reduced oral intake, lethargy,
sluggishness and new onset of confusion. She has a background of chronic alcohol misuse
and has been drinking 3 L wine a day for last 2 years. Her regular medications include
citalopram 20 mg daily, quetiapine 25 mg daily, pantoprazole 40 mg daily, thiamine 300 mg.
Her heart rate is 110 bpm, BP 110/80 mmHg and examines as being euvolaemic.

Her blood tests were as follows:
Na 112, Cl 61, K 2.3, urea 4.0, Cr 56, glucose 5, serum osmolality 234
AST 56, ALT 28, ALP 166, GGT 753.
Urinary sodium < 10, Urinary osmolality < 100

What is the aetiology of her hyponatraemia?

A previously well 40-year-old patient has unexplained weight loss over the last four weeks,
along with night sweats and fever. The patient has a history of dental work six weeks prior to
the onset of symptoms. Laboratory tests have identified gram-positive cocci in chains in their
blood culture. What is the most appropriate therapy for this patient?
questions.explanation: In cases of suspected subacute infective endocarditis with the presence

of gram-positive cocci in chains, Streptococcus viridans is a common causative organism. The most appropriate therapy is typically a combination of Penicillin G (or ampicillin) and gentamicin. Empirical therapy with vancomycin and gentamicin (Option A) may be considered if methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is suspected. Ceftriaxone and gentamicin (Option C) may be considered for enterococcal endocarditis, and doxycycline and ceftriaxone (Option D) are more commonly used for treating Coxiella burnetii endocarditis (Q fever). Given the patients history, he most likely has central nausea related to his progressive disease (persistent nausea). Cyclizine would therefore be the preferred choice. Metoclopramide is used 1st line if the nausea is related to a GI cause. Ondansetron is often avoided if possible due to its constipating effect. Olanzapine is normally considered second line due to its sedating effects.
questions.stem:
A 52-year-old man with progressive Glioblastoma Multiforme presents with worsening
headaches and persistent nausea but no vomiting. His bowels are opening normally. He is
currently not taking any regular anti-emetics. Which anti-emetic would you consider starting
1st line?

A 75-year-old man presents with progressive exertional shortness of breath on a background
of 60 pack years of smoking, ceased several years ago. He demonstrates diffusely reduced
breath sounds with a widespread expiratory wheeze and prolongation of the expiratory
phase of ventilation. He reports two episodes within the last year of significantly worsened
dyspnoea and a cough productive of discoloured sputum; he was given oral antibiotic and
oral corticosteroid on each of these occasions. His regular respiratory therapy consists of a
daily combination inhaler (umeclidinium/ vilanterol) and twice daily salbutamol via spacer. He
takes a 300 m walk over about 10 minutes three times a week. He has received four SARS-
CoV-2 vaccines but has not had an influenza vaccine for several years. His FEV1 is 35% of
the predicted value and his FVC is 55% of the predicted value; his TLC is 120% of the
predicted value and his RV is 155% of the predicted value. His chest CT shows anatomically
complete oblique fissures bilaterally, and extensive emphysema, predominantly in the upper
lobes. What is the LEAST appropriate intervention?
questions.explanation: This man could only be deconditioned and would be, all other things being equal, an excellent candidate for pulmonary rehabilitation. Influenza vaccination is always recommended in a COPD cohort, in the absence of any contraindication. He is a frequent exacerbator, so might benefit from the addition of inhaled corticosteroid. Valve insertion must always follow pulmonary rehabilitation, and patients do best if the RV is >250% predicted, so he is not a good candidate for this procedure.

options.text: Combination inhaler change to fluticasone furoate / umeclidinium / vilanterol

questions.explanation: This above condition is Chronic Spontaneous Urticaria (CSU). It is a condition characterized by the recurrent appearance of urticaria and/or angioedema for at least six weeks without a clear identifiable cause. The exact cellular and immunological mechanisms underlying CSU are complex and not fully understood. However, several cell types and immune pathways are believed to play a role in the development of CSU: Mast Cells are believed to be the main driver of the histamine release, Basophils have also been linked however are believed to have less of a role, CD4+ act on the mast cells to promote them and other parts of the cascade including Complement system, cytokines and IgE are also involved.
questions.stem:
An 18-year-old girl visits the clinic with complaints of recurrent episodes of itchy, raised, red
welts on her skin. She reports experiencing these episodes almost daily for the past few
months. The lesions typically last for a few hours and then resolve on their own. She denies
any known allergies or recent changes in her diet or environment. What is the most likely cell
type driving this underlying this pathophysiology?

questions.explanation: The syndrome presented in the case history is mucormycosis/zygomycosis, which is caused by a group of rapidly growing moulds. Liposomal amphotericin B (LAmB) is the only antifungal listed which has activity against zygomycetes, hence it is the most appropriate antifungal as adjunctive therapy to urgent surgical debridement. Fluconazole only has activity against yeasts, not moulds. Voriconazole does have anti-mould activity, however, it does not cover zygomycetes. Anidulafungin (along with other echinocandins e.g. caspofungin, micafungin) are mainly used in invasive candidiasis. Posaconazole and isavuconazole (not listed as options here) are broad- spectrum azoles that do have some activity against zygomycetes. They are often used as stepdown therapy (given that LAmB is intravenous- only) or when there are significant contraindications to LAmB.
questions.stem:

presents with sinus congestion, severe headache, and vision change. The photo below
demonstrates the salient physical findings:

You are concerned of invasive fungal sinusitis and urgently contact ophthalmology/ENT.
What is the most appropriate empirical antifungal to commence?

questions.explanation: Meta-analysis revealed that donepezil and rivastigmine were superior to placebo in improving cognitive function in DLB, and in fact DLB pts may even have a better response than AD pts. Fluctuating cognition (b) is one of the core diagnostic features. (a) and (d) are both supportive clinical features.

options.text: Poor cognitive response to cholinesterase inhibitors

questions.stem:
Which of the following is NOT a supportive feature for a diagnosis of Dementia with Lewy
Bodies (DLB)?

Urine microscopy and culture results are shown below:

    \xB7 White cell count: >100 x 106 cell/L
    \xB7 Red cell count: >100 x 106 cell/L
    \xB7 Epithelial cell count: <10 x 106 cell/L
    \xB7 Bacteria seen

Culture: >108 CFU/L of Escherichia coli isolated

In the absence of clinical features of urinary tract infection, for which of the following patients
is antibiotic therapy indicated based on this result?
questions.explanation: Asymptomatic bacteriuria is common, with a prevalence of: -20-25% amongst post-menopausal women -5-15% amongst older men -2-7% amongst young women -1-3% amongst neonates, infants, children, and adolescents, and young men Antibiotic therapy is only recommended in patients with asymptomatic bacteriuria and 1 of the following: -Pregnancy (decreased pyelonephritis, pre-term delivery, and low-birth- weight risks) -Renal transplantation recently (3 months) (decreased renal allograft failure risk) -Urinary tract surgery planned (pre- or peri-operative) (decreased infectious complication risk) Antibiotic therapy is not recommended for other patients with asymptomatic bacteriuria because of the benign natural history, increased risk of adverse effects and antimicrobial resistance, and unchanged risk of urinary tract infection and other infections.

options.text: 25-year-old male with neutropaenia following allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation for acute lymphoblastic leukaemia without fever
50-year-old male undergoing elective transurethral resection of the prostate
65-year-old female who underwent renal transplantation two years ago
80-year-old female presenting with a fall and nitrites detected on urinalysis

questions.stem:
A 59-year-old man with cleared hepatitis C-related cirrhosis presents to clinic reporting
worsening shortness of breath. On examination, the chest is clear to auscultation and there
is mild ascites, with a normal jugular venous pressure and no peripheral oedema. Resting
saturations are found to be 95% when lying for examination, and 88% when standing.
Which of the following is true regarding management?
questions.explanation: The condition described is hepatopulmonary syndrome with the typical

options.text: Ascitic drainage is likely to improve the dyspnoea
Prostacyclin analogues and/or phosphodiesterase inhibitors may improve the condition
Liver transplantation referral should be considered

questions.stem:
A 32-year-old female presents to the emergency department following being bitten on the leg
by her cat. The woman is well with no fevers or any haemodynamic instability. What would
the correct antibiotic treatment be for this woman?
questions.explanation: finding of orthodeoxia. The absence of peripheral fluid overload/elevated JVP also leans against portopumonary hypertension (in which case B and C would be correct), and the clear chest suggests an absence of hepatic hydrothorax (in which case A would be correct). The only treatment for HPS is liver transplantation, with some patients requiring oxygen therapy prior to this. Severe hepatopulmonary syndrome is a contraindication for transplant, but there is insufficient information in this picture to suggest a severe phenotype or contraindication to transplant. Candidates should be aware of the diagnostic criteria and treatment for all three causes of dyspnoea in decompensated cirrhosis patients. For non-systemic infections involving cat bites oral Augmentin is the first line therapy. Ciprofloxacin is used in combination with clindamycin for non-systemic infections. For infections with systemic features IV Augmentin or Tazocin can be used.

options.text: No antibiotics required if washed out

questions.stem:
A 67-year-old diabetic man presents with a 2-year history of frequent falls. He describes a
progressive decline in his ability to climb stairs and use his hands. On examination, there is
prominent weakness of finger flexion and knee extension bilaterally, with other muscles
preserved. His reflexes are preserved, and the sensory examination is normal. His gait is
normal with no festination. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The hallmark of inclusion body myositis (IBM) is preferential weakness

questions.stem:
A 34-year-old Caucasian male presents to the emergency department with tachypnoea,
hypoxia and acute unilateral chest pain that commenced whilst walking. He has a history of
tobacco use (15 pack years, current smoker) and marijuana use through a water pipe
(`bong'). There is no other medical history. A chest x-ray demonstrates a unilateral
pneumothorax. An intercostal catheter is placed and CT chest is performed, demonstrating
multiple, diffuse, irregularly shaped cysts. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: of finger flexion and quadriceps (knee extension), with relative preservation of other musculature. Progressive supranuclear palsy

questions.stem:
Which of the following laboratory findings is a specific marker for diagnosing Giant Cell
Arteritis (GCA)?
questions.explanation: would cause parkinsonian features such as festination and an abnormal gait. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy should affect both reflexes (reduced) and sensation (large fibre sensory loss). A cervical myelopathy would cause proximal weakness with brisk reflexes. A pneumothorax is a classic first presentation of a cystic lung disease. Regarding the options: A is a cystic lung disease seen almost exclusively in women. B is a cystic lung disease seen equally in younger men and women and is heavily associated with tobacco use. In patients with the pulmonary subtype tobacco cessation can be curative. C should be considered in a patient with particular exposures (as this patient has through their use of a water pipe) however imaging findings generally include nodular and ground glass change with an upper lobe predominance. D is a cystic lung disease typically seen in the setting of other autoimmune diseases/phenomena such as Sjogren's syndrome or Raynaud's disease. Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA) is a challenging diagnosis to confirm, and there is no single specific laboratory test that definitively confirms the condition. However, among the options provided, elevated levels of interleukin-6 (IL-6) have been associated with GCA and can serve as a useful biomarker. IL-6 is an inflammatory cytokine that is often elevated in patients with GCA. Option A (elevated ESR) and Option B (elevated CRP) are commonly used markers of inflammation and are often elevated in GCA, but they are not specific to this condition and can also be elevated in other inflammatory disorders. Option C (positive anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies or ANCA) is typically associated with other vasculitic conditions, such as Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (GPA) and Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (EGPA), rather than GCA. Therefore, IL-6 is the most specific marker listed for GCA, though it is still not exclusive for diagnosis and must be considered alongside clinical and imaging findings.

options.text: Assessing the risk of attrition bias in included studies
Comparing the magnitude and direction of primary results in included studies
Assessing the risk of publication bias

questions.explanation: A funnel plot is a scatterplot used in meta-analysis to visually detect the presented below. The plot displays the studies results (X-axis) and precision (Y-Axis). An ideal funnel plot is one where the included studies have scattered on either side of the overall effect line is a symmetrical manner. Severe asymmetry to either side is an indication that publication bias may be present.

You are seeing Mary, a 67-year-old female with limited systemic sclerosis for the first time
today. She reports increasing breathlessness over the past year. Her lung function tests over
the last twelve months are shown below

                       Feb 2022       Feb 2023

FVC                    82% predicted  80% predicted

FEV1                   82% predicted  80% predicted

FEV/FVC                92%            91%

TLC                    84% predicted  82% predicted

Gas Transfer           68% predicted  43% predicted

Which one of the following tests is most likely to reveal the cause of her breathlessness?
questions.explanation: PFTs show stable lung volumes (FVC and TLC) but a steadily declining

gas transfer. In the setting of Sys scl, this pattern is strongly suggestive of pulmonary hypertension and an echocardiogram is most likely to reveal this diagnosis. The correct answer is amyloid angiopathy which is associated with lobar intracerebral hemorrhages and T2-weighted gradient-echo imaging shows evidence of chronic microbleeds/haemosiderosis at the cortical junctions. Aspirin-related bleeds are generally associated with lobar intracerebral hemorrhages Hypertensive small vessel disease affects penetrating arteries which causes non-cortical lacunar infarcts Micro emboli is associated with cortical or subcortical infarcts
questions.stem:
An 80-year-old man is referred for an MRI scan of the brain because of mild forgetfulness.
Cognitive assessment shows minor abnormalities in episodic memory and slowed responses
on executive tasks. He has generally been quite healthy and active, has mild hypertension
treated with a beta-blocker and takes a daily low-dose aspirin. On T2/fluid-attenuated
inversion recovery imaging, hypointense lesions are seen. Follow-up T2 gradient-echo
imaging shows lesions consistent with microhemorrhages at the cortical-subcortical
junctions. What is the most likely etiology of these lesions?

A 78-year-old male was admitted to hospital with acute onset dyspnoea. Clinical examination
revealed bilateral lower limb pitting oedema, pleural effusion and elevated JVP. The N-
terminal pro-BNP level was 6173 pg/mL. Renal function was normal. Past history is notable
for CABG 10 years prior and ICD in situ. Usual medications include: Entresto, bisoprolol,
spironolactone, aspirin and frusemide. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm 90bpm, narrow
QRS. BP 126/72 mmHg. CXR showed venous congestion and confirmed moderate sized
bilateral pleural effusions. Bedside TTE estimated LV ejection fraction 35%.
Which of the following in-hospital treatment strategies is the next best step to treat his
current condition and facilitate early discharge?
questions.explanation: A. Incorrect \xAD digoxin reduced hospitalization in HFrEF patients in a failure is diuretics and decongestion. B. Incorrect \xAD similarly, these are GDMT used in chronic stable patients for prognosis and symptoms. Not for acute heart failure C. Incorrect \xAD no indication for IV heparin 500mg IV acetazolamide resulted in successful decongestion within 3 days and reduced hospital LOS.

options.text: Switch to IV frusemide, add digoxin
Switch to IV frusemide, add SGLT-2 inhibitor, give iron infusion
Switch to IV frusemide, stop bisoprolol, start IV heparin
Switch to IV frusemide, add IV acetazolamide

questions.stem:
A 46-year-old woman is noted incidentally to have moderate neck swelling. A symmetrical
goitre is noted on examination with no specific thyroid nodules palpable and no cervical
lymph nodes palpable. She is clinically euthyroid. TSH is normal. There is no family history
of thyroid cancer. A thyroid ultrasound is performed.
According to ACR-TIRADS criteria used for the assessment of thyroid nodules, which of the
following thyroid nodules would be most likely to warrant a fine needle biopsy to be
performed to exclude thyroid cancer?

Thyroid         Size    Composition Echogencity Shape         Margin Echogenic foci
Ultrasound
characteristic  1.4 cm  Mixed cystic  Hypoechoic    Taller    Lobulated    Microcalcifications

options.text: 3.1 cm  and solid     Isoechoic   than wide   Ill-defined Spongiform                            Smooth       Macrocalcifications
Wider     Ill-defined 0.4 cm Solid          Hypoechoic   than tall               Peripheral (rim
Wider                  calcifications) 2.2 cm Cystic         Anechoic     than tall               None

questions.explanation: TIRADS scoring as per table: Thyroid Siz Composition Echogencity Shape Margin Echogenic Score- FNA or Ultrasound e foci Follow characteristic A. 1.4 Mixed cystic Hypoechoic Taller Lobulated Microcalcificat 11 cm and solid than ions (punctate TR5 \xAD Highly wide echogenic suspicious 2 points 2 points foci) 3 points 3 points FNA as size > 1 point 1cm B. 3.1 Spongiform Isoechoic Wider Ill-defined Macrocalcifica 2 points cm than tall tions TR2 0 points Not suspicious 0 points 1 point 0 points 1 point No FNA C. 1.1 Solid Hypoechoic Wider Smooth Peripheral 6 points cm than tall (rim TR4 calcifications) Moderately 0 points 2 points suspicious 2 points 2 points 0 points Follow as 1.1- 1.5 cm D. 2.2 Cystic Anechoic Taller Ill-defined Nil 3 points cm than TR3 wide Mildly 0 points 0 points 3 points 0 points 0 points suspicious Follow as 1.5- 2.5 cm The ACR-TIRADS gives a score based in US imaging characteristics that gives a risk rating for the existence of thyroid cancer and together with the size of the lesion guides as to whether the nodule warrants further Ix with a US guided FNA biopsy or follow-up with repeat US. As per the TIRADS scoring features such as solid, hypoechogenic, taller than wide (growth against the thyroid plane), extra-thyroidal extension and micro-calcifications (also called punctate echogenic foci) are associated with a higher risk of thyroid cancer being present. The larger the lesion the higher risk also and together guide whether to perform thyroid US guided FNABx. The other features are less associated with risk but depending on the number of features and size of the lesion may still warrant FNA biopsy. Of course, other non-ultrasound characteristics of the patient should be taken into consideration as to whether to perform a thyroid biopsy such as family history of thyroid cancer, prior exposure to radiation and patient's level of concern. There are websites that facilitate calculation of risk eg: TI-RADS Calculator \xAD Calculates TI-RADS Score (tiradscalculator.com)

questions.stem:
Exposure to which antibiotic is associated with the LEAST risk of community-acquired C.
difficile infection?

A 35-year-old woman is evaluated for AKI. She gave birth by vaginal delivery 5 days prior to
the onset of AKI. Her peripartum course was complicated by haemorrhage requiring blood
transfusion. She has no history of preeclampsia. She has not experienced fever, diarrhoea,
or rash. Medications include ondansetron, prenatal vitamins, and aperients.
On physical examination, her BP is 160/100 mmHg. She has icteric sclera. A grade I/VI mid-
systolic murmur is present. Lung sounds are diminished at the bases. The abdomen is mildly
tender to palpation, with hypoactive bowel sounds. There is no appreciable
hepatosplenomegaly. She has 3+ lower extremity oedema. The skin is normal except for
jaundice.
Serologic test results for HIV, hepatitis B, hepatitis C, and syphilis are all negative. Urinalysis
is notable for 3+ blood and 2+ albumin; urine microscopy reveals 10-100 erythrocytes and
occasional granular casts. Frequent schistocytes are present on a peripheral blood smear.
Abdominal ultrasound demonstrates a small amount of pelvic ascites. The liver and biliary
tract appear normal. Kidney size and shape are normal, and there is no hydronephrosis.

             Result      Ref

Haemoglobin  67 g/L      115-165

Platelets    37          150-400

Creatinine   365 umol/L  60-100

ALP          110 U/L     30-120

ALT          50 U/L      10-40

AST          37 U/L      10-40

Bilirubin    55 umol/L   <20

LDH          724 U/L     120-250

Haptoglobin  0.15 g/L    0.30-2.15

ADAMTS13     40%         >60%

Haemodialysis was initiated for solute and volume management.
Which one of the following is the most effective treatment for this patient?
questions.explanation: The most effective treatment for this patient with postpartum haemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS) is eculizumab. This patient presents with AKI attributable to thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA). The differential diagnosis includes HUS, TTP, and haemolysis with elevated liver enzymes and low platelet count (HELLP) syndrome. ADAMTS13 activity level >10% essentially excludes the diagnosis of TTP, which is due to inherited or acquired loss, or functional loss, of this von Willebrand factor-cleaving protein. Treatment of TTP includes plasma exchange and corticosteroids. HELLP syndrome is unlikely in the absence of preeclampsia and more significant abnormalities in liver tests. Mild elevations of AST can occur with haemolysis and does not imply liver parenchymal injury at this level. HUS is more common in the peripartum and postpartum periods. HUS has several potential causes. HUS associated with a Shiga toxin- producing Escherichia coli (STEC) is unlikely in the absence of diarrhea. HIV infection and chemotherapy-induced HUS are similarly unlikely. Other aetiologies of HUS in the appropriate clinical setting include lupus, antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, scleroderma, and malignant hypertension. In the absence of these identifiable causes of HUS, atypical HUS (aHUS) is diagnosed by exclusion. Atypical HUS is a rare disease that is caused by defective regulation of the alternative complement pathway. It is characterized by microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and AKI due to TMA. Mutations have been described in complement factors H, I, and B and many other regulatory components of the alternative complement pathway. Antibodies to complement have also been discovered. Complement dysregulation leads to uncontrolled complement activation, resulting in endothelial injury and its associated clinical manifestations. Eculizumab, a monoclonal antibody that inhibits C5 activation, can be used to treat patients with conditions that result from dysregulated alternative complement activation. Eculizumab binds C5 and decreases production of C5a and the membrane attack complex, thereby reducing further endothelial damage. Occasionally, aHUS may respond to plasma exchange if the alternative complement pathway dysfunction is due to an acquired antibody, but eculizumab is preferred due to its more predictably reliable effect. Rituximab and IV immunoglobulin are sometimes added to treat TTP due to an acquired autoantibody, especially in the setting of relapse or failure to improve with plasmapheresis, but neither would take priority over eculizumab treatment for aHUS.

questions.stem:
A 24-year-old woman with MS is stable on anti-CD20 therapy. Which side effect do you NOT
need to monitor for?
questions.explanation: Recurrent upper respiratory infections are common and due to decreased immune globulins. Tx change is recommended if frequent infections occur due to low IgG or IgA, low IgM is common. Neutropenia occurs in 3% of patients and requires tx change in most cases or GMCSF. Recurrence of latent hepatitis B is a risk on anti-CD20 therapy, therefore hepatitis B vaccination is required prior to treatment start, but can potentially not be effective during therapy. Cardiac abnormalities are a risk on initiation of S1P inhibitors.
All of the above medications may cause seizures, particularly in high doses. Imipenem has the highest incidence of seizures.
questions.stem: Which of the following medications is most likely to cause symptomatic seizures?

A 40yo man has increasing dyspnoea and reduced exercise tolerance. As part of the work
up of his symptomatology, he undergoes a right heart catheterisation with the following
output:

mPAP 26 mmHg
PAWP 16 mmHg
PVR 3 woods units
In the context of the above, what is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: This RHC shows combined pre and post capillary pulmonary hypertension. He needs workup for left sided heart disease before treating any pre-capillary contribution to his disease.

options.text: Commence combined therapy with Ambrisentan and Sildenafil
Commence combined therapy with Sildenafil and Riociguat

questions.stem:
A 48-year-old female is presenting to clinic with 5 days history of arthralgias with no joint
swelling, and a non-specific rash on her chest. She started taking an antibiotics for urinary
tract infection 2 days prior to the rash developing. The referring doctor is concerned about
Systemic lupus. Which test would be most useful to rule out Systemic lupus?
questions.explanation: ANA is positive in >95% of SLE cases A positive ANCA with negative antibodies are not clinically significant in this setting. Anti-Sm has high specificity for lupus, but not low sensitivity. Absence of urinary abnormality does not rule out other organ involvement

options.text: Positive ANCA with negative PR-3 and MPO

questions.explanation: Option A will cause a frameshift mutation Option B will cause a splicing mutation and a frameshift mutation Option D will cause a splice site As per the HGSA agreed standards for pathogenicity of mutations: See Richards et al.: Standards Guidelines for the Interpretation of Sequence Variants (2015) (https://www.acmg.net/docs/Standards_Guidelines_for_the_Interpretati on_of_Sequence_Variants.pdf)

Deletion of a nucleotide at position 2
Substitution of a nucleotide at position 1
Substitution of a nucleotide at position 2

As such, post- exposure prophylaxis is not routinely recommended for this specific scenario according to Australian National Guidelines from ASHM (Post- Exposure Prophylaxis after Non-Occupational and Occupational Exposure to HIV). Case by case discretion is used though, particularly if there are concerns for more direct person-to-person blood contact, or suspicion that the story presented may not be complete. This trait has been known for decades and is encoded by chromosomal determinants in the core genome of these organisms, but its molecular basis remains incompletely understood. Thus far, in E. faecalis, a handful of genetic determinants have been shown to be required for intrinsic cephalosporin resistance: the low-affinity penicillin-binding protein Pbp5; a two-component signal transduction system, CroRS; a transmembrane Ser/Thr kinase, IreK; and one of the early enzymes involved in synthesis of peptidoglycan precursors, MurAA." https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK190420/#:~:text=Intrinsic%20c ephalosporin%20resistance- ,E.,molecular%20basis%20remains%20incompletely%20understood.

\xAD 1.50mmol/L], albumin 39 g/L [36\xAD 47 g/L], and creatinine 88 umol/L [60 \xAD 110 umol/L]. You

thromboembolism prophylaxis recommended?
questions.explanation: spontaneously clear the infection without intervention. Approximately 15-45% of people infected with hepatitis C will clear the virus from their bodies without the need for antiviral treatment. Option a) is incorrect because hepatitis C is primarily transmitted through exposure to infected blood, not through fecal-oral contact. Option c) is incorrect because hepatitis C can have extrahepatic manifestations, affecting organs such as the kidneys, skin, and nervous system. Option d) is incorrect because DAA therapy for chronic hepatitis C has significantly improved cure rates, with cure rates exceeding 90% in many cases. DAA therapy represents a major advancement in the treatment of hepatitis C. 1. Alter MJ. Epidemiology of hepatitis C virus infection. World J Gastroenterol. 2007;13(17):2436-2441 2. questions.explanation: Diagnosis of Parkinson disease (PD) in life is based on clinical diagnostic features, as the gold standard of diagnosis is have published clinical diagnostic criteria (2015), which require bradykinesia with either a rest tremor or rigidity. No absolute exclusion criteria are permitted, the presence of which rules out PD: unequivocal cerebellar abnormalities, downward vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, primary progressive aphasia in the first five years of the disease, Parkinsonian features only in the lower limbs for >3 years, treatment with dopamine-blocker to cause drug-induced parkinsonism, absence of observable response to high-dose levodopa, or unequivocal cortical sensory loss. Olfactory loss is instead a supportive criteria for diagnosis.

A 22-year-old woman presents with fatigue, palpitations, and breathlessness after a recent intravenous iron
    infusion. Lab results show severely low phosphate but normal calcium (2.3 mmol/L). Which of the following

In which tissue are thyroid hormones primarily converted from thyroxine (T4) to triiodothyronine (T3) through
    the action of type 2 iodothyronine deiodinase (D2)?
questions.explanation: Type 2 iodothyronine deiodinase (D2) is primarily expressed in the brain, where it converts thyroxine (T4) to the biologically active triiodothyronine (T3). This local conversion of T4 to T3 within the brain tissues allows for precise regulation of thyroid hormone action in neural functions and development. The other tissues listed (liver, kidneys, and skeletal muscle) predominantly express type 1 iodothyronine deiodinase (D1) for peripheral conversion of T4 to T3. Reference: Bianco AC, Kim BW. Deiodinases: implications of the local control of thyroid hormone action. J Clin Invest. 2006;116(10):2571-2579.

Delays gastric emptying. Glucagon-like peptide: Secreted by L cells in the distal ileum, colon, jejunum and duodenum. Responsible for glucose-dependent insulin secretion

questions.stem: Which of the following best explains the mechanism by which cholera toxin causes profuse watery diarrhoea?

options.text: Inhibition of Na/K-ATPase in enterocytes, leading to intracellular sodium accumulation
Activation of adenylate cyclase via ADP-ribosylation of Gs, increasing intracellular cAMP
Direct stimulation of CFTR gene transcription, increasing chloride channel expression
Inhibition of phosphodiesterase, leading to accumulation of intracellular cGMP reset

questions.explanation: Cholera toxin binds to GM1 ganglioside receptors on intestinal epithelial cells. It ADP-ribosylates the Gs subunit, locking it in an active state. This leads to persistent activation of adenylate cyclase, increasing cAMP levels. Naloxone is a pure antagonist. It has a very high affinity for mu receptors and a lower affinity for the other receptor binding sites. Naloxone has a half-life of 1-2 hours when given by injection and 10 Page 2 of 38 hours when given orally. Although naloxone is well absorbed via the oral route, it undergoes rapid first-pass metabolism. IVI administration reverses opioid toxicity in 1-3 minutes. Naloxone is also available within the combination product Suboxone with the opioid medication buprenorphine. Suboxone is used for the maintenance treatment of opioid dependence and addiction. When taken orally, naloxone has no pharmacological effect and does not reduce the effectiveness of the opioid effect of buprenorphine. The primary purpose of including naloxone within Suboxone is to act as a deterrent to injection, as injected naloxone would rapidly reverse the effects of buprenorphine. https://go.drugbank.com/drugs/DB01183

questions.explanation: In acute PE, a clot occludes pulmonary arteries, reducing perfusion to ventilated alveoli, resulting in a high ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) ratio or dead space. Simultaneously, blood is redirected to areas with lower ventilation, creating low V/Q units. This mismatch leads to ineffective oxygen exchange, contributing to hypoxaemia. \xB7 A is incorrect: alveolar ventilation is usually preserved or increased due to hyperventilation. \xB7 B is incorrect: oxygen-carrying capacity is not directly affected in PE. \xB7 D is incorrect: a RIGHT to LEFT shunt can be sometimes observed when there is increased pulmonary vascular resistance and pulmonary arterial pressure, where a portion of the blood is diverted through an existing PFO/ASD, resulting in hypoxaemia.

questions.explanation: Huntington's disease is caused by an expansion of CAG trinucleotide repeats in the HTT gene. Anticipation is where the number of CAG repeats increases in successive generations, leading to earlier onset and more severe disease in children than their affected parent. Genetic Page 4 of 38 testing can be performed before symptoms, especially in those with a family history, however, it is offered with genetic counselling. Regarding the repeat count, if <26 the patient is normal with no risk of developing Huntington's Disease. 27-35 repeats are intermediate, which means there is no disease, but there is a risk of expansion. 36-39 repeats are reduced penetrance, and they may develop disease later in life. If there are 40 or more repeats, that is full penetrance, and the patient will does not confirm a diagnosis, and D is incorrect as fewer than 26 repeats is normal.

questions.stem:
Which of the following statements is TRUE regarding Buruli ulcer (Mycobacterium
ulcerans) infection?

options.text: Disease is restricted to tropical areas of Australia only
Diagnosis is best made by culture of a swab or tissue biopsy
Treatment requires surgical management in all cases
Paradoxical reactions can complicate therapy in a significant proportion of patients

questions.explanation: Mycobacterium ulcerans causes Buruli ulcer (also known as Bairnsdale ulcer) and in Australia cases have traditionally occurred in the Northern Territory, Far North Queensland and Victoria around the Bellarine and Mornington Peninsulas. More recently since 2024, cases have been detected in New South Wales. The disease is mosquito-borne and the ring-tailed possums are a reservoir for infection. A swab of the undermined edge of the ulcer or a tissue biopsy can be submitted for dedicated PCR or mycobacterial culture; the former is more sensitive. Treatment generally consists of a combination of rifampicin and either clarithromycin, moxifloxacin or ciprofloxacin for at least 8 weeks, though surgical debridement or excision may occasionally be required. Page 5 of 38 Paradoxical reactions may occur (approximately 40% of children, 20% of adults), requiring concurrent corticosteroids.

questions.stem: Which result is most helpful in ruling out a diagnosis of asthma?

options.text: An FEV1/FVC ratio >70% with no evidence of bronchodilator reversibility
A fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) level of <25ppb
A normal peripheral blood eosinophil count and total IgE level

questions.explanation: A methacholine challenge test is a direct bronchoprovocation test with a high sensitivity. Therefore, a negative test is useful in ruling out asthma, but a positive test needs to be interpreted with caution. In contrast a mannitol challenge test is an indirect provocation test and has high specificity but lower sensitivity, therefore a negative test does not rule out asthma, but a positive one is helpful to rule it in. Normal spirometry without bronchodilator reversibility does not rule out asthma, which is a clinical diagnosis. Fractional exhaled nitric oxide can be useful in confirming a diagnosis of asthma in the appropriate clinical context. Levels > 50ppb are considered elevated, consistent with eosinophilic inflammation. Levels between 25-50ppb are intermediate and levels <25ppb are not consistent with uncontrolled eosinophilic inflammation. Levels can be suppressed by inhaled corticosteroid and FeNO can be a useful marker of compliance with inhalers. A normal FeNO does not exclude asthma. While peripheral blood eosinophilia, total IgE and skin prick testing can be useful tests to assess for atopy and/or eosinophilic inflammation, they are not useful in ruling in or out a diagnosis of asthma.

questions.explanation: Sotorasib is an oral tyrosine-kinase inhibitor directed at KRAS G12C mutations in NSCLC. Hepatic impairment is a known adverse effect which requires monitoring with liver function tests. Pneumonitis is also a significant adverse effect, particularly for patients with prior immunotherapy or radiotherapy treatment. Prompt cessation of treatment and early evaluation is required for any new respiratory symptoms. Peripheral oedema is also a commonly seen side effect (1- 10% of patients) and may manifest as localised oedema or periorbital oedema. Whilst sotorasib has drug interactions via the CYP3A4 and P- glycoprotein pathways, it has no recognised effect on QTc intervals.

At the same time, these agents increase glucagon secretion, resulting in a high glucagon-to-insulin ratio, which promotes lipolysis and hepatic ketogenesis--leading to euglycaemic DKA. \xB7 A is incorrect because SGLT2 inhibitors reduce glucose levels through urinary excretion, not increased insulin. Gastric pepsinogen secretion \xAD Important early on, but not the most critical limiting step. \xB7 C. Pancreatic protease secretion \xAD Most important physiological step. Without pancreatic proteases, B12 cannot be released from haptocorrin and therefore cannot bind intrinsic factor -- leading to malabsorption (e.g., in pancreatic insufficiency). \xB7 D. Transcobalamin-1 binding \xAD This occurs but is not essential for absorption (it protects B12 in the stomach). It's transcobalamin II, not I, that transports B12 systemically.

questions.explanation: Sotatercept is the newest agent in the management of PAH which acts on a different pathway to previously approved medications. Normally activins, in addition to, growth differentiating factor and other transforming growth factor (TGF)-beta ligands bind to activin receptor type IIa, which activates SMAD 2/3 signalling and downstream vascular proliferation. Sotatercept is a homodimeric recombinant fusion protein that acts as an activin signalling inhibitor. Its structure consists of a modified extracellular human activin receptor type IIa linked to a human IgG1 Fc domain (ActRIIa-Fc). Sotatercept traps activin A and GDF Page 12 of 38 ligands, thereby recalibrating TGF-beta proliferative and antiproliferative pathways, blunting vascular remodelling. The other three pathways which can be targeted pharmacologically in PAH include: (1) activation of the prostacyclin pathway using prostacyclin analogs or selexipag, (2) stimulation of the nitric oxide pathway by activating sGC with riociguat or using PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil), and (3) blunting the effects of endothelin with endothelin receptor antagonists (ambrisentan, bosentan, macitentan). C describes the mechanism of action of selexipag, which is the only oral prostacyclin agent available for PAH. A and D are incorrect Reference: Miranda AC, Cornelio CK, Tran BAC, Fernandez J. Sotatercept: A First-In-Class Activin Signaling Inhibitor for Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension. J Pharm Technol. 2025 Feb 22:87551225251317957. doi: 10.1177/87551225251317957. Epub ahead of print. PMID: 39995630; PMCID: PMC11847314.
