questions.explanation: Limited scleroderma is associated with pulmonary hypertension whilst systemic sclerosis is associated with ILD. Common antibodies in systemic sclerosis include Scl70/Topoisomerase I and RNA polymerase III. RNA polymerase III is associated with severe renal and skin disease whilst Scl70/topoisomerase I are associated with ILD.

Pairwise comparison of each participant using a pre-determined set of hierarchical outcomes, to allow tallying up of the ratio of `wins' and `losses' is known as win ratio analysis. This differs from a composite endpoint analysis, where each endpoint is combined and assessed defined as occurrence of death, hospitalisation or need for a specific higher-level therapy). This endpoint is then assessed in aggregate, whereas the win ratio analysis conducts a pair-wise comparison of only Page 13 of 38 the first outcome, and if there is a tie, progressing to the second or lower outcomes as needed. This allows for the definition of a more clinically important hierarchy of outcomes. Hierarchical cluster analysis is a method of cluster analysis to group a set of objects or samples together and is used for exploratory data analysis rather than for direct comparison of known groups. Stepped-wedge design is an alternative form of RCT, where each participant starts off in the control condition but over time is switched to the intervention, with randomisation occurring for the timing of when each participant (or cluster of participants) is switched over to the intervention. DOI: 10.1056/EVIDstat2500187
questions.stem:

arm is compared to each participant in the placebo arm, and the outcomes are

example, overall survival), with comparison of the secondary or tertiary outcome only
occurring in each pair if there was no obvious participant with a superior primary

questions.explanation: Osteoporosis, or osteopenia, is a chronic musculoskeletal disorder characterised by low bone mineral density (BMD) and microarchitectural deterioration, resulting in skeletal fragility and increased risk of fragility/minimal trauma fractures (spontaneous fractures or those resulting from impact equal to or less than a fall from standing height). The World Health Organization (WHO) has defined osteoporosis and osteopenia on the basis of T-score. Osteoporosis prevention, diagnosis and management in postmenopausal women and men over 50 years of age, 2nd edition: www.racgp.org.au Page 14 of 38 BMD is usually reported as a T-score, the number of standard deviations (SDs) of the BMD measurement above or below that of young healthy adults of the same sex. However, BMD is only one of several factors that contribute to an individual's risk of fracture. Approximately 50% of first or subsequent minimal trauma fractures occur in people who have T-scores in the normal or osteopenic range. Osteoporosis is a silent disorder until fractures occur. Although lower T- scores are associated with higher risk of fracture, most fractures at a population level occur in people with BMD T-scores above \xAD2.5. Osteoporosis is known as a silent disease because the deterioration of skeletal tissue proceeds with no outward symptoms until a symptomatic fracture occurs, and thus the condition is under-recognised and affected individuals are under-treated. Vertebral fractures may cause no recognisable symptoms for the patient or may present with an acute self-limiting episode of back pain. However, subclinical fractures are important predictors of future fracture risk. More commonly, vertebral fractures are associated with gradual height loss with increasing thoracic kyphosis and back pain. Non-vertebral or peripheral fractures usually present with more obvious fracture symptoms following a fall, although stress fractures may present as acute regional musculoskeletal pain. Reference: Kumar, Diamond; Osteoporosis a practical update on its management. Medicine Today 2022; 23(10). 36-48

options.text: WHO defines osteoporosis as a T-score of < -2.5SD as measured on a DXA BMD scan, that is, bone mineral density that is 2.5 standard deviations or more below the mean of a healthy young adult
Most osteoporotic fractures occur in people with a T-score of < -2.5SD
Prior fragility fracture is a strong predictor of future fractures
Osteoporosis is typically a silent disease

Treatment is generally recommended even in asymptomatic individuals, as untreated AIH can progress. TPMT activity should be checked before azathioprine is Page 15 of 38 started, but azathioprine is safe when TPMT is normal. Liver biopsy is part of the standard diagnostic approach, not limited to seronegative patients.

questions.stem:
Which of the following is most accurate regarding the initial management of
autoimmune hepatitis (AIH) in adults?

A 35-year-old man presents with progressive muscle weakness, fatigue, weight loss,
and hyperpigmentation over the past 6 months. Blood pressure is 100/60mmHg with a
postural drop of 20mmHg systolic. Laboratory studies demonstrate sodium 130 mmol/L,
potassium 6.2 mmol/L, glucose 3.2 mmol/L, markedly elevated plasma ACTH, and low
serum cortisol. A cosyntropin stimulation test shows no increase in cortisol after 60
minutes. Autoantibodies to 21-hydroxylase are positive. MRI of the pituitary gland is
normal.

Which best describes the mechanism leading to electrolyte abnormalities observed in
this patient?
questions.explanation: Autoimmune destruction of the adrenal cortex causes deficiency of both aldosterone and cortisol. Aldosterone loss leads to sodium wasting and potassium retention, while cortisol deficiency explains hypoglycemia and increased ACTH (causing hyperpigmentation). Why the others are incorrect: \xB7 B: Secondary adrenal insufficiency affects cortisol only; aldosterone is normal, so hyperkalemia and hyperpigmentation don't occur. \xB7 C: Mineralocorticoid excess causes hypertension and hypokalemia--not seen here. \xB7 D: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia presents in childhood and is genetic; positive 21-hydroxylase autoantibodies indicate autoimmune, not CAH

options.text: Autoimmune destruction of zona glomerulosa and zona fasciculata resulting in aldosterone and cortisol deficiency leading to sodium loss and potassium retention
Secondary adrenal insufficiency due to pituitary dysfunction causing isolated cortisol deficiency
Excessive secretion of mineralocorticoids causing renal potassium wasting and sodium retention
Congenital adrenal hyperplasia leading to cortisol deficiency and compensatory ACTH excess

atorvastatin, metoprolol, and lisinopril. Despite adherence, her LDL cholesterol remains

What is the next best step to further reduce her cardiovascular risk?

options.text: Add ezetimibe to her current statin therapy

questions.explanation: Recent large trials (e.g., IMPROVE-IT) support adding ezetimibe to statin therapy when LDL remains above target in secondary prevention to achieve further LDL lowering and reduce cardiovascular events. PCSK9 inhibitors (C) are highly effective but generally reserved for patients who do not achieve LDL goals with maximally tolerated statins plus ezetimibe due to cost and access considerations. Switching to fibrates (B) is not recommended for LDL lowering or cardiovascular risk reduction in this context. Page 18 of 38 Omega-3 fatty acid supplements (D) have limited evidence for routine use in secondary prevention.

questions.stem:
A 50-year-old man develops fever, widespread rash, facial swelling, eosinophilia, and
elevated liver enzymes three weeks after starting several new medications including
allopurinol, amoxicillin, and a statin.
What is the best step to determine the culprit agent?
questions.explanation: In DRESS syndrome, the most reliable and safest initial step to identify the culprit drug is a careful and detailed drug timeline and history, correlating the onset of symptoms with medication start dates, because DRESS typically develops 2-6 weeks after drug initiation and there are some medications which are more likely to be implicated than others. Skin prick testing and specific IgE are useful mainly for IgE-mediated (type I hypersensitivity) reactions, which are not the primary mechanism in DRESS (a T cell mediated reaction, Type IV). Drug rechallenge is generally contraindicated due to risk of severe reaction and should only be done under specialist supervision, if at all.

options.text: Drug rechallenge Page 24 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions

questions.explanation: Short acting agents such as IV hydralazine, IV labetalol, oral immediate release (IR) nifedipine or IV diazoxide should be used in managing acute (severe) hypertension. The choice of short acting antihypertensive should be based on the unit's access and familiarity with agent of choice. IV Magnesium sulphate is not an antihypertensive and is used for foetal neuroprotection in maternal hypertensive crisis. Topical Nitro-glycerine patch and oral enalapril are not recommended for first line management of pre-eclampsia. Reference: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline 2023

A 26-year-old woman, currently 30 weeks pregnant presents with headache and is

What antihypertensive medication would you initiate first?

questions.explanation: Cocaine-associated chest pain and cocaine-induced myocardial infarction are treated identically to non\xADcocaine-associated acute coronary syndromes with one important exception: beta-blockers are contraindicated. The use of cocaine leads to increased neurotransmitter release into the neuronal synapse. It also inhibits the reuptake of norepinephrine. This leads to increased adrenergic tone causing tachycardia, hypertension, and hyperthermia. Cocaine has been shown to both accelerate atherosclerosis and cause Page 19 of 38 vasoconstriction of the coronary arteries. Use of beta-blockers in the setting of cocaine use can lead to unopposed alpha-agonism, which can increase the toxic effects by worsening tachycardia, hypertension, and vasoconstriction.

questions.explanation: Malignancy can be associated with PLA2R membranous GN, however positivity suggests a lower incidence compared to seronegative patients with the exception of anti-THS7DA associated membranous which is commonly associated with an underlying malignancy. Glucocorticoids are ineffective as a single agent for this condition. membranous glomerulonephritis. His anti-PLA2R antibody is strongly positive.

options.text: Sertraline Page 27 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions

questions.explanation: The clinical features described are consistent with the cholinergic toxidrome, which is caused by acetylcholinesterase inhibitors such as organophosphates, physostigmine or rivastigmine, and sarin or VX, and Page 21 of 38 cholinergic receptor agonists such as bethanechol, nicotine, and pilocarpine. Amphetamine and other stimulants (e.g. adrenaline, noradrenaline, cocaine) cause the sympathomimetic toxidrome, which is characterised by agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, tachypnoea, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, and dilated pupils. Sertraline overdose causes serotonin syndrome, which is characterised by agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, tachypnoea, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, and dilated pupils, as well as hyperreflexia, hypertonia, and myoclonus. Psilocybin is a prodrug of psilocin, a tryptamine alkaloid and 5-HT2A receptor agonist. Overdose causes hallucinations and serotonin syndrome.

elective intravenous 1.5g iron infusion. She has Crohn's disease treated with infliximab
and end-stage kidney disease on maintenance dialysis. Other medicines include

options.text: Missed dialysis causing hyperuraemia and hypercalcaemia
Excessive dialysis causing hypophosphataemia and hypokalaemia
Fishbane reaction to ferric carboxymaltose Page 33 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions

questions.explanation: Ferric carboxymaltose and polymaltose can both cause significant and refractory hypophosphataemia. This lady likely has ESKD mineral bone disease on phosphate binders, so this is a risk factor for her to experience more severe hypophosphataemia. Missed dialysis will cause hyperphosphataemia with hypocalcaemia. Excessive dialysis is Page 24 of 38 more likely to cause dehydration, muscle cramps, and could be associated with hypokalaemia and hypophosphataemia. Fishbane reaction to iron infusion is a rare (1% of population) immediate reaction (not allergic) causing widespread erythema to face and body during or immediately after iron infusion. It is also associated with myalgia and arthralgias. Symptoms resolve within minutes of cessation of iron infusion, and do not recur with re-infusion.

The clinical picture is consistent with a venous ulcer, typically found on the medial gaiter region of the leg, often surrounded by signs of chronic venous hypertension (e.g. hemosiderin staining, oedema, lipodermatosclerosis). These ulcers are caused by venous valve dysfunction, leading to elevated venous pressure, capillary leakage, and poor wound healing. \xB7 A. Microvascular thrombosis (e.g. in antiphospholipid syndrome) more often causes livedo reticularis or digital ischemia, not typical venous ulcers. \xB7 B. Arterial ulcers occur over pressure points (e.g. toes, heels), are painful, and have punched-out appearance with poor pulses. \xB7 D. Pressure ulcers occur over bony prominences (e.g. sacrum, heels) in immobilised patients and are unrelated to venous pathology.

Elevated venous hydrostatic pressure due to valvular incompetence

questions.stem:
A 68-year-old woman with a history of chronic venous insufficiency presents with a
shallow ulcer over the medial aspect of her lower leg just above the ankle. The ulcer
has irregular borders, a granulating base, and is surrounded by hyperpigmented,
oedematous skin. She denies claudication or rest pain. Peripheral pulses are palpable.
What is the most likely underlying pathophysiological mechanism?
Which is NOT a feature of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) on imaging?
questions.explanation: IPF is characterised by a UIP (usual interstitial pattern) on imaging which consists of basal predominance, traction bronchiectasis, honeycombing, subpleural reticulations and the absence of inconsistent features such as (ground-glass opacities, profuse micronodules, or a peribronchovascular distribution).

options.text: Traction bronchiectasis Page 34 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions

questions.explanation: Why A is correct: A mid-shaft humeral fracture commonly injures the radial nerve where it runs in the spiral groove. Injury here produces Page 25 of 38 weakness of wrist and finger extensors (wrist drop) plus sensory loss over the dorsolateral hand (radial superficial branch territory). The normal elbow/triceps function fits a lesion distal to the triceps motor branches. Why B is wrong (PIN palsy): The posterior interosseous nerve is a motor branch of the radial nerve. PIN palsy causes weakness of finger and thumb extension but usually spares cutaneous sensation (PIN is motor only) and often preserves significant wrist extension because extensor carpi longus (innervated proximal to the division) may still function. Why C is wrong (ulnar nerve palsy): Ulnar neuropathy produces sensory loss of the little finger and ulnar half of the ring finger and weakness of intrinsic hand muscles (finger abduction/adduction, clawing), not a classic wrist drop with dorsolateral sensory loss. Why D is wrong (carpal tunnel/median nerve compression): Carpal tunnel syndrome causes numbness in the palmar radial 3\xBD digits, thenar weakness/atrophy and problems with thumb opposition -- it does not cause wrist drop or sensory loss on the dorsal radial hand.

options.text: Radial nerve palsy at the spiral (radial) groove

questions.explanation: The clinical vignette is most in keeping with acute SLE: Malar rash and pleurisy (pleuritis and pericarditis). Therefore, we would expect her complements to be low, particularly C3, positive DsDNA (which is also a marker of disease activity), and ECG showing pericarditis Page 26 of 38 (widespread PR depression + ST elevation). Other findings consistent with SLE would be positive ANA, Anti-Ro/La, elevated ESR (more than CRP elevation due to chronic inflammatory process), and perhaps evidence of renal involvement. but the ECG findings describe AMI which, though possible, is less likely compared with pericarditis, in this scenario. however the clinical vignette did not mention small joint symmetric arthropathy hypereosinophilia, dysmorphic RBC and casts in urine would be characteristic of EGPA (Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis) with renal involvement.

questions.explanation: This patient has a positive screening ARR on a background of mild hypertension on a single agent. However, beta-blockers can suppress renin levels and potentially spuriously increase the ARR. The first step in the diagnostic workup of primary aldosteronism is to ensure the ARR is accurate, which often requires stopping interfering medications. \xB7 The ARR should be interpreted off interfering drugs (e.g. calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, diuretics, ACEi/ARB - duration of cessation 2-4 weeks depending on specific medication). \xB7 If repeat ARR off interfering medications remained elevated, saline suppression test (D) would be appropriate to confirm primary aldosteronism. \xB7 CT adrenal glands (A) help identify structural causes but are not done before biochemical confirmation. Adrenal incidentolomas are common can may confuse the picture \xB7 Adrenal venous sampling (B) is used after confirmation of primary aldosteronism to determine if excess aldosterone production lateralises (in certain circumstances [i.e. young age <35 years, presence of adrenal adenoma], AVS may be skipped and progress straight to surgery.

questions.explanation: Serotonin syndrome typically has clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia, and rapid onset after serotonergic excess; NMS evolves over days with lead-pipe rigidity, bradykinesia, elevated CK and autonomic instability after dopamine blockade.

questions.stem:
A 56-year-old male with a recent flare of gout presents with diarrhoea and lethargy. On
his medication list you notice colchicine 0.5mg daily, allopurinol 100mg daily, metformin
XR 500mg daily, perindopril 5mg daily and aspirin 100mg daily. On examination, he has
no evidence of a gout flare, but you notice multiple mouth ulcers and he is clinically
hypovolaemic. His investigations reveal an eGFR of 10ml/min/1.73m2 and a white cell
count of 1.0 x109/L (normal 6-11 x109/L).
What is the most important first step?
questions.explanation: This is a patient with colchicine toxicity marked by the leukopenia and clinical evidence of mouth ulcers (suggestive of neutropenia). Page 28 of 38 Furthermore, diarrhoea is a common side effect of colchicine. The other clues that explain why he likely has gotten colchicine toxicity are the higher than recommended dose of colchicine (0.5mg used instead 0.25mg daily) and a low renal function (eGFR of 10). Therefore, stopping colchicine is the most important first step. Intravenous fluid resuscitation should also be commenced at the same time as stopping colchicine. There is no indication to start dialysis or administer antibiotics.

What would be the next best agent to add to this patient's regimen?
questions.explanation: In a woman of childbearing age, we would avoid Methotrexate (option A) and Mycophenolate (option B) as these are contraindicated in pregnancy. Cyclosporine is typically used in lupus nephritis which is not present for this patient. Azathioprine is the preferred agent in this scenario as it is safe in pregnancy.

questions.explanation: \xB7 A. Fried Frailty score- assess 5 domains: Weight loss (4.5kg in last year), Exhaustion, Physical activity, Walking speed, Muscle strength (measured by grip strength). Whilst validated in outpatient settings it requires special equipment and assessor training. \xB7 B. Frailty index-best tool for inpatients-total number of deficits out of a list of 40 \xB7 C. The Edmonton frail scale assesses level of frailty based on 9 components: Cognition, Functional independence, General health, Social support, Medication, Nutrition, Mood, Continence, Functional performance. It is validated for inpatient/outpatient settings. \xB7 D. Timed get up and go test. Doesn't give information on frailty, only on risk of falling Page 29 of 38 Reference: Dolenc E, Rotar-Pavlic D. Frailty Assessment Scales for the Elderly and their Application in Primary Care: A Systematic Literature Review. Zdr Varst. 2019 Mar 26;58(2):91-100.

performing daily activities. He reports a history of unintentional weight loss over the past

What is the most appropriate frailty scoring tool to use in this clinical scenario?

A 55-year-old man presents 12 months after sleeve gastrectomy with a symmetric distal
"glove-and-stocking" peripheral neuropathy. He has achieved appropriate weight loss
and his HbA1c remains 6.5% on metformin and an SGLT2 inhibitor. He adheres to
lifestyle and behavioural measures and to his prescribed medications and supplements
(daily multivitamin, B-complex, folate, protein supplementation). He also frequently
consumes energy drinks and coffee.
Which is the most likely contributor to his peripheral neuropathy?
questions.explanation: \xB7 Excess pyridoxine (vitamin B6) can cause a length-dependent sensory axonal neuropathy. Post-bariatric patients are at higher risk due to overlapping supplementation (multivitamins, B- complex, some protein powders); energy drinks may also contain vitamin B6. \xB7 Vitamin B12 toxicity is not a recognised cause of peripheral neuropathy (in contrast to B12 deficiency). Reported features of B12 excess include flushing/erythema, palpitations, nausea, vomiting and diarrhoea. \xB7 Diabetic neuropathy is possible but less likely here given sustained glycaemic control (HbA1c ~6.5%) and the timing relative to surgery/supplementation. \xB7 Folate excess is not typically associated with a length-dependent neuropathy; adverse effects are more often gastrointestinal or non-neuropathic.

options.text: Inadequate absorption of the erythropoietin stimulating agent
The development of anti-erythropoietin antibodies Page 40 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions

questions.explanation: Inflammation from chronic disease can lead to a functional iron deficiency in CKD patients. Due to the limited mobilisation of iron from its stores, the bone marrow will be limited in its production of red blood cells, despite the increased stimulation from ESAs. There should be no impairment in the absorption of ESAs when given intravenously Hyperparathyroidism can cause bone marrow fibrosis, but this is a less likely reason for an inadequate response compared to iron deficiency Anti-erythropoietin antibodies are rare. questions.explanation: This question examines duration of therapy for MSSA bacteraemia in the absence of metastatic sites of infection. The duration of therapy in this case is guided by whether the infection is complicated or uncomplicated. eTG sets out the criteria that would define a SAB episode as complicated, including: positive BC >48 hours after appropriate Abx, a fever >72 hours after appropriate Abx, abnormal valves/regurgitation on TTE/TOE, unclear initial source or evidence of metastatic infection. These are not present here. HOWEVER, the final criteria for complicated infection is if intravascular prosthetic material is present, which it is in this case (PPM). Therefore the infection is classified as complicated and at least 4 weeks of Rx is needed despite clinical/microbiological/biochemical response was slow, but it was clearly not from the stem. A. and D. are options for uncomplicated disease only.

questions.stem:
An 80-year-old man develops hospital-acquired methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus
aureus bacteraemia (SAB) as a result of a peripheral intravenous cannula (PIVC)
infection. His medical history includes atrial fibrillation with a permanent pacemaker
(PPM) in-situ, ischaemic heart disease, hypertension and obesity (BMI 35). The PIVC is
removed and IV cefazolin 2g TDS is commenced. Within 48 hours he is afebrile and
subsequent blood cultures following removal of the PIVC are negative. A trans-
oesophageal echocardiogram reveals no valvular abnormalities or signs of
endovascular infection. There is no evidence of metastatic infection clinically.

Timed from the first negative blood culture, what is the most appropriate duration and
administration of antibiotics:
What is the most common cardiac arrhythmia in digoxin poisoning?

options.text: Complete heart block Page 41 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions

questions.explanation: Digoxin toxicity affects the heart by increasing vagal tone and enhanced vagal tone leads to bradyarrhythmias, particularly sinus bradycardia and AV nodal block, which are very common early features. \xB7 Bradycardia is the most common arrhythmia in mild to moderate digoxin toxicity. \xB7 It reflects increased parasympathetic (vagal) activity on the SA and AV nodes. \xB7 Other ECG findings include prolonged PR interval, PVCs, and various degrees of AV block -- but sinus bradycardia is usually the earliest and most frequent. Ventricular tachycardia and complete heart block can occur with digoxin toxicity but are much less common than bradycardia Page 31 of 38 \xB7 Digoxin toxicity can cause VT, particularly in severe poisoning. It is often due to increased automaticity and triggered activity (e.g. delayed afterdepolarisations). It typically occurs in the context of high levels of toxicity, especially if hypokalaemia is also present. \xB7 Complete heart block can occur in severe digoxin poisoning, particularly with high-grade AV nodal blockade. Would usually be preceded by lesser AV block or bradycardia.

questions.stem:
A 75-year-old woman presents to the Emergency department with a 3-day history of
worsening headaches and a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home earlier
that day. She has a history of mild Alzheimer's disease, currently receiving monthly
infusions of Donanemab, an anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody, for the past 3 months
and she is a carrier of the Apolipoprotein E (APOE) 4 gene. On examination, she has
bilateral papilloedema.

Which of the following would be the LEAST appropriate initial management?
questions.explanation: This represents a case of severe amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA). They take the form of oedema (ARIA-E) and haemorrhage (ARIA-H). Many cases occur within the first 3 to 6 months of treatment and APOE 4 carriers have an increased risk. APOE 4 homozygotes are excluded from treatment in Australia. She has signs of increased intracranial pressure and treatment with intravenous corticosteroids would be appropriate. Thrombolytics are contraindicated due to the high risk of intracerebral haemorrhage. Other management includes discontinuation of anti-amyloid therapy and antiepileptic treatment if seizures occur.

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old man who had recently emigrated from China presented to the emergency
department after 4 generalized tonic-clonic seizures in the preceding 48 hours. He has
a background of hypertension and atrial fibrillation, and is on perindopril, amlodipine and
dabigatran.
Which anti-seizure medication would be most appropriate to initially commence?
questions.explanation: Carbamazepine is contraindicated in persons of Asian ancestry due to the increased risk for Steven's Johnson Syndrome unless they have had HLA testing performed to exclude at risk alleles e.g. HLA-B15:02. Phenytoin will potentially cause interactions with his dabigatran (reducing its bioavailability and therefore efficacy) and is not an ideal drug to use long term due to potential side effects if more suitable alternatives are available. In addition, phenytoin also poses a risk of Steven's Johnson Syndrome, which is elevated in people of some Asian backgrounds. Lamotrigine must be introduced slowly over many weeks to reduce to the risk of drug reactions, which is not ideal in a patient with an acute presentation of multiple seizures in whom more rapid up-titration to a therapeutic antiseizure dose is needed. Whilst potential drug interactions between sodium valproate and DOAC Page 32 of 38 therapy would need to be considered, it is the most appropriate initial therapy in this scenario.

options.text: Lamotrigine Page 42 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions

A 66-year-old female has been diagnosed with metastatic hormone positive breast
cancer with bony metastasis.
What is the most appropriate management plan?
questions.explanation: CDK4/6 inhibitors combined with endocrine therapy are the standard- of-care for ER-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer, with improved progression-free survival and OS and a good toxicity profile efficacy versus chemotherapy and is associated with less toxicity, making it the preferred treatment unless a patient has imminent organ failure.

It is associated with nosocomial infection, has a high rate of fluconazole +/- echinocandin resistance and is difficult to clear from the hospital environment so poses a significant infection control concern. It is public health notifiable.

This patient's clinical presentation is that of medial compartment osteoarthritis. The x-ray shows chondrocalcinosis which can co-exist with OA or associate with acute pseudogout or chronic calcium pyrophosphate (CPP) inflammatory arthritis. In this case, neither the aspirate or clinical presentation is inflammatory and hence the CPP and urate crystals are incidentals. creatinine 185\xB5mol/L, uric acid is 0.69 mmol/L (NR < 0.43 mmol/L) and C-reactive
protein 8 mg/L. You aspirate 2mL of viscous, slightly blood tinged fluid. It is reported as
having 200 x 106 white cells/L with urate crystals and calcium pyrophosphate crystals
present. Her knee X-Ray is shown below:

What is the most appropriate advice for this patient?

questions.explanation: This young female patient presents with a mucocutaneous pattern of bleeding. Haemophilia A and B are very rare in women (given X-linked recessive inheritance) and moreover the bleeding pattern in haemophilia patients is that of parenchymal bleeding (joints, muscle, ICH). Type 1 vWD is the most common bleeding disorder and given its Page 33 of 38 autosomal dominant inheritance, it affects males and females equally. vWD presents with a mucocutaneous bleeding phenotype like is displayed in this patient. vWF protects factor VIII in the circulation and therefore patients with mild quantitative deficiency of vWF will often have a mild quantitative deficiency in FVIII which often results in a mildly prolonged APTT as is seen in this case. BSS is a rare platelet function disorder that is typically associated with thrombocytopenia, giant platelets on blood film and a normal APTT.

A 17-year-old female presents with menorrhagia since menarche and easy bruising.
Pathology results are as follows: APTT 40s (25-35). PT 13s (11-15). Platelet count 300
x 109/L (150-400). Platelet morphology on blood film was normal.
What is the most likely diagnosis?

questions.explanation: The splenic macrophages play an important role in opsonisation of encapsulated bacteria and thus in patients post-splenectomy, they are at increased risk of overwhelming infection. The most common associated pathogen is Streptococcus pneumoniae, but risk of infections with other pathogens are also increased post-splenectomy including Neisseria meningitidis, Haemophilus influenzae, Escherichia Coli, Salmonella, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Capnocytophaga canimorsus (dog/cat bites), and severe malaria. It is important to learn the post- splenectomy vaccination schedule required as well as the recommendation of at least 2-3 years of prophylactic Amoxycillin 250mg daily (lifelong if particularly elevated risk) and importance of patients having an emergency supply of 1g TDS to commence if they develop a fever. Live vaccines are not contraindicated in patients due to post-splenectomy status.

questions.explanation: This patient is HBsAg+, HBeAg+, has a high viral load (312,000 IU/mL) and is pregnant. Tenofovir is first-line and safe in pregnancy, preventing vertical transmission. Entecavir is contraindicated in pregnancy and termination not indicated.

options.text: Graves' Disease Page 46 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions

questions.explanation: A suppressed TSH level means any uptake activity is autonomous, and a uniform uptake pattern in an autonomous thyroid on nuclear medicine screening represents Graves' disease. Page 34 of 38 Subacute thyroiditis (A) can lead to a suppressed TSH level with elevated FT4 and FT3 levels but is often clinically accompanied with neck pain and/or a tender thyroid gland with a history of viral illness commonly precipitating it. A thyroid uptake scan in subacute thyroiditis often has low or no uptake. Subacute thyroiditis is usually a self-limiting illness. Hashimoto's thyroiditis often presents with an elevated TSH level, although early phases of this illness can begin with hyperthyroidism, leading to suppressed TSH levels, before progressing to overt hypothyroidism. A thyroid uptake scan in Hashimoto's thyroiditis often has low or no uptake. A toxic multinodular goitre is a condition where the thyroid gland is enlarged and contains multiple nodules that produce excess thyroid hormone, leading to hyperthyroidism, where TSH level can also be suppressed. However, a thyroid uptake scan in a toxic multinodular goitre will show heterogeneous uptake of radioactive iodine, with at least one nodule exhibiting increased uptake, indicating autonomous hormone production. This is in contrast to Graves' disease, where uptake is typically diffuse and high.

A 52-year-old woman with no active medical history complained of worsening hot
flushes and insomnia. Investigations revealed a TSH of <0.01 mIU/L (RR 0.4-3.5

options.text: Intravenous heparin with anti-Xa monitoring Page 47 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions

questions.explanation: She likely has a lupus anticoagulant given the clinical history of SLE and her raised APTT at baseline. Accordingly, Apixaban would not be appropriate. Clexane is not appropriate due to her elevated creatinine. Warfarin is not appropriate in the acute setting. Thus, IV heparin and anti-Xa monitoring is correct.

Instead vasodilator stress testing Page 35 of 38 should be used to cause hyperemia and flow disparity (dipyridamole, regadenoson and adenosine)

questions.stem:
A 72-year-old male with a history of hypertension presents to the emergency
department with central chest pain which resolved with GTN spray. He has no other
significant past medical history. His serial troponins were negative, and he is currently
pain free. His ECG is below:

An ECG last year demonstrated a left bundle branch block
What is the next best investigation to investigate his chest pain?

A 32-year-old man who has sex with men, presents with painful lesions over his perioral
area and hands as shown below. He reports fevers and malaise for 3 days prior to the
appearance of the lesions. He is haemodynamically stable and exhibits a temperature
of 37.8\xB0C. He is otherwise well with no significant medical history.

What is the most appropriate treatment for this patient?
questions.explanation: The lesions shown are characteristic of Mpox virus infection, with papules, which are often umbilicated, progressing to vesicles or pustules then ulcerating. A prodrome of fevers, malaise, headache, and/or lymphadenopathy often precedes the eruptive phase of the illness. The global pandemic Mpox virus since 2022 has predominantly affected men who have sex with men, transgender women, and sex workers. Fortunately, most cases of Mpox infection are mild and resolve spontaneously without antiviral therapy. Antiviral therapy with tecovirimat is only recommended for Mpox cases with complicated or severe disease or significant immunocompromise.

options.text: Valaciclovir Page 49 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions
Upper gastrointestinal bleeding secondary to erosion of a gastric ulcer
Microscopic haematuria secondary to nephritic syndrome

questions.explanation: AML and MDS are recognised haematological complications of PARP inhibitor therapy, with an incidence of approximately 1.5%, and are often fatal. Dyspepsia is a common side effect of PARP inhibitor therapy, but rarely severe enough to cause significant complications such as UGI bleeding. AIHA is not a recognised complication of PARP inhibitor therapy, noting Olaparib is a PARP inhibitor and not an immunotherapeutic agent. While increased creatinine is a recognised complication of Olaparib, nephritic syndrome is not.
questions.stem:
A 75-year-old woman is diagnosed with stage IV high grade serous carcinoma of the
ovary. She undergoes surgical debulking of the tumour, which is followed by 6 cycles of
adjuvant chemotherapy with carboplatin and paclitaxel. She is known to have a
germline pathogenic mutation of the BRCA1 gene. At the completion of chemotherapy,
she is commenced on olaparib 300mg twice daily. After 4 months of treatment, she
attends clinic for routine evaluation, and reports feeling fatigued and lightheaded. Blood
tests reveal a haemoglobin level of 56 g/L (compared to a pre-olaparib blood test which
revealed a normal haemoglobin level). She is promptly transfused with packed red
blood cells.

This presentation should prompt investigation and exclusion of which condition?

A 55-year-old woman presents to your clinic with a progressive history of unsteady gait,
frequent falls, sensory neuropathy and vertigo. She mentions a persistent, dry, chronic
cough. She reports no known family history of neurological disease, though her parents
are first cousins. Brain MRI reveals cerebellar atrophy. She has previously seen an
overseas geneticist who tested her for genetic causes of her symptoms with whole
exome sequencing which was negative.

Which genetic test is the most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Suspect CANVAS in patients aged ~50\xAD60 who present with cerebellar ataxia, sensory neuronopathy, vestibular areflexia, and chronic unexplained cough--especially in the setting of consanguinity. If exome sequencing is unrevealing, repeat-expansion testing of RFC1 is the appropriate next step, as CANVAS is a genetic disorder caused by biallelic intronic pentanucleotide (AAGGG) repeat expansions in the RFC1 gene. Chromosome microarray detects copy-number variants Page 36 of 38 Karyotype identifies large chromosomal rearrangements, not subtle intronic changes. Mitochondrial diseases typically present with multisystem involvement (e.g. ophthalmoplegia, cardiomyopathy), not the triad of ataxia + neuropathy + vestibulopathy + cough seen here.

options.text: Mitochondrial DNA testing Page 50 of 53 2025/26 NSW-ACT State BPT Trial Exam \xAD Paper Three - Questions
Glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD) F. Leucine-rich, glioma inactivated 1 (LGI1) G. Myelin-oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) H. N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor (NMDAR)

questions.explanation: Constellation of constitutional, respiratory, neurological symptoms with active urinary sediment suggests a small vessel vasculitis such as ANCA vasculitis. Whilst lupus nephritis may present with a similar clinical picture, the age of the patient is more suggestive of a new diagnosis of ANCA vasculitis (tends to occur older patients) compared to lupus nephritis (tends to occur younger patients). The normal range C3/C4 is also more consistent with ANCA vasculitis compared to lupus nephritis (which tends to result in complement consumption and thus low C3/C4). Page 37 of 38 specific viral like prodrome (respiratory or gastrointestinal symptoms) followed by psychiatric and cognitive issues (both positive and negative symptoms). There is a known association with ovarian teratomas, which can present on ultrasound as a complex cystic mass with features of fatty infiltration (echogenic areas without shadowing) and calcification (echogenic with shadowing).

(RR 0.16-0.48).

50-year-old male undergoing elective transurethral resection of the prostate

questions.explanation: Asymptomatic bacteriuria is common, with a prevalence of: -20-25% amongst post-menopausal women -5-15% amongst older men -2-7% amongst young women -1-3% amongst neonates, infants, children, and adolescents, and young men Antibiotic therapy is only recommended in patients with asymptomatic bacteriuria and 1 of the following: -Pregnancy (decreased pyelonephritis, pre-term delivery, and low-birth- weight risks) -Renal transplantation recently (3 months) (decreased renal allograft failure risk) -Urinary tract surgery planned (pre- or peri-operative) (decreased infectious complication risk) Antibiotic therapy is not recommended for other patients with asymptomatic bacteriuria because of the benign natural history, increased risk of adverse effects and antimicrobial resistance, and unchanged risk of urinary tract infection and other infections.

Findings meet diagnostic criteria for primary biliary cholangitis (cholestatic enzymes + AMA with no extrahepatic obstruction). UDCA 13­15 mg/kg/day is first-line therapy; OCA/fibrates are second-line in inadequate responders and OCA is contraindicated in advanced cirrhosis.
questions.stem:
A 54-year-old woman with fatigue and pruritus has ALP 3× ULN, GGT elevated, and
positive AMA. Ultrasound is normal.

What is the best initial treatment?

questions.explanation: At present, there are many pangenotypic treatments available for HCV. HCV treatment is based on an 8-12 week course of combination therapy of direct acting antivirals, using a combination of protease inhibitors, NS5A and NS5B inhibitors. Pangenotypic treatments are first line in Australia, so while the HCV genotype may be important in second line or complex therapy, it is less relevant. Protease inhibitors are contraindicated in patients with CKD stage IV or worse (therefore answer B is incorrect). A positive HCV IgG alone is not grounds for treatment as about 30% of people will spontaneously clear the virus, and previously treated patients will always have a positive IgG ­ therefore a positive viral load is required to confirm current infection (answer C is incorrect). Patients with decompensated disease are significantly limited in the choice of therapy and many (not all) DAAs are contraindicated due to the risk of further decompensation. An excellent summary of the current Australian guidelines is available at https://www.hepcguidelines.org.au/
questions.stem: A 45-year-old intravenous drug user is noted to have a positive Hepatitis C IgG. He has not previously been treated for Hepatitis C. Which of the following is the least important factor in determining the treatment strategy?

questions.explanation: In men, Sulfasalazine decreases sperm motility, density and can be associated with abnormal morphology. This is reversible - generally seen to normalise in 2 months.
questions.stem: A 28-year-old man has recently been diagnosed with ulcerative colitis. His past medical history includes depression and type I diabetes. He presents to clinic with his female partner to discuss the management and mentioned that the couple have been trying to conceive for some time and considering in vitro fertilization. Which of the following potential first line therapies may reduce male fertility?
A 24-year-old woman with multiple sclerosis is commenced on monoclonal antibody therapy by her neurologist. Which of the following medications is an Anti-CD52 Monoclonal antibody?
questions.explanation: Alemtuzumab is an anti-CD52 monoclonal antibody that causes complement-mediated lysis of circulating lymphocytes.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old lady presents for assessment. She has a 4-year-old daughter with bilateral renal cysts and hepatic fibrosis. Genetic testing identified biallelic pathogenic variants in PKHD1. At the time of diagnosis, both parents were confirmed to be carriers. The mother now has a new partner and wishes to quantify the chances of her and her partner having a child with the same condition as her daughter. The incidence of PKHD1-related autosomal recessive polycystic kidney disease in the population is 1 in 20,000. The chances (with each pregnancy) are closest to:
questions.explanation: We know mum is a carrier. For couple to have affected child, new partner would also need to be carrier Requires calculation of carrier frequency based on supplied incidence: p2 + 2pq + q2 = 1 and p + q = 1 q2 = 1/20000 q = 1/140 = 0.007 p = 1 ­ 0.007 =~1 2pq = 2 x 1 x 1/140 = 1/70 Carrier frequency is therefore 1 in 70 Chances of affected child each pregnancy = 1/70 x 1/4 = 1 in 280

This is hopefully quite straightforward. There are clinical clues that the patient is likely to be vitamin K deficient (probably not eating well, biliary obstruction limiting absorption, antibiotics limiting production). She needs vitamin K replacement. It may not be the entire answer. A prolonged PT with a normal Echis can be due to vitamin K deficiency/ antagonism OR an isolated factor 7 deficiency which can occur in early liver disease (because factor 7 has the shortest half-life and is the first factor depleted when the liver fails). In any case, unless the patient is supposed to be on an anticoagulant which I have told you that she is not, the most appropriate initial action is to give her some vitamin K. You'd want to assess her liver too but I didn't list that as an option.

options.text: Proceed to the procedure given the abnormalities are mild

questions.stem: A 75-year-old female with metastatic cholangiocarcinoma presents with cholangitis. She is treated with antibiotics. 7 days after admission, she is planned for an ERCP +/- stenting for biliary obstruction. She is on prophylactic clexane (last dose 24 hours ago). Her routine coagulation profile is as follows. What is the most appropriate initial intervention to optimise her coagulation for a procedure?

A 28-year-old male is brought to ED unresponsive on Monday afternoon, after being found on the floor by his female partner, who had been away for the weekend. Other than h/o substance abuse in the past, there was no significant medical history and he was not on any medications. Physical examination showed the patient had GCS 6/15 and was intubated and ventilated. Pulse was 115/min with BP of 110/70. There was no focal neurological deficit and the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Urine output was < 20 mls/2 hours. Investigations showed ­ FBC normal. S.Na+ - 152 mmol/L, S. K+ - 5.5 mmol/L, S.HCO3 ­ 15 mmol/L, Chloride ­ 110 mmol/L, S.creatinine ­ 250 micromole/L, S.cCa++ - 1.7 mmol/L; S.PO4- 1.9 mmol/L, LFTs ­ raised AST and ALT. Urine ­ Blood 4+, Prot 2+, reddish brown granular casts+; Urine toxicology screen positive for cocaine and opiates; Urine Na+ - 12 mmol/L; urine myoglobin screen ­ Positive What is the most important next step?

options.text: IV isotonic sodium bicarbonate in 5% dextrose

questions.explanation: The clinical picture is very much in keeping with substance abuse, prolonged immobilization from the unconscious state with rhabdomyolysis. The evidence for rhabdomyolysis comes from the acute renal failure, raised AST and ALT with positive urine myoglobin test and reddish-brown granular casts. This can be confirmed further by Serum Creatine Kinase levels which will be markedly elevated. The appropriate treatment for this is to replace the volume depletion with IV normal saline initially. Hemodialysis is indicated for patients who remain in severe AKI despite volume replacement with persistent hyperkalemia or metabolic acidosis or volume overload. Hypocalcemia is due to calcium sequestration in the damaged muscles. Intravenous Calcium should only be given for patients who are symptomatic or are at risk for arrythmias due to severe hyperkalemia with ECG changes. Although there is a theoretical advantage of alkalinizing urine to pH >6.5 to prevent intratubular pigment cast formation, reduce release of free iron from myoglobin or risk of tubular precipitation of uric acid, clear benefit is lacking. IV bicarbonate must be used only after diuresis is established with volume repletion Reference: Clinical manifestations and diagnosis of rhabdomyolysis, Prevention and treatment of heme pigment-induced acute kidney injury (including rhabdomyolysis) - UptoDate

options.text: Continue current therapy with regular cardiology follow-up and referral to obstetric medicine
Cease all current medications with regular cardiology follow-up and referral to obstetric medicine
Cease spironolactone, continue perindopril and bisoprolol at current doses
Cease spironolactone and perindopril, continue bisoprolol at current dose and consider adding hydralazine and a nitrate

questions.explanation: The question focuses on the use of medications during pregnancy. ACEI/ARB and spironolactone are contraindicated during pregnancy due to teratogenicity. Complete cessation of heart failure medications, particularly where LVEF is not normal pre-pregnancy would not be recommended due to risk and consequences of worsening heart failure closer to term. Beta blockers are recommended to continue with beta-1 selective agents preferred Hydralazine and nitrates, in combination, have trial evidence of survival benefit in non-pregnancy HF settings and are a relatively safe alternative to ACEI during pregnancy with decompensated heart failure. See: https://www.escardio.org/Guidelines/Cl inical-Practice-Gui deli nes/Cardiovascular-Diseases- duri ng- Pregnancy-Management-of
questions.stem: A 27-year-old female presents for urgent outpatient assessment at the request of her general practitioner (GP) for advice about her medications at 9 weeks gestation during her first pregnancy. She has a known history of a familial dilated cardiomyopathy. Her LV ejection fraction has improved from 35% at diagnosis 2 years ago to 45% on medical therapy. She is currently taking bisoprolol 2.5mg, perindopril 5mg and spironolactone 12.5mg. On examination today she is comfortable at rest with blood pressure of 130/60, heart rate is 64 bpm and she is clinically euvolaemic and in sinus rhythm. Haemoglobin, renal function, potassium and thyroid function tests are within the normal ranges. She is working in retail and walks to and from work each day with no shortness of breath or chest discomfort. You are happy with her clinical progress. How would you advise her GP regarding her medications?
Which is not a risk factor for progression of asbestosis?
questions.explanation: Asbestos related pleural plaques indicate asbestos exposure, but they do not predict risk of developing other asbestos related pulmonary conditions such as malignancy, cancer, or asbestosis.

options.text: Type of asbestos mineral exposure ­ crocidolite (blue) asbestos exposure as opposed to crysotile (white) asbestos exposure
Check FBC to assess for myelotoxicity
Arrange IV fluids and septic screen
Check cortisol levels and give IV hydrocortisone when awaiting results
Arrange short synacthen test next morning

questions.explanation: Ipilimumab has been well described to trigger autoimmune hypophysitis with a predominant effect on central ACTH /cortisol axis which can quickly snowball into a medical emergency. As the patient is clearly hypotensive, the appropriate answer would be to also give hydrocortisone after checking for cortisol levels. Ipilimumab is not a myelotoxic and IV fluids won't help by themselves; there is no evidence of sepsis on the provided information. SST can be performed anytime and arranging an elective test is not recommended as compared to option C.
questions.stem: A 70-year-old gentleman is on treatment with Ipilimumab for systemic metastases with melanoma. He has just completed his 3rd cycle of the medication 2 weeks ago and now presents to ED with fatigue, nausea and postural dizziness for the last 4 days. His wife reports he has been sleeping for long hours and has noticed a reduced appetite. His examination shows mildly hypovolemic status and BP of 90/58 mm Hg supine with an upright BP of 70/40 mm Hg. Which of the following is the next best step as the admitting medical registrar at 4pm in the afternoon?
All of the following drugs have shown benefit in treatment of thyroid eye disease except:
questions.explanation: Teprotumumab is an insulin-like growth factor-1 receptor inhibiting monoclonal antibody which has shown RCT evidence for TED. Tocilizumab is an IL-6 receptor antibody which has shown significant benefit for TED in small scale studies, both in IV and sub cut forms. Mycophenolate has shown evidence as a second line agent for TED, in concurrence or sequential therapy with IV methyl prednisolone which remains 1st line treatment. No evidence for Golimumab (no trials at this point).

questions.stem: A new gene therapy aims to insert a copy of the normal gene into the cells. Conditions with which of the following modes of inheritance are most likely to show improvement with this therapy?
questions.explanation: Question relies on an understanding of mutational mechanisms Gain of function Loss of function, haploinsufficiency If inserting a new (functional) copy of the gene, this implies the mechanism of disease is `loss of function' Many (not all) emerging CRISPR therapies target AR conditions (SMA, CFTR, LCA) AD/XLD conditions if gain of function mechanism, inserting new copy of gene won't work if haploinsufficiency/LoF, would help, but difficult to achieve complete haplosufficiency Polygenic: doesn't make sense, as due to combined effects of multiple genes

questions.stem: According to the 2017 McDonald criteria for RRMS, a patient can NOT be diagnosed if:
questions.explanation: Anti-MOG antibodies are not part of diagnostic criteria of MS. This is an independent illness (Neuromylitis Optica/Devic's disease).

options.text: They have 2 definite clinically evident relapses of MS
They have 1 definite clinical event and more than 2 demyelinating lesions on MRI brain/spine
They have unilateral optic neuritis and anti-MOG antibodies
They have no relapses, but progressive disability, demyelinating lesions on MRI brain/spine and CSF oligocloncal bands
They have unilateral clinical optic neuritis with demyelinating lesions on MRI spine

questions.explanation: Direct (head-to-head) comparisons are the most trustworthy means for determining efficacy so one would want to see indirect comparisons giving consistently similar results (although in most network meta- analysis there is a fair bit of inconsistency). You do not have to have more direct than indirect comparisons if the former involve reasonably large trials. While between-study differences in outcomes (homogeneity) and trial characteristics (transitivity) are preferably small, they are not as important as consistency in outcome results between direct and indirect comparisons. The level of precision around the pooled estimates for different treatments, as measured by the CrIs, often overlap but this does not prevent a valid ranking of treatments according to their level of comparative effectiveness.

options.text: There should be more direct (head-to-head) comparisons than indirect comparisons
Studies of the same treatment/comparator yielded similar outcome results
Studies of the same treatment/comparator were similar in regard to populations, study design or the way outcomes were measured
Direct and indirect comparisons of the same treatment/comparator demonstrated similar outcome results
The credible intervals around the pooled effect estimates of the different treatments do not overlap

questions.stem: In a network meta-analysis attempting to determine the most effective of 10 hypoglycaemic agents to add to first-line metformin in patients with type 2 diabetes, which of the following attributes of the meta- analysis is MOST IMPORTANT in assessing the validity of the results?

A randomised trial compared treatment A with treatment B in 10,000 patients and found the treatments to be equivalent in effect. The investigators also performed 20 pre-specified sub-group analyses and found that in patients who were older compared to those who were younger, the treatment was more effective (absolute risk reduction 3.1% vs 1.6%) with an unadjusted p-value for subgroup-treatment interaction equal to 0.01. Which one of the following statements MOST ACCURATELY summarises this finding?

options.text: The finding confirms a subgroup effect because the analysis was pre-specified, and the result is statistically significant
The finding suggests a benefit in older patients and warrants a separate randomised trial
The finding would remain statistically significant after adjustment for multiple analyses
The subgroup analysis was likely to have been underpowered in view of the number of subgroup analyses
The chance that a finding with a p value <0.05 would occur in 20 subgroup analyses is over 5%.

questions.explanation: In the setting of 20 subgroup analyses, the chance of finding a single analysis associated with a statistically significant p value is at least 5% and probably more. After correcting the level of statistical significance for multiple analyses (usually by applying the Bonferonni method which is p/number of analyses), this p-value of 0.01 is much higher than the threshold stipulated by this method (0.05/20 = 0.0025). The question of power is irrelevant to this unadjusted likely chance finding and going to the time and expense of a new randomised trial on the basis of this finding alone is not justified.

Propensity scores by their very nature will vary among individual patients, from 0 to 1. Any variable that may influence disease outcome, which includes the decision to give the patient CPAP as well as prognostic factors within that individual, must be included in the regression model used to generate the score. While a AUROC of 0.9 suggests a very discriminatory model, it does not ensure it can create evenly balanced groups. The key test of adequate adjustment is reducing standardised differences in all included variables to less than 10%.

options.text: The propensity score is the same for all patients
The propensity score has been derived from a regression model that only includes disease-related variables
The propensity score has been derived from a regression model that only includes variables that influence treatment selection
The propensity score has generated standardised differences in variables that are less than 10%
The propensity score has an area under the curve (AUROC) of more than 0.9 indicating excellent discrimination

questions.stem: An observational study of retrospective data is performed in determining if patients with obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) who receive CPAP therapy have fewer cardiovascular events than OSA patients who do not receive CPAP. The investigators use a propensity score to match the two groups in regard to baseline differences in factors that may influence disease outcomes. Which one of the following statements is MOST INDICATIVE of a score that can successfully adjust for these differences?

questions.explanation: Randomisation can be carried out using permuted blocks whereby, in small centres with few patients, the allocation sequence is designed so that even if only 8 patients are enrolled, when all are randomised, 4 will be allocated to treatment and 4 to control group.

options.text: Selection bias is minimised in allocating patients to either group
Unmeasured confounders are evenly balanced between the groups
The sequence of allocation often involves computer-generated random tables
Comparing the baseline patient characteristics of each group is a reliable test of successful randomisation
Randomisation is unlikely to assign participants evenly to treatment or control groups in trial centres that enrol very few patients

questions.stem: In a clinical trial comparing a new treatment with an old treatment, which one of the following statements is FALSE in regard to the process of randomisation?

questions.explanation: Biotin is known to be present in hair /nail supplements in higher doses and well-known interference with TFTs.
questions.stem: A 35-year-old female has blood tests done for long-term fatigue including thyroid function testing. She has no past medical history but is taking a Vitamin B complex supplement hair and nail supplement. This has shown a free T4 20 pmol/L (7-17) and TSH <0.05 (0.3-4.5). Further testing has shown negative TSH receptor antibodies and negative TPO antibodies. A nuclear medicine thyroid uptake scan shows normal uptake with no focal areas of uptake. On examination there is no goitre, no tremor, no ophthalmopathy with a resting heart rate of 74bpm. What is the most likely cause for her abnormal thyroid function tests?

A 55-year-old woman presents with self-neglect. She lives in squalor. Her neurological examination is unremarkable. Her Addenbrooke's Cognitive Examination score is 32/100. Her MRI brain is shown below. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is a heterogeneous disorder characterized by disturbances in behaviour, personality, and language accompanied by focal degeneration of the frontal and/or temporal lobes.
Rituximab has Phase 2B data on RR-MS but is not approved for MS therapy.
questions.stem: Approved therapies for Multiple Sclerosis in Australia do NOT include:

A 28-year-old woman presents with a twin pregnancy in her first trimester with intermittent palpitations. Blood tests show a TSH of 0.02 (ref 0.4-4), FT4 20 (ref 10-20) and FT3 5.8 (ref 3-6). The most likely diagnosis is:

options.text: Normal for first trimester of pregnancy

questions.explanation: S/c hyperthyroidism common in first trimester, especially with twin pregnancies.

options.text: Increased Na+ reabsorption in the Proximal convoluted tubule
Reduced vasopressin activity in the collecting duct

questions.explanation: The excessive sympathetic nervous system activation in congestive cardiac failure leads to increased Na+ reabsorption in the Proximal convoluted tubule directly as well as through the renin-angiotensin system. The Na+ retention contributes to extracellular fluid retention. There is reduced effective circulating blood volume, which stimulates Vasopressin secretion, which in turn, increases collecting duct reabsorption of more solute free water. There is no suggestion of increased fluid intake in this patient. Even though natriuretic peptide levels are increased in CCF, their effect is blunted by the factors favoring sodium retention ­ especially sympathetic nervous system activation and Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system activation. Vasopressin levels are increased in CCF due to reduced effective circulating volume. Ref ­ Hyponatremia in heart failure ­ UptoDate
questions.stem: A 72-year-old male is seen in the emergency dept with severe congestive cardiac failure for the first time. He is not on any medications. Physical examination shows pulse of 105 bpm, BP 104/72. JVP is elevated. Chest showed bi-basal crepitations. There was marked pedal oedema. Investigations showed Hb 108 gm/L, S. Na+ is 127 mmol/L, S.creatinine -160 micromol/L. The hypervolemic hyponatremia seen in this situation is due to:

questions.explanation: Rebound metabolic alkalosis may occur following remission of hypercapnia. During hypercapnia, the kidney generates bicarbonate to compensate for the respiratory acidosis with increase in NH4Cl excretion. When acidosis corrected, the HCO3- generated may not be excreted if low effective arterial blood volume, reduced glomerular filtration rate, and/or chloride depletion (diuretics, los NaCl diet). Post hypercapnic metabolic alkalosis ensues. In this scenario, the patient has correction of hypercapnia with treatment of NIV, on background of impaired renal function.

options.text: Worsening respiratory acidosis following cessation of bilevel ventilation
Metabolic alkalosis from concurrent use of frusemide as adjunct treatment for respiratory failure
Evidence for inadequate CPAP in setting of OSA ­ surrogate marker for nocturnal hypercapnia
Rebound metabolic alkalosis following correction of respiratory acidosis

questions.stem: A 72-year-old gentleman was admitted with acute type 2 respiratory failure with a pH of 7.31 and a PCO2 of 65 on arterial blood gas. This is on a background of untreated obstructive sleep apnoea, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and stage III chronic kidney disease. This patient was initiated on non-invasive ventilation with an inspiratory pressure of 16cmH20 and expiratory pressure of 8cm H2o. A subsequent blood gas demonstrate correction in the pCO2 and pH to normal levels. The patient was transitioned to continuous pulmonary airway pressure for sleep as treatment for OSA and bilevel ventilation ceased. On a serum blood test 2 days post cessation of bilevel ventilation, the serum HCO3 is noted to be elevated, having increased from 28 to 33. The most likely explanation for the elevation in serum HCO3 is?

options.text: The probability that the null hypothesis is true
The probability of the observed result if the null hypothesis is true
The overall probability of obtaining the observed sample
The significance level of the statistical test
The probability that the null hypothesis is false

questions.explanation: Formally the p value is the probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as the observed results of a statistical hypothesis test, assuming that the null hypothesis of no difference is correct. A smaller p-value means that there is stronger evidence in favour of the alternative hypothesis of a true difference.

Comparing the calibration curves of both rules
Calculating the integrated discrimination index (IDI) for rule A vs rule B
Calculating the net reclassification improvement (NRI) for rule A vs rule B
Obtaining the results of a clinical impact analysis for rule A vs rule B

questions.explanation: While all the stated measures are useful measures of performance, the one that is most precise and discriminatory is the NRI, calculated as [Pr (up/cases) - Pr (down/cases] + [Pr (down/non-cases) - Pr (up/non-cases)]
questions.stem: A new risk prediction rule (rule A) that combines genetic risk and traditional clinical risk factors is said to be superior to a more conventional risk prediction rule (rule B) based on only clinical risk factors. questions.explanation: A basic approach is to apply the SpPin acronym ­ highly specific test, if positive, rules disease in. But we have three tests where the specificity is quite high ­ C, D, E, and D in particular also has high sensitivity. The answer is to choose the test with the highest LR+, calculated as Sn/1-Sp, which for C comes out highest at 12.0 vs 6.0 and 7.0 respectively for D and E.

questions.explanation: There are multiple molecular targets for the treatment of IBD and this is a rapidly developing field. The earliest developed was Infliximab, which targets TNF- and is a chimeric antibody (designated by `xi' in the name). A similar molecule which is human derived (adalimumab ­ `u' for human) has the same target. Vedolizumab targets the 47integrin found on the gut wall, to prevent leukocyte chemotaxis. Ustekinumab is another human derived monocloanal antibody which targets IL-12 and IL-23. There is scant strong evidence for CD20 modulation (e.g. Rituximab) in the treatment of IBD and this is not a recommended treatment. In addition to monoclonal antibodies, small molecules have also shown promise in IBD, and Janus Kinase (JAK) inhibitors (tofacitinib) have recently been approved for use in Australia in patients with ulcerative colitis.

questions.explanation: Thiazide diuretics inhibit Na+ reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule by blocking the NCC channel in the renal cortex. This prevents generation of solute free tubular fluid (in fact leads to increased solute rich tubular fluid) which flows into the collecting duct. Thus, it impairs generation and excretion of solute free water. The thiazides act mainly in the cortical distal convoluted tubule. It does not act on the medulla. The intravascular volume depletion generated by Na+ wastage in the DCT by the diuretic will lead to secretion of vasopressin from the posterior pituitary. It can act on the medullary collecting duct and facilitate free water reabsorption. Increased free water consumption is possible, but it is not the optimal answer. Ref ­ Diuretic associated hyponatremia ­ UptoDate

options.text: Enhanced delivery of solute free water to the collecting duct
Reduced excretion of solute free water
Reduced effect of arginine vasopressin in medullary collecting duct

questions.explanation: Tofacitinib's main mechanism of action is more selective for JAK1 and JAK3. Its primary metabolism is via the liver (70%) by CYP3A4, with the remainder being renally cleared. Caution should therefore be exercised when co-prescribed with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors or inducers. Lymphocyte/neutrophil counts, creatinine and LFTs should be monitored and patients may require dose adjustment (or withholding tofacitinib) depending on severity of the abnormality. Side note: tofacitinib is currently approved in Australia for use in rheumatoid arthritis, psoriatic arthritis and ulcerative colitis. It is approved in New Zealand for use in rheumatoid arthritis. A 48-year-old male was admitted to hospital with poorly controlled BP and elevated S.Creatinine. He has a background history of poorly controlled T2DM and hypertension. Current medications include Insulin, Atorvastatin, amlodipine and aspirin. Physical examination showed BP – 150.92mmHg. There was bilateral pedal edema, non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy and impaired vibration sense in both feet.

Investigations: FBC – normal; S.Creatinine 170 micromol/L; S. Albumin – 31 gm/L; urine m/e – normal; Urine prot/creat ratio – 670; ANA negative; Hep B, Hep C, HIV screen negative. HbA1C – 9%
USS showed no urinary obstruction with left kidney 11 cm and right kidney 11.5 cm.

You initiate measures to optimize glycemic control. What would be your next step?

questions.explanation:
This patient has nephrotic range proteinuria associated with hypoalbuminemia. At this age, it can be caused by membranous nephropathy, followed by FSGS, Minimal change disease, amyloidosis, diabetic nephropathy and Mesangiocapillary Glomerulonephritis. This can be secondary to underlying malignancy (membranous nephropathy) or NSAID use (FSGS and minimal change disease).

At this age, empirical steroid use is not recommended as the cause is varied, unlike in childhood when minimal change disease is the commonest cause of nephrotic syndrome.

Prophylactic anticoagulation is recommended when there is severe hypoalbuminemia due to the risk of thrombotic events from loss of intrinsic anticoagulant factors such as Protein C, Protein S, antithrombin III etc – guidelines vary whether to start when s.Albumin is 20 or 25. In this patient, the first step is do a biopsy and have the diagnosis before considering anticoagulation.

Renal function is normal here. Dialysis would be considered in the setting of severe renal failure accompanied by severe hyperkalemia, intractable metabolic acidosis, refractory pulmonary edema, uremic pericarditis, uremic symptoms and drug intoxications.

questions.explanation: This patient likely has symptomatic anaemia, with his blood tests indicating that he has iron deficiency anaemia. In an 85-year-old man, a high level of suspicion needs to be maintained for gastrointestinal cancers. His ex-smoking history puts him at greater risk as well. As such, endoscopy and colonoscopy should be performed looking for blood loss from the gastrointestinal tract.

Always cover for gram +/- and anaerobes, treating with broad spectrum antibiotics such as tazosin or meropenem.

questions.explanation: CAR-T therapy is a new line of treatment for certain haematological malignancies currently. It is a form of immunotherapy whereby patients own T-cells are removed and engineered to attack malignant cells. The most life threatening risk of this treatment is cytokine release syndrome. This typically presents with fever, headache, low BP with progression to shock. The mainstay of treatment for this is Tociluzimab which is an IL-6 inhibitor. questions.stem: A 40 year old woman is booked for a resection of a neuroendocrine tumour. What class of medication should be commenced to avoid a carcinoid crisis?
questions.explanation: Patients with Carcinoid syndrome are at risk for developing a carcinoid crisis during either minor or major surgery. Patients with large tumour load, high chromograffin A levels and high urinary 5-HIAA are more likely to experience crisis during surgery. No clear guidelines however current consensus is to use octreotide prophylaxis to dimmish risk. IV octreotide can be give acutely if emerging crisis present. Often presents with ILD and there is a strong association with malignancy or the development of malignancy with onset of disease. A study was undertaken to assess the comparative efficacy of paracetamol compared to NSAIDs for the relief of postoperative pain following knee arthroscopy . Investigators recruited 5242 patients (response rate 96%) across five major metropolitan hospitals over 3 years, stratifying them into those who had been prescribed paracetamol (3508), NSAIDS (1389) or both (345). The primary outcome was pain assessed using a visual analogue scale at 24 hours post surgery. What was the main limitation of the study?
questions.explanation: There was likely to have been systematic differences in the groups being compared (such as terms of age and comorbidities). These differences would have distorted the perceived association between type of analgesia and pain.

Three distinct clinical syndromes are recognised within CAPS including: familial cold autoinflammatory syndrome (FCAS), Muckle-Wells syndrome (MWS), Neonatal-onset multisystem inflammatory disease/chronic infantile neurological, cutaneous and articular syndrome (NOMID/CINCA).
